External therapy & care: Progressive isometric rotator cuff training (avoid impact); moderate overhead motion and avoid loads that worsen the tear.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gan Di Huang 8 liang, Shan Yao 4 liang, Shan Zhu Yu 4 liang, Ze Xie 3 liang, Fu Ling 3 liang, Mu Dan Pi 3 liang, Gui Zhi 1 liang, Fu Zi 1 piece (blast-fried); combine Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Jiang Huang

Shen Qi Wan supplements the Kidney root, seeking Yang within Yin to restore the tendon's generative source, combined with Tao-Hong-Jiang Huang to transform the tear's stasis and guide to the shoulder, matching Liver-Kidney deficiency with stasis. For Yin-deficient heat, remove Gui and Fu for the Liu Wei method.

Textbook reference: Duhuo Jisheng Tang (shoulder modification)

Du Huo, Sang Ji Sheng, Du Zhong, Niu Xi, Qin Jiao, Fang Feng, Xi Xin, Rou Gui, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Shao Yao, Gan Di Huang, Ren Shen, Fu Ling, Gan Cao; add Jiang Huang to guide to the shoulder

The gold standard for chronic Bi pain with Liver-Kidney deficiency: supporting the upright and expelling the pathogen together, with Du Zhong, Sang Ji Sheng, Niu Xi nourishing Liver-Kidney to restore sinew and bone; add Jiang Huang to guide to the shoulder.

Formula analysis: Shen Qi Wan purely supplements the Kidney root with stasis-transformation, suiting deficiency-dominant with stasis; Duhuo Jisheng Tang supports upright and expels pathogen together, suiting deficiency with Bi. Pure deficiency with stasis takes the former; deficiency with wind-damp Bi takes the latter.

Modifications:

  • Marked tear stasis, night pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Ji Xue Teng
  • Yin-deficient heat — remove Gui and Fu, add Zhi Mu, Huang Bai
  • Qi deficiency, weak elevation — add Huang Qi, Dang Shen

Key point: Empty-can weak = true tear; a partial tear can be treated conservatively with nourishment plus needling, but a full-thickness tear with marked weakness requires surgical referral — do not delay the window.

Acromioclavicular Joint Origin Shoulder Pain

Principle: Release the peri-AC soft tissue and free the local channel Qi

Target muscle / point: Peri-AC soft tissue, upper trapezius insertion, anterior deltoid tension points

Technique: Precisely localize the pain point at the shoulder top, encircle-needle the Ashi points and release the surrounding taut bands; horizontal-adduction-provoked pain is the localizing basis.

Safety: The AC joint is superficial, shallow needling suffices; acute red-hot swelling must exclude infection before treating.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously below and lateral to the shoulder top, needle tip toward the AC joint

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: the patient slowly horizontally adducts back and forth, sweeping to release the surrounding fascia.

Primary points: LI15 (Jianyu), LI16 (Jugu, adjacent to the AC joint), GB21 (Jianjing), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: TE14 (Jianliao), SI12 (Bingfeng), GB34 (Yanglingquan)

Point rationale: LI16 (Jugu) sits right beside the AC joint, the local key point; GB21 (Jianjing) releases upper-trapezius traction; together they free the shoulder-top channel Qi and release the surrounding soft tissue.

External therapy & care: Avoid carrying loads and cross-body motions on the affected side; relative rest in the acute phase.

Classical formula: Choose by accompanying pattern

With stasis: Guizhi Fuling Wan; with wind-damp: Guizhi-plus-Gegen modification

The AC joint is mainly treated by local release; herbs follow the accompanying pattern to mind the constitution.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Jing Jie, Du Huo, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Qing Pi, Niu Xi, Wu Jia Pi, Du Zhong, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke

Relaxes sinew, quickens Blood, frees the collaterals, consolidating the local treatment, not the main weapon.

Formula analysis: This pattern is led by local release and Ashi points, with herbs as adjunct; when acute inflammation is marked, immobilization and local care come first.

Modifications:

  • Acute swelling and pain — immobilize and treat locally first, cautious with strong stimulation
  • Chronic strain — strengthen scapular stability training

Key point: Pain at the shoulder top rather than the lateral shoulder, plus positive horizontal adduction, is the localizing key; exclude infection first with acute red-hot swelling.

⚠ Warning Signs — Surgical Referral / Exclude Referral / Emergency Guide

Principle: Mostly no pattern identification, no needling; recognize — exclude/refer — follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling-related handling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Left shoulder pain with chest tightness and cold sweat = suspect cardiac, screen the heart at once, do not needle; full-thickness tear with marked weakness = seize the surgical window.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of the critical condition.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: suspect cardiac — immediate emergency screening; full-thickness tear with marked weakness — orthopedic surgical assessment; progressive rest/night pain with weight loss — tumor-direction screening; red-hot-swollen with fever — infection, blood count and inflammatory markers; provoked by neck movement — check the cervical spine (see the neck-pain nerve-root branch).

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes these situations outside first-line acupuncture before writing any shoulder treatment plan.

Key point: Left shoulder pain with chest tightness and cold sweat — think heart first; full-thickness tear with marked weakness — do not delay; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Knee Pain

The knee is the mansion of the sinews and the pivot of weight-bearing. The three foot-Yin channels ascend through the medial knee (Spleen, Liver, Kidney); the three foot-Yang channels descend past the lateral knee (Stomach, Gallbladder, Bladder): medial knee pain is mostly Foot-Taiyin and Jueyin (SP9 Yinlingquan the key point), lateral knee pain mostly Foot-Shaoyang (GB34 Yanglingquan, sinew meeting and Gallbladder He-sea), anterior infrapatellar pain belongs to Foot-Yangming (ST35 Dubi and EX-LE5 Neixiyan, the two ｢knee eyes｣). Fixing the channel by pain location is the first rod of knee-pain localization.

Structurally, knee stability relies on static and dynamic systems: the static ones are the collateral and cruciate ligaments and the menisci; the dynamic ones are the quadriceps (especially the VMO stabilizing patellar tracking), the hamstrings, and the pes anserinus three. The meniscus buffers and locks; when torn it causes locking. A weak VMO lets the patella track laterally, raising patellofemoral pressure and anterior knee pain. The most tender point on the joint line is both an Ashi point and a trigger point of the peri-articular soft tissue.

The pattern of pain identifies the tissue: medial joint-line pain with worse descent of stairs and a positive McMurray is mostly medial meniscus; anterior pain worse ascending stairs with abnormal patellar tracking is mostly patellofemoral syndrome; diffuse pain with crepitus in the elderly is mostly osteoarthritis. The functional pattern (up/down stairs, locking/giving way, morning stiffness) distinguishes cartilage, meniscus, and ligament better than static localization.

In its ordinary course, excess knee pain is mostly Wind-Cold-Damp obstructing the joint, or Blood-stasis-phlegm in the meniscus or ligaments (trauma or strain); deficient knee pain is mostly Liver-Kidney depletion in the elderly, bone withering and cartilage degeneration causing osteoarthritis, with the root in the Kidney and the branch in phlegm-stasis (osteophytes). So knee osteoarthritis is mostly root-deficiency with branch-excess — supplement Liver-Kidney to secure the root, transform phlegm-stasis and free the joint to treat the branch.

Clinical pursuit of knee pain locates first, characterizes second, and must screen warning signs before all: step one asks the pain location and functional pattern (stairs, locking, giving way); step two uses special tests (McMurray, Lachman, joint-line palpation) to fix meniscus / ligament / patellofemoral; step three palpates to lock the responsible muscle (especially the VMO); step four settles the Qi disturbance by cold-heat and deficiency-excess. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

The pain is in the knee — the source may not be inside the joint. Trace it in order of probability:

VMO Referred Knee Pain (Patellofemoral Syndrome)

Principle: Release the VMO trigger point and restore patellar tracking — treat the VMO to cure the anterior knee

Target muscle / point: VMO trigger point (teardrop belly above and medial to the patella), vastus lateralis and iliotibial band (the lateral traction source); this layer is the mainstay

Technique: Palpate the VMO taut band above and medial to the patella, needle to elicit the twitch and reproduce the anteromedial knee pain, then test squatting and stair-climbing pain — often immediately reduced without touching the joint; release the over-tight vastus lateralis as well to balance patellar tracking.

Safety: Peri-patellar needling must not enter the joint cavity; work in the superficial layer of the anterior knee with clear muscular contact.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously at the mid-to-lower thigh, needle tip toward the knee (VMO direction)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: the patient slowly performs straight-leg raises and short-arc extension (activating the VMO), contracting the quadriceps while sweeping, re-testing patellofemoral pain in real time.

Primary points: ST35 (Dubi) + EX-LE5 (Neixiyan) paired opposing needling, SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP10 (Xuehai)

Adjunct points: ST34 (Liangqiu), GB34 (Yanglingquan), local Ashi points (at the VMO)

Point rationale: ST35 (Dubi) and EX-LE5 (Neixiyan) are needled toward each other, the ｢open the knee gate｣ technique opening the patellofemoral passage directly; SP10 (Xuehai) and SP9 (Yinlingquan) sit at the VMO and medial knee, quickening Blood and draining damp to open the medial channel; ST34 (Liangqiu), the Stomach-channel cleft point, treats acute knee pain.

External therapy & care: Progressive quadriceps (especially VMO) and hip abductor strengthening; correct foot overpronation, avoid prolonged knee flexion in sitting and excessive downhill loading.

Classical formula: Shaoyao Gancao Tang (★Classical Original★) with Niu Xi and Mu Gua

Bai Shao, Zhi Gan Cao (original 1:1, from 30 g each today), add Niu Xi to guide to the knee, Mu Gua to relax sinew

The Shang Han Lun Shaoyao Gancao Tang governs cramping spasm of the legs; sour and sweet transform Yin and relax sinew urgency, matching the peri-patellar sinew tension; Niu Xi guides the formula specifically to the knee, Mu Gua relaxes sinew and frees the joint.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (knee modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Jing Jie, Du Huo, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Qing Pi, Niu Xi, Wu Jia Pi, Du Zhong, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke; add Mu Gua

Relaxes sinew, quickens Blood, frees the collaterals, consolidating VMO release and patellofemoral adjustment.

Formula analysis: The essence of this pattern is in needle and training (release the VMO, strengthen the VMO, restore tracking), with herbs as adjunct: sinew spasm dominant takes Shaoyao Gancao Tang to relax sinew, strain with stasis takes Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang.

Modifications:

  • Marked lateral patellar tracking — weight VMO training, herbs as adjunct
  • Sedentary occupation — prescribe hip-knee strength-balance training
  • With damp — add Yi Yi Ren, Cang Zhu

Key point: Releasing the VMO often immediately eases anterior knee pain without touching the joint; without restoring patellar tracking (strengthen VMO, release vastus lateralis), release is only temporary.

Blood-Stasis-Phlegm Knee Pain (Meniscus · Ligament Injury)

Principle: Quicken Blood and transform stasis, transform phlegm and free the collaterals, release the peri-articular soft tissue, and assess stability

Target muscle / point: The most tender joint-line point (Ashi), peri-meniscal soft tissue, collateral ligament insertions, protective taut bands of the hamstrings and quadriceps

Technique: Encircle-needle the Ashi points at the joint-line point pain and release the surrounding protective taut bands; keep it light in the acute swollen phase, needle through old nodules in the chronic phase. Pair with gentle joint mobilization.

Safety: Do not needle into the joint cavity; acute marked swelling must exclude hemarthrosis and instability, refer for assessment first if needed.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously at the distal thigh above the joint line, needle tip toward the affected joint line

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: the patient slowly flexes and extends the knee within the painless range, sweeping to restore fascial circulation and release protective spasm.

Primary points: ST35 (Dubi) + EX-LE5 (Neixiyan), SP9 (Yinlingquan), GB34 (Yanglingquan), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: BL17 (Geshu), SP10 (Xuehai) to quicken Blood; medial SP10-LV8 (Ququan); lateral GB34

Point rationale: The two knee eyes open the knee gate and free the infrapatellar passage; medial meniscus takes SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP10 (Xuehai), LV8 (Ququan) to move Spleen-Liver channel stasis, lateral takes GB34 (Yanglingquan, sinew meeting); BL17 (Geshu, Blood meeting) aids stasis transformation.

External therapy & care: Relative rest and icing in the acute phase; progressive quadriceps and hamstring strengthening to stabilize the joint in recovery.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with Niu Xi

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, add Niu Xi to guide to the knee, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals

Slowly dissolves the formed stasis at the knee joint line, warming and dissolving together, matching old stasis knots of meniscus and ligament; Niu Xi guides the formula specifically to the knee, quickening Blood and strengthening sinew.

Textbook reference: Shen Tong Zhu Yu Tang (knee modification)

Qin Jiao, Chuan Xiong, Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Gan Cao, Qiang Huo, Mo Yao, Dang Gui, Wu Ling Zhi, Xiang Fu, Niu Xi, Di Long; add Mu Gua

For acute traumatic stasis-dominant cases, quickening Blood, moving Qi, and freeing collaterals; Niu Xi and Mu Gua guide to the knee and relax sinew.

Formula analysis: Shen Tong Zhu Yu Tang moves and scatters, suiting acute heavy stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves formed knots, suiting chronic old cases. Acute trauma takes the former; chronic recurrence takes the latter in slow persistence.

Modifications:

  • Acute swelling with effusion — add Yi Yi Ren, Ze Xie, Fang Ji to drain water
  • Marked locking — add Mu Gua, Shen Jin Cao to relax sinew and free the joint
  • Old stasis unresolved — add a small dose of Tu Bie Chong

Key point: Joint-line point tenderness plus positive McMurray fixes the meniscus; locking unable to extend or marked instability first excludes bucket-handle tear and ACL rupture.

Liver-Kidney Deficiency · Wind-Cold-Damp Bi Knee Pain (Knee Osteoarthritis)

Principle: Supplement Liver and Kidney and strengthen sinew and bone to secure the root; dispel Wind, scatter Cold, remove Damp, transform phlegm-stasis and free the joint to treat the branch

Target muscle / point: Peri-knee diffuse taut bands, VMO and hamstring trigger points; gentle technique, precise point selection

Technique: Release the peri-knee trigger points, prefer warm needling; a moxa box over the knee-eye region may be added to warm and scatter cold-damp. Deficient bodies take light stimulation and few needles.

Safety: Avoid strong local stimulation of the degenerated joint; exclude septic or crystal arthritis before treating acute red-hot swelling.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously at the mid-to-lower thigh, needle tip toward the knee

Sweep & reperfusion: Gentle slow sweeping with reperfusion (slow knee flexion-extension); FSN is adjunct, supplementing Liver-Kidney and freeing the joint is principal.

Primary points: ST35 (Dubi) + EX-LE5 (Neixiyan), SP9 (Yinlingquan), GB34 (Yanglingquan), KI3 (Taixi)

Adjunct points: BL23 (Shenshu), GV4 (Mingmen, moxa), GB39 (Xuanzhong, marrow meeting), knee-eye moxa

Point rationale: The two knee eyes open the knee gate, moxaed to warm and scatter cold-damp; SP9 (Yinlingquan) drains medial damp, GB34 (Yanglingquan, sinew meeting) frees the joint; KI3 (Taixi), BL23 (Shenshu), GV4 (Mingmen) supplement the Kidney root, GB39 (Xuanzhong) boosts marrow and strengthens bone.

External therapy & care: Moxa box over the knee eyes to warm and free; isometric quadriceps strengthening, avoid deep squats and stair impact, control body weight to reduce load.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) with the Duhuo Jisheng intent

Gan Di Huang 8 liang, Shan Yao 4 liang, Shan Zhu Yu 4 liang, Ze Xie 3 liang, Fu Ling 3 liang, Mu Dan Pi 3 liang, Gui Zhi 1 liang, Fu Zi 1 piece (blast-fried); combine Niu Xi, Mu Gua, Yi Yi Ren

Shen Qi Wan supplements the Kidney root, seeking Yang within Yin to secure the source of bone withering; Niu Xi guides to the knee, Mu Gua relaxes sinew and frees the joint, Yi Yi Ren transforms damp and reduces inflammation — matching the Kidney-deficiency bone-withering with damp-phlegm mechanism of knee osteoarthritis.

Textbook reference: Duhuo Jisheng Tang (knee-specific modification)

Du Huo, Sang Ji Sheng, Du Zhong, Niu Xi, Qin Jiao, Fang Feng, Xi Xin, Rou Gui, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Shao Yao, Gan Di Huang, Ren Shen, Fu Ling, Gan Cao; add Mu Gua, Yi Yi Ren

The gold standard for chronic Bi pain with Liver-Kidney deficiency, with Niu Xi as the knee-guiding herb (as Ge Gen guides the neck, Jiang Huang the shoulder); add Mu Gua to relax sinew and free the joint, Yi Yi Ren to transform damp and reduce inflammation, forming the ｢knee-specific modification｣ addressing both the Liver-Kidney root and local knee damp-phlegm.

Formula analysis: Shen Qi Wan purely supplements the Kidney root, suiting deficiency-dominant; Duhuo Jisheng Tang supports upright and expels pathogen together, suiting deficiency with wind-damp Bi. Pure deficiency with little pathogen takes the former; deficiency with Bi (most osteoarthritis) takes the latter.

Modifications:

  • Severe cold with intense pain — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Gan Jiang to warm the channel and scatter cold
  • Damp-heat swelling (hot painful joint) — remove Gui and Fu, add Cang Zhu, Huang Bai (Si Miao intent), Yi Yi Ren
  • Marked stasis with osteophyte pain — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Tu Bie Chong

Key point: Knee osteoarthritis is root-deficiency with branch-excess: supplement Liver-Kidney to secure the root, transform phlegm-stasis and free the joint to treat the branch; Niu Xi is the knee-guiding herb, the course runs in months, and weight control plus quadriceps strength is the root of maintenance.

Wind-Cold-Damp Bi Knee Pain (Sinew-Channel · Iliotibial Band · Pes Anserinus)

Principle: Dispel Wind, scatter Cold, remove Damp, release the sinew-channel knots and free the local channel Qi

Target muscle / point: The iliotibial band and its lateral knee insertion, pes anserinus three-muscle (sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus) insertion taut bands and trigger points

Technique: Palpate the nodules along the taut sinew band, needle obliquely to release with a local response; ITB laterally, pes anserinus inferomedially, paired with gentle movement. Warm needling for cold-damp.

Safety: Lateral knee needling avoids the common peroneal nerve course (posteroinferior to the fibular head); an electric shooting sensation to the dorsum of the foot means withdraw and redirect.

Entry: For ITB, insert subcutaneously at the distal lateral thigh toward the lateral knee; for pes anserinus, insert subcutaneously inferomedial to the knee toward the insertion

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: for ITB the patient slowly flexes-extends the knee or side-lying abducts, for pes anserinus slowly flexes-extends the knee, sweeping to release the taut sinew band.

Primary points: Lateral: GB34 (Yanglingquan), GB33 (Xiyangguan), local Ashi points; inferomedial: SP9 (Yinlingquan), pes anserinus Ashi points

Adjunct points: Lateral add GB31 (Fengshi); medial add SP10 (Xuehai), LV8 (Ququan); moxa for cold-damp

Point rationale: GB34 (Yanglingquan, sinew meeting and Gallbladder He) governs lateral tendon disorders, GB33 (Xiyangguan) at the lateral knee; pes anserinus pain takes SP9 (Yinlingquan) and local Ashi; moxa to warm and scatter for cold-damp.

External therapy & care: For ITB, release the tensor fasciae latae and gluteus medius, correct running gait and foot alignment; for pes anserinus, check flatfoot and knee valgus.

Classical formula: Shaoyao Gancao Tang (★Classical Original★) modified

Bai Shao, Zhi Gan Cao from 30 g each, add Niu Xi, Mu Gua, Wei Ling Xian; add Gui Zhi for heavy cold

Sour and sweet transform Yin and relax sinew urgency, matching sinew-channel spasm; Niu Xi guides to the knee, Mu Gua relaxes sinew, Wei Ling Xian dispels wind-damp and frees the collaterals.

Textbook reference: Juanbi Tang (knee modification)

Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Gui Zhi, Qin Jiao, Hai Feng Teng, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao; add Niu Xi, Mu Gua, Yi Yi Ren

Dispels wind-damp, frees the collaterals, relaxes sinew and frees the joint, the textbook general formula for lower-limb Bi pain; Niu Xi and Mu Gua guide to the knee and relax sinew.

Formula analysis: The Shaoyao Gancao modification leans toward relaxing sinew urgency, suiting spasm-dominant; Juanbi Tang leans toward dispelling wind-damp and freeing collaterals, suiting diffuse Bi pain. Localized spasm takes the former; diffuse wind-damp takes the latter.

Modifications:

  • Severe cold — add Gui Zhi, blast-fried Fu Zi
  • Heavy damp with swelling — add Yi Yi Ren, Fang Ji, Cang Zhu
  • Recurrent strain — check lower-limb alignment, prescribe hip-foot training

Key point: ITB and pes anserinus pain often source at the hip and foot alignment; in recurrent cases check running gait and foot arch — treating the knee must also treat its upstream and downstream.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: Mostly no pattern identification, no needling; recognize — exclude/refer — follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling-related handling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Joint locked unable to fully extend = bucket-handle tear, orthopedic assessment; red-hot-swollen with fever = suspect septic arthritis, joint fluid analysis, do not needle.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of an intra-articular emergency.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: locked unable to extend — orthopedics (bucket-handle tear); marked instability giving way + soft Lachman end-feel — ACL tear referral; red-hot-swollen with fever — joint fluid to exclude infection; acute monoarticular red-hot severe pain — exclude crystal arthritis such as gout (see the Gout chapter); progressive rest/night pain with weight loss — tumor screening.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes these intra-articular emergencies and situations outside first-line acupuncture before writing any knee treatment plan.

Key point: Locked unable to extend, marked instability, red-hot-swollen with fever — these three must be referred and excluded first; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Sciatica

Pain radiating from the buttock down the leg is the disease domain where this module's ｢source tracing｣ idea shows most clearly. What patients call ｢sciatica｣ has at least three sources, only one of which is true sciatica: nerve-root compression from a disc, piriformis entrapment, and sacroiliac referral — three different mechanisms, channels, and treatments; misidentify them and the treatment misses.

By channel, radiating leg pain mostly follows two lines: pain down the posterior midline of the leg to the heel and lateral foot (little toe) is Foot-Taiyang; pain down the lateral leg to the dorsum and great toe is Foot-Shaoyang. Below-knee dermatomal precision is the confirming sign of true radicular pain — below-knee electric pain along a specific dermatome with a positive straight-leg raise means root compression at the lumbar level; above-knee or diffuse pain without dermatomal specificity is mostly piriformis or sacroiliac referral.

Structurally, the sciatic nerve arises from the lumbosacral roots and descends through the greater sciatic foramen, passing beneath (or through) the piriformis. So one nerve can be involved at three sites: the foramen (radicular), the piriformis (GB30 Huantiao, entrapment), and near the sacroiliac joint (referral). GB30 (Huantiao) sits right at the sciatic notch where the piriformis passes — the target for pseudo-sciatica; BL23 (Shenshu) is near the lumbar root — the seat of true radicular pain.

In its ordinary course, excess sciatica is mostly cold-damp obstructing the lumbar Taiyang root or piriformis obstruction of the Gallbladder channel; deficiency is mostly Kidney deficiency of the elderly with disc and ligamentum flavum degeneration narrowing the canal (Phlegm-stasis), presenting as neurogenic intermittent claudication. So acute is mostly blockage, chronic mostly root deficiency with phlegm-stasis.

Clinical pursuit of sciatica screens warning signs first, then sorts the source with three tests: step one asks whether radiation passes the knee and follows a dermatome; step two uses the straight-leg raise (SLR) / FAIR / FABER trio to separate radicular, piriformis, and sacroiliac; step three checks dermatomal sensation and reflexes to fix the root level; and finally cold–heat and deficiency-excess settle the nature. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain radiating from buttock to leg — three sources, only one is true sciatica; trace by probability:

Cold-Damp Obstruction Sciatica (Radicular Type)

Principle: Scatter cold and dispel damp, warm and free the channel, restore descending Qi; treat root and branch together

Target muscle / point: Deep erector spinae and multifidus at the involved lumbar segment, plus gluteus medius/minimus and piriformis (their referral routinely overlays the radicular radiation; releasing it markedly shrinks the radiation)

Technique: Release the overlaid gluteal-and-piriformis component first, then the deep involved segment; each session assess whether the radiation centralizes (centralization is favorable). Cold cases take warm needling or channel moxibustion.

Safety: Deep piriformis needling must respect the sciatic nerve — electric shooting to the leg means withdraw and redirect; lumbar needling toward the lamina is safe.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal on the radiation line (buttock or posterior thigh), tip toward the lumbosacral region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly raises the straight leg and dorsiflexes the ankle (Bladder-channel tension activity), comparing SLR angle and radiation extent in real time.

Primary points: GB30 (Huantiao), BL40 (Weizhong), EX-B2 (Jiaji, involved segment), BL23 (Shenshu)

Adjunct points: Foot-Taiyang line: add BL57 (Chengshan), BL60 (Kunlun); Foot-Shaoyang line: add GB34 (Yanglingquan), GB39 (Xuanzhong); cold cases moxa GV4 (Mingmen)

Point rationale: GB30 (Huantiao) is the master point for hip and leg pain, strongly stimulating the Gallbladder channel; BL40 (Weizhong) drains Bladder-channel congestion; EX-B2 (Jiaji) takes the involved segment directly; distal points deploy along the involved channel line, with channel moxibustion for cold.

External therapy & care: Keep the low back and leg warm, avoid cold-damp; Bladder-channel cupping on the upper back helps expel wind-cold; avoid prolonged sitting and loading in the acute phase.

Classical formula: Ganjiang Ling Zhu Tang (Shenzhuo Decoction) (★Classical Original★) with additions

Gan Cao 2 liang, Bai Zhu 2 liang, Gan Jiang 4 liang, Fu Ling 4 liang; add Niu Xi to guide downward, Du Huo for lower-burner wind-damp, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals

The Jin Gui Shenzhuo Decoction treats cold heaviness below the waist as if sitting in water — warming the center and dispelling damp without treating the Kidney itself, exactly matching cold-damp lodged in the low back and leg; Niu Xi and Du Huo lead downward and free the collaterals.

Textbook reference: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang with Fuzi

Du Huo, Sang Ji Sheng, Du Zhong, Niu Xi, Xi Xin, Qin Jiao, Fu Ling, Rou Gui, Fang Feng, Chuan Xiong, Ren Shen, Gan Cao, Dang Gui, Shao Yao, Gan Di Huang; add blast-fried Fu Zi for marked cold

The gold standard for chronic painful obstruction with Liver-Kidney deficiency: Du Huo and Fang Feng dispel lower-burner wind-cold-damp, Du Zhong-Niu Xi-Sang Ji Sheng nourish Liver-Kidney and restore sinew and bone, Fu Zi warms and scatters cold when it dominates.

Formula analysis: Shenzhuo Decoction purely scatters low-back-and-leg cold-damp, simple and focused, for fresh cold-damp; Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang supports the upright while expelling the pathogen, for long illness with Liver-Kidney deficiency. New excess illness — the former; long deficiency-excess illness — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Marked numbness — add Huang Qi, Ji Xue Teng (weak Qi failing to move brings numbness)
  • Marked stasis with night pain — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Di Long
  • Severe cold with cold leg — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Gui Zhi

Key point: Releasing the overlaid gluteal-and-piriformis component often markedly shrinks the radiation; record SLR angle and radiation extent each visit — centralization is the iron proof of recovery.

Piriformis Entrapment Sciatica (Pseudo-sciatica · GB30)

Principle: Deep-release the piriformis MTrP, warm the channel and transform stasis — treat the piriformis to cure the pseudo-sciatica

Target muscle / point: Piriformis belly (at GB30 Huantiao, the sciatic notch) plus gluteus medius and minimus trigger points; this layer is the absolute mainstay, all three muscles almost always involved

Technique: Prone or side-lying with hip flexed, locate the piriformis obliquely from PSIS to greater trochanter, palpate the taut nodule 4–6 cm medial to the trochanter, needle deep 4–6 cm (piriformis lies deep) to elicit a whole-muscle twitch; FAIR improves immediately after release. Also needle gluteus medius (PSIS-trochanter line, fanning laterally) and minimus (slightly deeper and more lateral).

Safety: The deep piriformis lies against the sciatic nerve: needle on clear muscular contact, stop at the twitch, and on any sustained electric sensation withdraw and redirect — never needle directly into the nerve.

Entry: After MTrP release, insert subcutaneously in the buttock, tip toward the piriformis lesion

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes-adducts-internally rotates the hip (FAIR direction) or walks slowly, sweeping to release the gluteal fascia.

Primary points: GB30 (Huantiao), BL54 (Zhibian), BL40 (Weizhong), GB34 (Yanglingquan)

Adjunct points: GB39 (Xuanzhong), local Ashi points, posterior-leg component add BL57 (Chengshan)

Point rationale: GB30 (Huantiao), on the outer third of the trochanter-to-sacral-hiatus line, reaches all three gluteal muscles and the piriformis at once — the most powerful single local point for hip and sciatic conditions; BL54 (Zhibian), at the fourth posterior sacral foramen, faces the piriformis origin; BL40 (Weizhong) releases posterior-leg Bladder-channel tension.

External therapy & care: Piriformis stretch (supine flexion-adduction-internal rotation) and gluteal release; correct prolonged sitting, avoid hard cold surfaces, chronic overuse and cold exposure are the seeds of relapse.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with the Danggui Sini intent

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, combined with Dang Gui, Xi Xin, Tong Cao to warm the channel; add Niu Xi and Mu Gua to guide downward and relax the sinew

Piriformis syndrome mostly carries cold with stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan warms, unblocks, and dissolves stasis while slowly dissolving the contracted knot, and the Danggui Sini intent warms the channel against the cold leg — matching cold-stasis obstruction of the hip sinew.

Textbook reference: Juan Bi Tang (leg modification)

Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Gui Zhi, Qin Jiao, Hai Feng Teng, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao; add Niu Xi, Mu Gua to guide down and relax sinew

Dispels wind-damp, warms and scatters cold, frees the collaterals; Niu Xi and Mu Gua lead the formula downward — a general formula for leg painful obstruction.

Formula analysis: The key here is in the needle, not the herb: deep GB30 release of the piriformis MTrP is sovereign, gluteus medius/minimus release is minister, herbs warming and transforming stasis are assistant. Guizhi Fuling Wan combination favors cold-stasis; Juan Bi Tang favors diffuse wind-damp painful obstruction.

Modifications:

  • Marked cold with cold leg — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Gui Zhi
  • Long stasis with night pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Di Long
  • Recurrent post-surgical residual sciatic pain — see the post-surgical residual pattern in the Piriformis Syndrome chapter

Key point: Pseudo-sciatica is treated at the GB30 piriformis MTrP, not the lumbar root; worse sitting + FAIR positive + SLR negative is the divide from true radicular pain. See the dedicated Piriformis Syndrome chapter.

Sacroiliac Referred Sciatic-like Pain

Principle: Reset the sacroiliac pivot and release its surrounding knots — full treatment in the low back sacroiliac pattern

Target muscle / point: Gluteus maximus origin, sacral multifidus, and sacrotuberous ligament tension points around the sacroiliac joint

Technique: Prone, palpate the inner and outer margins of the sacroiliac groove for tenderness and tension points and release one by one; pairing with pelvic reduction (flexed hip-knee technique) doubles the effect.

Safety: Needle only shallowly over the posterior sacral foramina — deep lies the sacral canal outlet.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously inferolateral to the sacroiliac groove, tip toward the joint

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: alternating single-leg stance, pelvic clock.

Primary points: BL32 (Ciliao), BL27 (Xiaochangshu), BL28 (Pangguangshu), GB30 (Huantiao)

Adjunct points: BL40 (Weizhong), local Ashi points, BL62 (Shenmai)

Point rationale: BL32 (Ciliao) sits on the posterior sacral foramen — the anchor for sacroiliac pain; BL27 (Xiaochangshu) and BL28 (Pangguangshu) flank the upper joint margin; BL62 (Shenmai) opens the Yang Qiao to tune the leg's force line.

External therapy & care: A sacroiliac belt assists fixation (especially postpartum); avoid leg-crossing and long sitting on soft sofas. Full external care and differentiation in the low back sacroiliac pattern.

Classical formula: Choose by accompanying pattern (see the low back sacroiliac pattern)

Postpartum Blood deficiency: Danggui Shaoyao San (★Classical Original★); with stasis: Guizhi Fuling Wan

Structural pain is led by reduction and needling, herbs tune the inner terrain; full herbal treatment is in the low back sacroiliac pattern.

Textbook reference: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang

See the cold-damp pattern

The general base formula for chronic joint painful obstruction with Liver-Kidney deficiency.

Formula analysis: This pattern shares mechanism and treatment with the low back sacroiliac pattern; here it only catches the sciatic-like presentation, with full treatment navigated to the low back sacroiliac pattern rather than repeated.

Modifications:

  • Postpartum — add Du Zhong, Xu Duan, Sang Ji Sheng to secure Kidney and strengthen the waist
  • Young man with morning stiffness over an hour — check HLA-B27 and sacroiliac MRI

Key point: Worse rolling over and rising, FABER positive, no neuro signs fixes the localization; full treatment is in the low back sacroiliac pattern, with the inflammatory-spondyloarthropathy red line kept taut.

Kidney-Deficiency Blood-Stasis Sciatica (Spinal Stenosis · Neurogenic Claudication)

Principle: Supplement Kidney to secure the root, transform phlegm-stasis to open the passage, free the Taiyang Qi

Target muscle / point: Lumbar erector spinae, multifidus, and gluteals; gentle technique, elderly deficient bodies cannot afford wide expenditure

Technique: Light-to-moderate release of lumbar and gluteal taut bands, prefer warm needling; the emphasis is Kidney supplementation and opening rather than forceful breaking.

Safety: Elderly deficient bodies — treat lying down with few needles to prevent fainting; marked motor deficit means avoid strong local stimulation and refer for assessment first.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the lumbosacral or gluteal region, tip toward the symptomatic area

Sweep & reperfusion: Gentle slow sweeping with reperfusion (slow flexion-extension, canal-opening flexed posture); FSN is adjunct, Kidney supplementation and opening the passage lead.

Primary points: BL23 (Shenshu), KI3 (Taixi), GV4 (Mingmen, moxa), GB30 (Huantiao)

Adjunct points: EX-B2 (Jiaji), BL40 (Weizhong), GB39 (Xuanzhong, marrow meeting), BL25 (Dachangshu)

Point rationale: BL23 (Shenshu) with KI3 (Taixi) and GV4 (Mingmen) supplement the Kidney root to secure the degenerating spine; GB30 (Huantiao) and EX-B2 (Jiaji) open the descending channel Qi; GB39 (Xuanzhong) boosts marrow and strengthens bone.

External therapy & care: Flexion-based core stabilization (canal-opening direction); weight control, avoid extension and prolonged standing.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gan Di Huang 8 liang, Shan Yao 4 liang, Shan Zhu Yu 4 liang, Ze Xie 3 liang, Fu Ling 3 liang, Mu Dan Pi 3 liang, Gui Zhi 1 liang, Fu Zi 1 piece (blast-fried); combined with Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Niu Xi, Di Long

Shen Qi Wan supplements the Kidney root and seeks Yang within Yin to secure the degenerating source, combined with Tao-Hong-Niu Xi-Di Long to transform canal phlegm-stasis and free the descending Qi — matching Kidney-deficiency Blood-stasis stenosis.

Textbook reference: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang with the Buyang Huanwu intent

Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang as base; with Qi-deficiency numbness add Huang Qi, Dang Gui Wei, Di Long (Buyang Huanwu intent)

Supports the upright while expelling the pathogen; for Qi-deficiency Blood-stasis leg heaviness and numbness, heavy Huang Qi supplements Qi to move Blood and free the collaterals.

Formula analysis: Shen Qi Wan favors Kidney-root supplementation with stasis transformation; Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang favors supporting the upright while expelling the pathogen. Pure deficiency with stasis — the former; deficiency-excess with Qi deficiency — the latter with Buyang Huanwu intent.

Modifications:

  • Marked claudication with leg heaviness — heavy Huang Qi, add Di Long and a small dose of Shui Zhi to free the collaterals
  • Severe cold — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Gui Zhi
  • With damp-heat (stenosis with inflammation) — remove Gui-Fu, add Cang Zhu, Huang Bai

Key point: Relief on flexion and sitting with intermittent claudication is the stenosis signature; marked motor deficit or cauda equina signs mean surgical referral — do not delay and miss the window.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Imaging / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, no needling; recognize, refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling, complete referral documentation, and grade the urgency.

Safety: Saddle numbness + bowel/bladder dysfunction + bilateral leg weakness = cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency, delay measured in hours.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may blur the window for observing disease progression.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: cauda equina signs — emergency surgical assessment; progressive weakness/foot drop — imaging + specialist; worsening night pain with weight loss — tumor screening; fever with back pain — infection, blood count and inflammatory markers.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user walks this step before writing any treatment plan.

Key point: Saddle numbness with bowel/bladder dysfunction is a cauda equina emergency, hours matter; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Piriformis Syndrome

Piriformis syndrome is a condition clinically distinct from disc sciatica yet easily confused with it: the piriformis runs from the anterior sacrum (S2–S4) through the greater sciatic foramen to the greater trochanter, with the sciatic nerve passing beneath it (or partly through it). When the piriformis develops a trigger point, it mechanically compresses the sciatic nerve outside the spinal canal, producing ｢sciatica｣ without any disc lesion.

Structurally, the piriformis externally rotates and abducts the hip. By the sciatic nerve–piriformis relationship (Beaton types), about 84% of nerves pass entirely below the piriformis (type A), about 12% have the common peroneal nerve partly through it (type B), and a few pass above (type C). In types B and C, a piriformis trigger point can directly compress the nerve. The surrounding gluteus medius and minimus are almost always co-involved — gluteus minimus trigger points can refer widely to the ankle, the representative muscle of ｢pseudo-sciatica｣.

By channel, the piriformis region lies exactly on the Foot-Shaoyang and Foot-Taiyang path through the posterior hip. GB30 (Huantiao), on the outer third of the trochanter-to-sacral-hiatus line, reaches all three gluteal muscles and the piriformis at once — the most powerful single local point for hip and sciatic conditions; BL54 (Zhibian), at the fourth posterior sacral foramen, faces the piriformis origin anatomically. Most piriformis syndrome carries a cold component (worse in cold-damp weather and sitting on cold surfaces) plus chronic overuse leading to Blood stasis.

In its ordinary course piriformis syndrome is mainly excess: cold-damp obstructs the Foot-Shaoyang sinew and chronic overuse brings Blood stasis, the piriformis contracts into a knot and entraps the sciatic nerve — deep buttock ache, worse sitting, radiation mostly not past the knee. Differentiation from disc sciatica is the crux: past the knee + SLR positive + dermatomal numbness means disc; buttock-dominant + worse sitting + FAIR positive + SLR negative means piriformis, and the two can coexist.

Clinical pursuit of piriformis syndrome differentiates from disc sciatica first, then fixes the cold-stasis nature: step one uses FAIR/SLR to separate piriformis from disc; step two palpates the piriformis MTrP (pressure reproducing the familiar sciatic radiation confirms it); step three recognizes post-surgical residual pain, a commonly missed type; and finally cold-stasis-deficiency settles the Qi dynamics. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Deep buttock pain radiating to the leg — first separate piriformis from disc; trace by probability:

Cold-Damp Blood-Stasis Piriformis Obstruction (Piriformis Syndrome)

Principle: Warm the channel and scatter cold, transform stasis and release the knot, release the piriformis MTrP — treat the piriformis to cure the pseudo-sciatica

Target muscle / point: Piriformis belly (at GB30 Huantiao, the sciatic notch) plus gluteus medius and minimus trigger points; this layer is the absolute mainstay, all three almost always co-involved

Technique: Prone or side-lying with hip flexed, locate the oblique piriformis from PSIS to trochanter, palpate the deep taut nodule 4–6 cm medial to the trochanter, needle deep 4–6 cm (piriformis lies deep) to elicit a whole-muscle twitch; FAIR improves immediately after release. Also needle gluteus medius (PSIS-trochanter line, fanning laterally) and minimus (slightly deeper and more lateral).

Safety: The deep piriformis lies against the sciatic nerve: needle on clear muscular contact, stop at the twitch, and on any sustained electric sensation withdraw and redirect — never needle directly into the nerve.

Entry: After MTrP release, insert subcutaneously in the buttock, tip toward the piriformis lesion

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes-adducts-internally rotates the hip (FAIR direction) or walks slowly, sweeping to release the gluteal fascia.

Primary points: GB30 (Huantiao), BL54 (Zhibian), BL40 (Weizhong), GB34 (Yanglingquan)

Adjunct points: GB39 (Xuanzhong), local Ashi points, posterior-leg component add BL57 (Chengshan), cold cases moxa GV4 (Mingmen)

Point rationale: GB30 (Huantiao), on the outer third of the trochanter-to-sacral-hiatus line, reaches all three gluteal muscles and the piriformis at once — the most powerful single local point for hip and sciatic conditions; BL54 (Zhibian), at the fourth posterior sacral foramen, faces the piriformis origin; BL40 (Weizhong) releases posterior-leg Bladder-channel tension; GB34 (Yanglingquan), the sinew meeting, benefits the hip.

External therapy & care: Piriformis stretch (supine flexion-adduction-internal rotation) and gluteal release; correct prolonged sitting, avoid hard cold surfaces and leg-crossing — chronic overuse and cold exposure are the seeds of relapse.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with the Danggui Sini intent

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, combined with Dang Gui, Xi Xin, Tong Cao to warm the channel; add Niu Xi, Mu Gua to guide down and relax the sinew and benefit the hip

Piriformis syndrome mostly carries cold with stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan warms, unblocks, and slowly dissolves the contracted knot, and the Danggui Sini intent warms the channel to scatter the cold leg, with Niu Xi and Mu Gua guiding downward and relaxing the sinew — matching cold-stasis obstruction of the hip sinew.

Textbook reference: Juan Bi Tang (leg modification)

Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Gui Zhi, Qin Jiao, Hai Feng Teng, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao; add Niu Xi, Mu Gua to guide down and relax sinew

Dispels wind-damp, warms and scatters cold, frees the collaterals; Niu Xi and Mu Gua lead downward — a general formula for leg painful obstruction.

Formula analysis: The key here is in the needle, not the herb: deep GB30 release of the piriformis MTrP is sovereign, gluteus medius/minimus release is minister, herbs warming and transforming stasis are assistant. Guizhi Fuling Wan combination favors marked cold-stasis; Juan Bi Tang favors diffuse painful obstruction.

Modifications:

  • Marked cold with cold leg — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Gui Zhi
  • Long stasis with night pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Di Long
  • Recurrent overuse strain — favor hip external-rotator release and gluteal strength balance training

Key point: Deep GB30 needling seeking a whole-piriformis twitch is the core of this pattern; gluteus medius/minimus are almost always co-involved and released together; FAIR positive + SLR negative + worse sitting is the divide from disc sciatica.

Post-Lumbar-Surgery Residual Sciatic Pain (Missed Piriformis Type)

Principle: Release the pre-operatively missed piriformis MTrP — treat the residual knot

Target muscle / point: Piriformis belly (GB30, multi-needle approach) plus gluteus medius and minimus trigger points; this is the true source of the residual pain

Technique: Prone, multi-needle approach to the piriformis region (long-needle BL54 approach) seeking the twitch; also release gluteus medius/minimus (GB30 approach). After release, reassess residual sciatic pain and FAIR. Technique as in the cold-stasis type, with emphasis on finding and treating the pre-operatively missed knot.

Safety: The post-surgical region may have scar adhesion; needle avoiding the sciatic nerve, stop at the twitch, withdraw and redirect on electric sensation.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the buttock, tip toward the piriformis lesion

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: slow hip flexion-internal rotation or walking, releasing post-surgical gluteal fascial adhesion.

Primary points: GB30 (Huantiao), BL54 (Zhibian), BL40 (Weizhong), GB34 (Yanglingquan)

Adjunct points: GB39 (Xuanzhong), local Ashi points, encircling needling around GB30 to release adhesion

Point rationale: Points as in the cold-stasis type — GB30 (Huantiao) and BL54 (Zhibian) take the piriformis directly, BL40 (Weizhong) distally releases the Bladder channel; post-surgical adhesion can be released by encircling needling around GB30.

External therapy & care: Post-surgical rehabilitation with piriformis stretch and gluteal activation; correct prolonged sitting, avoid chronic single-side loading.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with collateral-freeing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, add Niu Xi, Di Long, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals (post-surgical stasis-adhesion)

Post-surgical residual mostly carries stasis with adhesion; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, with Di Long and Ji Xue Teng freeing the collaterals and dispersing adhesion, Niu Xi guiding downward — matching post-surgical residual stasis obstruction.

Textbook reference: Shen Tong Zhu Yu Tang (leg modification)

Qin Jiao, Chuan Xiong, Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Gan Cao, Qiang Huo, Mo Yao, Dang Gui, Wu Ling Zhi, Xiang Fu, Niu Xi, Di Long

Quickens Blood, moves Qi, frees the collaterals; for post-surgical stasis-adhesion with leg painful obstruction.

Formula analysis: The key here is still the needle: finding and releasing the pre-operatively missed piriformis MTrP is decisive, with herbs transforming stasis and dispersing adhesion as adjunct.

Modifications:

  • Marked adhesion — add Di Long, a small dose of Shui Zhi to free collaterals and disperse binding
  • Post-surgical Qi-Blood deficiency — add Huang Qi, Dang Gui to nourish Blood and support the upright
  • Marked cold — add Gui Zhi, blast-fried Fu Zi

Key point: In any persistent post-surgical sciatica with buttock-dominant pain, worse sitting, post-op SLR negative, FAIR positive — the piriformis is the source until proven otherwise; this pattern is extremely common and almost always untreated.

Disc Sciatica (Must Exclude · Not This Disease)

Principle: This is true sciatica, not a piriformis treatment — full treatment in the Sciatica chapter

Target muscle / point: Not applicable to piriformis technique; the true radicular target is the involved lumbar segment (see the Sciatica chapter)

Technique: This is a differentiation item: for radiation past the knee, SLR positive, dermatomal signs, transfer to the Sciatica chapter's radicular type, do not transfer piriformis deep needling.

Safety: In true radicular cases, strong piriformis-region stimulation is unhelpful and may mask the picture; establish the diagnosis first.

Entry: Not applicable (see the Sciatica chapter radicular type)

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: See the Sciatica chapter radicular type

Adjunct points: See the Sciatica chapter

Point rationale: The true radicular target is the lumbar nerve root and the Taiyang/Shaoyang line, different from the piriformis type; here only differentiation, not repeated treatment.

External therapy & care: See the Sciatica chapter.

Classical formula: See the Sciatica chapter

Radicular type: Shenzhuo Decoction with collateral-freeing / Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang with Fuzi

True radicular herbal treatment is in the Sciatica chapter, not repeated.

Textbook reference: See the Sciatica chapter

Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang with Fuzi

As above.

Formula analysis: This item is a differentiation, not this disease's treatment; for confirmed disc sciatica, full treatment is navigated to the Sciatica chapter.

Key point: Past the knee + SLR positive + dermatomal numbness = true sciatica, see the Sciatica chapter; it can coexist with the piriformis type — prioritize, do not treat only one.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, no needling; recognize, refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling, complete referral documentation, and grade the urgency.

Safety: Saddle numbness + bowel/bladder dysfunction + bilateral leg weakness = cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency, delay measured in hours.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of a critical condition.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: cauda equina signs — emergency; progressive weakness/foot drop — imaging + specialist; worsening night pain with weight loss — tumor screening; deep buttock pain with fever and redness — deep infection workup.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes a true emergency before writing any treatment plan.

Key point: Saddle numbness with bowel/bladder dysfunction is a cauda equina emergency; do not mislabel a true emergency as ｢piriformis｣ — once cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Headache (Myofascial · Cervicogenic)

This chapter covers only myofascial and cervicogenic headache — headache whose source lies in the head-and-neck muscles and cervical spine and can be released by needling. Internal-origin headache (Liver-Yang-rising migraine, Phlegm-damp obscuring clear Yang tension-type, Qi-Blood deficiency dull ache, etc.) has its own methods — see the Internal Medicine headache chapter. This chapter and the Internal headache chapter are warp and weft: one treats ｢source in the sinew｣, the other ｢source in the organ｣.

By channel, the location of head pain fixes the channel: occiput-nape (Foot-Taiyang · Du Mai) is mostly wind-cold or cervicogenic, temporal (Foot-Shaoyang) mostly cervicogenic or Shaoyang, vertex (Jueyin Liver) and whole-head (Qi-Blood deficiency) mostly internal, transferred to Internal Medicine. Myofascial/cervicogenic headache mainly involves the Taiyang and Shaoyang path over neck and occiput — the trigger points of upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and suboccipital muscles are its palpable lesions.

Structurally, the mechanism of cervicogenic headache is the ｢trigeminocervical nucleus｣: C1–C3 nerves and the trigeminal nerve converge here, so trigger points of the upper cervical spine, suboccipital, and upper trapezius can ｢project｣ pain to occiput, temple, and behind the eye, even sensitizing this nucleus to lower the migraine threshold. This explains why treating the neck can stop a ｢headache｣, and even abort some migraine attacks.

In its ordinary course, myofascial/cervicogenic headache is mostly excess: wind-cold invading Taiyang (acute occipital pain) or phlegm-stasis obstructing the cervico-occipital junction (cervicogenic); myofascial tension-type is a ｢head-band tightness｣ from sustained tension of the pericranial muscles (upper trapezius, temporalis, occipitofrontalis). The common thread: pressing the responsible muscle precisely reproduces the headache, and neck movement can provoke it — this is the divide from internal-origin headache.

Clinical pursuit of headache screens warning signs first, then separates myofascial/cervicogenic from internal: step one asks the pain nature-location and whether there is aura or nausea (to identify migraine and other internal types); step two uses MTrP palpation and the neck-movement reproduction test to fix cervicogenic; step three fixes the responsible muscle and channel; and finally cold-stasis settles the Qi dynamics. Any headache precisely reproduced by pressing the MTrP is myofascial/cervicogenic, treated here; otherwise transfer to Internal Medicine. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Headache — first separate ｢source in the sinew｣ (this chapter) from ｢source in the organ｣ (Internal Medicine); trace by probability:

Cervicogenic Headache (Upper Trapezius · Suboccipital Referral)

Principle: Release the upper trapezius and suboccipital trigger points, open the neck's channel Qi, treat the neck to cure the head

Target muscle / point: Upper trapezius (referring to the temple), suboccipital four muscles (referring behind the eye), sternocleidomastoid trigger points; this layer is the main battleground

Technique: Pincer-palpate the upper trapezius to find the active point, oblique-needle to elicit the twitch that reproduces then dissolves the ｢headache｣; palpate the suboccipital muscles at the occipital ridge with the head slightly flexed supine, shallow-needle to release, not deep toward the foramen magnum; pincer-fix the sternocleidomastoid and needle shallowly.

Safety: The deep suboccipital lies near the foramen magnum and vertebral artery — needle shallowly with clear bony contact, never deep inward-upward; the sternocleidomastoid needling avoids the carotid sinus.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the upper thoracic region, tip toward the cranial neck-occiput

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly rotates and extends the neck; referred headache often eases immediately after upper trapezius and suboccipital release.

Primary points: GB20 (Fengchi), BL10 (Tianzhu), EX-HN5 (Taiyang), GV20 (Baihui)

Adjunct points: SI3 (Houxi) distal, GB21 (Jianjing), LV3 (Taichong) to soothe the Liver, local Ashi points

Point rationale: GB20 (Fengchi) sits at the lateral margin of the suboccipital muscles, essential for head-neck wind disorders and cervicogenic headache, needled toward the opposite eye canthus; BL10 (Tianzhu) frees the Taiyang to the occiput; SI3 (Houxi) distally opens the Du Mai; stress types add LV3 (Taichong) to soothe the Liver and lower the trapezius tension.

External therapy & care: Chin-tuck and deep cervical flexor training; limiting screen time and correcting forward-head posture is the root of maintaining the effect.

Classical formula: Gegen Tang (★Classical Original★) with additions

Gegen Tang as base, add Chuan Xiong, Bai Zhi to ascend to the head; with Liver constraint add Chai Hu, Bai Shao

Ge Gen relaxes the posterior neck sinew and opens the Taiyang Qi; once the neck sinew loosens the referred headache eases; Chuan Xiong and Bai Zhi carry the medicine up to the head and face — matching the ｢treat the neck to cure the head｣ mechanism of cervicogenic headache.

Textbook reference: Chuanxiong Chatiao San (cervicogenic modification)

Chuan Xiong, Jing Jie, Bai Zhi, Qiang Huo, Xi Xin, Fang Feng, Bo He, Gan Cao; add Ge Gen, Man Jing Zi

The general formula for dispelling wind and stopping headache, with Chuan Xiong as the headache herb carrying medicine up, plus Ge Gen to guide to the neck and Man Jing Zi to clear the head and eyes — suited to wind with a cervicogenic component.

Formula analysis: Gegen Tang chiefly relaxes the neck and opens the Taiyang, treating the cervicogenic root; Chuanxiong Chatiao San chiefly dispels wind and stops pain, treating the head-face branch. Marked cervicogenic pain with nape stiffness — the former; wind headache predominant — the latter, or combine.

Modifications:

  • Occiput-dominant, marked Taiyang — heavy Ge Gen, add Qiang Huo
  • Temple-dominant, marked Shaoyang — add Chai Hu, Huang Qin
  • Deep retro-orbital pain, marked suboccipital — add Bai Zhi, Man Jing Zi

Key point: Pressing the responsible muscle to precisely reproduce the ｢headache｣ is this pattern's diagnostic moment; no aura, no nausea, shifting with neck posture is the divide from migraine; same mechanism as the cervicogenic headache pattern in the Neck Pain chapter, cross-referable.

Myofascial Tension-Type Headache

Principle: Release the pericranial tension, soothe the Liver to ease its pull, raise clear Yang

Target muscle / point: Upper trapezius, temporalis, occipitofrontalis, galea aponeurotica tension points and trigger points

Technique: Oblique-needle the temporalis along its fibers to release the taut band, release along the occipitofrontalis and galea, pincer-palpate the upper trapezius to dissolve the point. The head muscles are thin, shallow needling suffices.

Safety: Temporal needling avoids the superficial temporal artery; scalp needling stays shallow, not deep.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the upper thoracic or cervico-scapular region, tip toward the cranium

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly rotates the neck and shrugs-and-depresses the shoulders; the band eases after pericranial release.

Primary points: GV20 (Baihui, raising clear Yang), EX-HN5 (Taiyang), GB20 (Fengchi), GB8 (Shuaigu, temporal)

Adjunct points: LV3 (Taichong) to soothe the Liver, LI4 (Hegu), GB21 (Jianjing), EX-HN3 (Yintang)

Point rationale: GV20 (Baihui) raises clear Yang and relieves vertex pressure; EX-HN5 (Taiyang) and GB8 (Shuaigu) release the temporal sinew; GB20 (Fengchi) frees the head-neck Qi; LV3 (Taichong) with LI4 (Hegu) opens the Four Gates, soothing the Liver to ease the pericranial tension.

External therapy & care: Self-massage release of temporalis and occipitofrontalis; stress reduction, regular routine, correcting jaw clenching (temporalis overuse).

Classical formula: Sini San (★Classical Original★) with Chuanxiong

Chai Hu, Shao Yao, Zhi Shi, Gan Cao; add Chuan Xiong, Bai Zhi to ascend and stop pain

Tension-type often carries Liver constraint with pericranial tension; Sini San soothes the Liver and resolves constraint, coursing out the constrained Yang to ease the tension, with Chuan Xiong and Bai Zhi carrying medicine up to stop headache — matching the Qi-stagnation-with-muscle-tension mechanism.

Textbook reference: Chuanxiong Chatiao San

Chuan Xiong, Jing Jie, Bai Zhi, Qiang Huo, Xi Xin, Fang Feng, Bo He, Gan Cao

The general dispel-wind stop-headache formula, with Chuan Xiong carrying medicine up, suited to myofascial tension with wind.

Formula analysis: Sini San favors soothing the Liver and easing tension, treating the root of stress-related pericranial tension; Chuanxiong Chatiao San favors dispelling wind and stopping pain, treating the head-face branch. Marked Liver constraint with stress — the former; wind headache predominant — the latter. If heavy foggy head with phlegm-damp, it is internal, transfer to Internal Medicine.

Modifications:

  • Marked Liver constraint, high stress — add Xiang Fu, Yu Jin
  • Temporalis overuse, night grinding — add Bai Shao, Mu Gua to relax spasm
  • With Blood-deficiency malnourishment — add Dang Gui, Bai Shao (avoid over-warming-drying)

Key point: ｢Head-band tightness｣ bilateral pressing with pericranial tension and tenderness is this pattern; heavy foggy head with greasy coat is phlegm-damp internal, transfer to the Internal Medicine headache chapter.

Wind-Cold Invading Taiyang Headache (Acute Occipito-Nape Pain)

Principle: Dispel wind and scatter cold, open the Taiyang channel Qi

Target muscle / point: Secondarily tense upper trapezius, suboccipital muscles, and splenius of the occipito-nape

Technique: Oblique-needle the tense occipito-nape muscles to release and elicit a local reaction; cold cases take warm needling or Taiyang-channel moxibustion.

Safety: The suboccipital region near the foramen magnum takes only shallow needling with clear bony contact.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the upper thoracic region, tip toward the neck-occiput

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly rotates and extends the neck; pair with upper-back Bladder-channel cupping to help expel wind-cold.

Primary points: GB20 (Fengchi), BL10 (Tianzhu), GV16 (Fengfu), GV14 (Dazhui)

Adjunct points: SI3 (Houxi) distal, BL12 (Fengmen), EX-HN5 (Taiyang)

Point rationale: GB20 (Fengchi) and GV16 (Fengfu) dispel wind-cold at the head-neck exterior; BL10 (Tianzhu) and GV14 (Dazhui) open the Taiyang and Du Mai; SI3 (Houxi) distally frees the Du Mai; BL12 (Fengmen) with Dazhui dispels wind-cold from the exterior.

External therapy & care: Upper-back Bladder-channel cupping expels wind-cold; keep the occiput-nape warm and shielded from wind-cold, usually eased in one to three sessions.

Classical formula: Gegen Tang (★Classical Original★)

Ge Gen 4 liang, Ma Huang 3 liang, Gui Zhi 2 liang, Sheng Jiang 3 liang, Shao Yao 2 liang, Gan Cao 2 liang, Da Zao 12 pieces (Ge Gen now from 15–30 g); severe headache add Chuan Xiong, Bai Zhi

The Shang Han Lun states: for Taiyang disease with stiff rigid nape-and-back, absence of sweat and aversion to wind, Gegen Tang governs. Ge Gen relaxes the nape and opens the Taiyang, Ma Huang and Gui Zhi scatter cold and release the exterior — matching wind-cold invading Taiyang occipito-nape headache; add Chuan Xiong and Bai Zhi to ascend and stop pain.

Textbook reference: Chuanxiong Chatiao San

Chuan Xiong, Jing Jie, Bai Zhi, Qiang Huo, Xi Xin, Fang Feng, Bo He, Gan Cao

Dispels wind and scatters cold to stop headache, with Chuan Xiong carrying medicine up, suited to wind-cold headache.

Formula analysis: Gegen Tang chiefly opens the Taiyang and scatters cold, relaxing the nape, for nape stiffness with aversion to cold and no sweat; Chuanxiong Chatiao San chiefly dispels wind and stops pain, for wind predominance. Exterior-excess nape stiffness — the former; wind headache predominant — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Severe nape stiffness — heavy Ge Gen, add Qiang Huo
  • Vertex pain reaching Jueyin — add Gao Ben, Wu Zhu Yu
  • With cervicogenic myofascial component — refer to the cervicogenic headache needling

Key point: Sudden occipital pain and nape stiffness after cold exposure, worse with cold, is this pattern; upper-back cupping + Gegen Tang is often fast-acting; fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia together require excluding meningitis — go to warning signs.

Internal-Origin Headache (Liver-Yang / Phlegm-Damp / Qi-Blood Deficiency · to Internal Medicine)

Principle: This is internal-origin, source in the organ — full pattern-based treatment in the Internal Medicine headache chapter

Target muscle / point: Not applicable to this chapter's myofascial technique; if a cervicogenic myofascial component coexists, refer to the cervicogenic headache release of upper trapezius and suboccipital muscles

Technique: This is a differentiation item: for cases where MTrP pressure does not reproduce, neck movement does not provoke, with aura and nausea or heavy foggy head, transfer to the Internal Medicine headache chapter for internal treatment; if a cervicogenic component coexists, the two chapters can be treated together.

Safety: Internal-origin headache is treated chiefly by organ Qi-Blood pattern identification, not by local needling alone.

Entry: Not applicable (refer to cervicogenic headache when a cervicogenic component coexists)

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: See the Internal Medicine headache chapter (Liver-Yang: GB20 Fengchi, LV3 Taichong, KI3 Taixi anchoring Yang; phlegm-damp: ST40 Fenglong, GV20 Baihui; Qi-Blood deficiency: ST36 Zusanli, GV20 Baihui with supplementing)

Adjunct points: With a cervicogenic component, refer to the cervicogenic headache points

Point rationale: Internal-origin headache targets the organ Qi-Blood, different from myofascial/cervicogenic treatment; here only differentiation, full treatment in the Internal Medicine headache chapter, not repeated.

External therapy & care: See the Internal Medicine headache chapter.

Classical formula: See the Internal Medicine headache chapter

Liver-Yang migraine: Tianma Gouteng / Zhen Gan Xi Feng intent; phlegm-damp: Banxia Baizhu Tianma Tang; Qi-Blood deficiency: Ba Zhen / Gui Pi intent

Internal-origin headache herbal treatment is in the Internal Medicine headache chapter, not repeated here.

Textbook reference: See the Internal Medicine headache chapter

Choose by pattern

As above.

Formula analysis: This item is a differentiation, not this chapter's treatment; for confirmed internal-origin cases, the full ascent-descent-exit-entry treatment is navigated to the Internal Medicine headache chapter. When a cervicogenic myofascial component coexists, treating the two chapters together is often twice as effective.

Key point: MTrP pressure not reproducing, neck movement not provoking, with aura and nausea or heavy foggy head — this is internal-origin headache, transfer to the Internal Medicine headache chapter; a cervicogenic component can be treated in both chapters.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, no needling; recognize, emergency-refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and refer to emergency immediately.

Safety: Sudden ｢worst-ever｣ thunderclap headache = suspect subarachnoid hemorrhage; fever + neck stiffness + photophobia = suspect meningitis; waking worsening + projectile vomiting = raised intracranial pressure — all emergencies, hours matter.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of an intracranial emergency.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: thunderclap headache — emergency workup for subarachnoid hemorrhage; fever with neck stiffness and photophobia — emergency workup for meningitis; waking worsening with pupillary abnormality — emergency workup for raised ICP / mass; new onset over 50 with temporal tenderness — workup for temporal arteritis.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes an intracranial emergency before writing any headache treatment plan.

Key point: Thunderclap, fever with neck stiffness, waking worsening — these three require immediate emergency care; once warning signs are cleared, the myofascial/cervicogenic component returns to the needling pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Elbow Pain

The elbow is the pivot where the three hand-Yang and three hand-Yin channels cross the arm, its flexion, extension, and rotation depending on coordinated sinews and tendons. The lateral epicondyle is the attachment of all wrist and finger extensor common tendons, with the Hand-Yangming channel at the fore (LI11 Quchi lies over it); the medial epicondyle is the attachment of the wrist and finger flexor and pronator common tendons, with the Hand-Shaoyin and Hand-Jueyin channels nearby (HT3 Shaohai adjoins the medial epicondyle). Elbow pain is first differentiated by inner-outer channel and by aggravating movement.

Structurally, the core of elbow pain is mostly at the ｢enthesis｣ — the tendon-bone junction. The extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) enthesis at the lateral epicondyle and the flexor-pronator common tendon enthesis at the medial epicondyle are both avascular zones that heal poorly after repetitive overload, becoming chronic enthesopathy (modern histology shows degeneration rather than acute inflammation, the same as plantar fascia and Achilles tendon).

Pain location directly indicates the involved channel and common tendon: lateral pain worse with gripping and lifting is Hand-Yangming and the extensor common tendon (tennis elbow); medial pain worse with resisted wrist flexion and forearm pronation is Hand-Shaoyin/Jueyin and the flexor-pronator common tendon (golfer's elbow). Medial epicondyle disease, adjacent to the ulnar nerve, may carry ulnar tingling to the ring and little fingers, more disabling than the lateral form.

In its ordinary course, elbow pain is mostly repetitive strain causing enthesis Blood stasis and tendon degeneration; the elbow may also be invaded by wind-cold-damp with stiff pain worse in cold weather. And enthesis pain is often driven upstream by a ｢muscle-belly trigger point｣ — a trigger point in the forearm extensor or flexor belly refers back to the enthesis and drives its tension and inflammation, so treating the elbow cannot address the enthesis alone but must first release the upstream belly.

Clinical pursuit of elbow pain separates inner-outer channel first, then differentiates enthesis from cervicogenic: step one asks whether pain is medial or lateral, which movement aggravates, and occupation/sport; step two uses Cozen / golfer's-elbow tests to localize the epicondyle; step three palpates the forearm muscle-belly trigger point (the upstream driver of enthesis overload) and uses Spurling to exclude C6/C7 radicular referral (pseudo-tennis-elbow); and finally cold-stasis-deficiency settles the Qi dynamics. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain in the elbow — enthesis pain often arises from the upstream muscle belly; trace by probability:

Large-Intestine-Channel Stasis Elbow Pain (Tennis Elbow)

Principle: Quicken Blood and free the collaterals, release the forearm extensor belly trigger point, release the enthesis — belly first, then enthesis

Target muscle / point: Forearm extensor belly trigger point (4–6 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle) as the upstream driver, plus the lateral epicondyle ECRB enthesis; this layer is the mainstay, belly first then enthesis

Technique: First needle the forearm extensor belly trigger point to elicit the twitch, and Cozen pain eases immediately; then needle along the bone face at the lateral epicondyle enthesis in several directions (equivalent to needling tendinopathy to stimulate collagen remodeling). Post-enthesis needling may bring 24–48 hours of soreness, to be forewarned.

Safety: Lateral epicondyle needling along the bone face is safe; the deep radial nerve branch is near the lateral elbow, do not probe blindly into the forearm depth.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid-forearm, tip toward LI11 (the lateral epicondyle)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly extends the wrist against gravity while the clinician sweeps; FSN restores extensor fascial circulation and responds well in chronic tennis elbow.

Primary points: LI11 (Quchi), LI10 (Shousanli), local Ashi point (enthesis), LI4 (Hegu)

Adjunct points: TE5 (Waiguan), LI12 (Zhouliao), forearm extensor belly trigger point

Point rationale: LI11 (Quchi) lies over the lateral epicondyle, a key Hand-Yangming point; LI10 (Shousanli) sits over the forearm extensor belly, directly releasing the upstream driver; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Hand-Yangming Qi; together they free the channel and quicken Blood, release the tendon and benefit the elbow.

External therapy & care: Eccentric wrist-extensor training remodels the tendon; a counterforce brace reduces enthesis load; avoid repetitive gripping and lifting and forceful gripping, and change grip posture.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with collateral-freeing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, add Jiang Huang to guide to the arm, Ji Xue Teng and Sang Zhi to free the collaterals and benefit the joint

Chronic enthesopathy's formed stasis cannot be removed abruptly; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, with Jiang Huang guiding to the arm and Sang Zhi–Ji Xue Teng freeing the collaterals — matching tennis elbow enthesis stasis.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (upper-limb modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Jing Jie, Du Huo, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Qing Pi, Wu Jia Pi, Du Zhong, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke; remove Niu Xi, add Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood; removing the downward-guiding Niu Xi and adding upward Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi, it consolidates the belly release and enthesis treatment.

Formula analysis: The key here is the needle (belly first, then enthesis), herbs adjunct: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors slowly dissolving enthesis stasis, Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang favors freeing the collaterals and relaxing the sinew. Hard stasis-knot enthesis pain — the former; diffuse sinew strain — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Marked stasis with night pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Yan Hu Suo
  • With wind-cold stiffness — add Gui Zhi, Qiang Huo
  • Long illness Qi-Blood deficiency with slow healing — add Huang Qi, Dang Gui

Key point: Enthesis pain is driven by the upstream muscle-belly trigger point; the sequence needles the belly first then addresses the enthesis; eccentric training and changed grip posture are the root of relapse prevention.

Pericardium-Channel Stasis Elbow Pain (Golfer's Elbow)

Principle: Quicken Blood and free the collaterals, release the forearm flexor belly trigger point, release the enthesis — belly first, then enthesis, avoid the ulnar nerve

Target muscle / point: Forearm flexor belly trigger point (4–6 cm distal to the medial epicondyle) as the upstream driver, plus the medial epicondyle flexor-pronator enthesis

Technique: First needle the forearm flexor belly trigger point to elicit the twitch; then needle along the bone face at the medial epicondyle enthesis, staying anterior to the ulnar groove and avoiding the ulnar nerve. Post-enthesis needling may bring soreness, to be forewarned.

Safety: Medial epicondyle needling must avoid the ulnar groove (the ulnar nerve lies behind it) — operate anterior to the groove with gentle stimulation; on ulnar-side finger electric sensation, withdraw and redirect.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the medial mid-forearm, tip toward the medial epicondyle

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes the wrist and pronates against resistance; sweeping avoids the ulnar-nerve course.

Primary points: HT3 (Shaohai, draining or gentle stimulation), PC3 (Quze), local Ashi point (enthesis), forearm flexor belly trigger point

Adjunct points: PC6 (Neiguan), HT7 (Shenmen), SI8 (Xiaohai, note ulnar-nerve proximity)

Point rationale: HT3 (Shaohai), the Heart He-sea point near the medial epicondyle, is drained or gently stimulated to free and quicken while avoiding strong stimulation near the ulnar nerve; PC3 (Quze) at the medial elbow releases the flexor origin; together they free the Hand-Shaoyin/Jueyin Qi and release the flexor common tendon.

External therapy & care: Eccentric wrist-flexor and pronator training; a counterforce brace reduces enthesis load; avoid repetitive forceful wrist flexion, pronation, and throwing.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with collateral-freeing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, add Jiang Huang to guide to the arm, Ji Xue Teng and Sang Zhi to free the collaterals
