Same enthesis-stasis mechanism as tennis elbow; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the formed enthesis stasis, with Jiang Huang, Sang Zhi, and Ji Xue Teng guiding to the arm and freeing the collaterals.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (upper-limb modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Wu Jia Pi, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke; remove Niu Xi, add Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood, consolidating the flexor belly release and enthesis treatment.

Formula analysis: Same as tennis elbow: needling belly-first-then-enthesis leads, herbs transforming stasis and freeing collaterals adjunct; the medial side especially must avoid the ulnar nerve.

Modifications:

  • With ulnar tingling — add Quan Xie, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals (and assess the degree of ulnar entrapment)
  • Marked stasis with night pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao
  • Long illness Qi-Blood deficiency — add Huang Qi, Dang Gui

Key point: The medial epicondyle adjoins the ulnar nerve — needle anterior to the ulnar groove, avoiding the nerve; progressive ulnar intrinsic wasting requires warning-sign assessment.

Cervicogenic Referred Elbow Pain (C6/C7 Radicular · Pseudo-tennis-elbow)

Principle: This is cervicogenic, target in the neck — full treatment in the Neck Pain radicular type

Target muscle / point: Deep splenius and semispinalis at the involved cervical segment (C6/C7), plus overlaid forearm extensor trigger points; the true tennis elbow enthesis is not the main battleground

Technique: This is a differentiation item: for positive Spurling and neck-movement provocation, transfer to the Neck Pain radicular type; the overlaid forearm trigger points can be released together, but the root is cervical decompression.

Safety: Cervical needling toward the lamina is safe; on arm electric sensation, withdraw and redirect.

Entry: See the Neck Pain radicular type (insert subcutaneously proximal on the radiation line toward the cervical segment)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves neck and affected arm, comparing radiation extent.

Primary points: See the Neck Pain radicular type (EX-B2 Jiaji C6/C7, GB20 Fengchi, GV14 Dazhui, LI11 Quchi distal)

Adjunct points: Hand-Yangming distal LI4 (Hegu), LI11 (Quchi)

Point rationale: The cervicogenic target is the cervical root and the Hand-Yangming line; here only differentiation, full treatment in the Neck Pain radicular type, not repeated.

External therapy & care: See the Neck Pain radicular type; limit prolonged neck flexion, deep cervical flexor training.

Classical formula: See the Neck Pain radicular type

Gegen Tang with the Guizhi-jia-Gegen intent, combined by cold-stasis-deficiency

Cervicogenic treatment is in the Neck Pain radicular type, not repeated.

Textbook reference: See the Neck Pain radicular type

Juan Bi Tang with insect medicinals

As above.

Formula analysis: This item is a differentiation, not this disease's treatment; for confirmed cervicogenic cases, full treatment is navigated to the Neck Pain radicular type; it can coexist with true tennis elbow — prioritize.

Key point: Before diagnosing tennis elbow, always palpate the forearm extensor MTrP and perform Spurling; neck-movement provocation + Spurling positive = cervicogenic, target in the neck not the elbow.

Wind-Cold-Damp Obstruction Elbow Pain

Principle: Dispel wind, scatter cold, dispel damp, warm and free the elbow Qi

Target muscle / point: Elbow sinew taut bands, around the lateral and medial epicondyles, and the forearm belly

Technique: Oblique-needle the taut sinew to release and elicit a local reaction, cold cases with warm needling or local moxibustion; this type often overlays enthesopathy and the main enthesis must be addressed together.

Safety: Medial epicondyle avoids the ulnar nerve, lateral along the bone face; cold-case moxibustion avoids burns.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid-forearm, tip toward the painful epicondyle

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes-extends elbow and wrist, warming and freeing the elbow fascia.

Primary points: LI11 (Quchi), LI10 (Shousanli), TE5 (Waiguan), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: LI4 (Hegu), LI12 (Zhouliao), moxa for cold cases

Point rationale: LI11 (Quchi) and LI10 (Shousanli) free the Hand-Yangming and benefit the elbow Qi; TE5 (Waiguan) opens the Yang Wei and dispels wind; together they dispel wind-cold-damp and warm-free the elbow sinew, with moxa for cold.

External therapy & care: Keep the elbow warm and shielded from wind-cold-damp; avoid prolonged elbow use in cold environments.

Classical formula: Gui Zhi Tang (★Classical Original★) with Yi Yi Ren

Gui Zhi, Shao Yao, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao, Gan Cao, add Yi Yi Ren to dispel damp, Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb; add Qiang Huo for severe cold

Gui Zhi Tang harmonizes Ying and Wei and releases the flesh to scatter cold, with Yi Yi Ren dispelling damp and Jiang Huang–Sang Zhi guiding to the arm — matching wind-cold-damp obstruction of the elbow sinew; combine the Ma Xing Yi Gan intent for heavy damp.

Textbook reference: Juan Bi Tang (upper-limb modification)

Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Gui Zhi, Qin Jiao, Hai Feng Teng, Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao; add Jiang Huang, Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb

Dispels wind-damp, warms the channel, frees the collaterals to stop pain; Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi guide to the upper limb — a general formula for upper-limb painful obstruction.

Formula analysis: Gui Zhi Tang with Yi Yi Ren favors harmonizing Ying-Wei and dispelling damp, for exterior-deficiency wind-cold-damp; Juan Bi Tang favors dispelling wind-damp and freeing collaterals, for diffuse painful obstruction. Exterior-deficiency sweating — the former; heavy wind-damp — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Heavy damp with stiff pain — add Cang Zhu, Fang Ji
  • Severe cold — add blast-fried Fu Zi, Xi Xin
  • With enthesis stasis-knot — combine the Guizhi Fuling Wan intent, add Jiang Huang, Tao Ren

Key point: This type often overlays chronic enthesopathy and must be prioritized against pure epicondylitis, addressing the main enthesis together; moxa for cold cases.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Red hot swollen with fever = infection, no needling; progressive ulnar intrinsic wasting = advancing ulnar entrapment, requires nerve conduction and specialist assessment.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of infection, fracture, or nerve injury.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: red hot swollen with fever — infection, blood count and inflammatory markers; trauma deformity — imaging and immobilization; ulnar intrinsic wasting + progressive numbness — ulnar entrapment, nerve conduction + specialist; worsening night rest pain with weight loss — tumor screening.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes these non-first-line-acupuncture situations before writing any elbow treatment plan.

Key point: Red hot swollen with fever — exclude infection first; do not let progressive ulnar intrinsic wasting linger as golfer's elbow; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Ankle Pain

The ankle is the pivot of the foot, bearing the whole body's weight and governing gait. The three foot-Yang channels descend past the lateral ankle (Stomach, Gallbladder, Bladder), the three foot-Yin channels ascend past the medial ankle (Spleen, Liver, Kidney). The lateral ankle belongs to Foot-Shaoyang and Foot-Taiyang (GB40 Qiuxu, BL60 Kunlun lie there), the medial ankle to Foot-Shaoyin and Foot-Taiyin (KI3 Taixi, SP6 Sanyinjiao nearby), the posterior heel to Foot-Taiyang (the Achilles tendon and its calcaneal insertion, BL60 Kunlun and BL61 Pucan adjacent). Fixing the channel by pain location is the first measuring rod of ankle-pain localization.

Structurally, ankle stability depends on the medial and lateral ligaments and tendons: the lateral anterior talofibular (ATFL) and calcaneofibular (CFL) ligaments guard against inversion, the medial deltoid ligament is stronger and its injury rare but severe; posteriorly the Achilles tendon is the body's strongest tendon governing push-off, its mid-portion (2–6 cm above the calcaneal insertion) an avascular ｢critical zone｣ prone to degeneration under chronic overload. About 85% of ankle sprains are lateral inversion, directly involving ATFL and CFL.

Pain location and history indicate the involved structure: anteroinferior lateral malleolar pain after an inversion sprain with positive anterior drawer is ATFL/CFL (lateral instability); posterior ankle or Achilles-course pain worse with activity is the Achilles tendon (the most distal Bladder-channel sinew); posteroinferior medial malleolar pain with flatfoot is mostly tibialis posterior tendinopathy. Palpating the point of maximal tenderness to fix the structure and channel leads ankle-foot differentiation.

In its ordinary course, acute ankle pain is mostly trauma causing ligament Blood stasis (fresh sprain); chronic: an incompletely healed old sprain leaves ligament laxity and recurrent sprains (chronic instability), and chronic Achilles overload leads to degenerative tendinopathy. Deficiency appears in the elderly with Liver-Kidney insufficiency and malnourished sinews. So acute is Blood stasis, chronic is laxity or degeneration with underlying deficiency.

Clinical pursuit of ankle pain screens warning signs first (fracture · Achilles rupture), then localizes and characterizes: step one asks acute-versus-chronic and the sprain mechanism; step two uses Ottawa-ankle-rule thinking to exclude fracture and the Thompson test to exclude Achilles rupture; step three uses anterior drawer/talar tilt to fix ligament laxity and palpation to fix the sorest structure; and finally stasis-deficiency settles the Qi dynamics. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain in the ankle-foot — trace the responsible structure by probability:

Blood-Stasis Ankle Pain (Acute Lateral Sprain)

Principle: Quicken Blood and transform stasis, reduce swelling and free the collaterals; immobilize and reduce swelling first in the acute phase, then free the collaterals and restore stability

Target muscle / point: Anteroinferior lateral malleolar ATFL/CFL Ashi points, peroneus longus/brevis taut bands; in the acute swelling phase keep it light, distal-dominant

Technique: Within the acute 48 hours keep local needling light, take distal and contralateral-balance points first; after swelling subsides, encircle the lateral malleolar pain points with Ashi needling and pair with gentle ankle motion. Old stasis spots may take a small amount of collateral-pricking bloodletting.

Safety: Exclude fracture before needling; needle the anteroinferior lateral malleolus shallowly, avoiding the dorsalis pedis artery and superficial peroneal nerve.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the distal lateral leg, tip toward the lateral malleolar pain area

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: after swelling subsides, patient slowly dorsiflexes-plantarflexes and inverts-everts (pain-free range), restoring fascial circulation.

Primary points: GB40 (Qiuxu), BL60 (Kunlun), GB34 (Yanglingquan), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: SP6 (Sanyinjiao), ST41 (Jiexi), BL62 (Shenmai), marked stasis add collateral-pricking cupping

Point rationale: GB40 (Qiuxu) at the lateral malleolus takes the Foot-Shaoyang lateral stasis directly; BL60 (Kunlun) with BL62 (Shenmai) frees the Foot-Taiyang and benefits the ankle; GB34 (Yanglingquan), the sinew meeting, benefits the joint; acute stasis-swelling may take local collateral-pricking bloodletting.

External therapy & care: Acute-phase RICE (relative rest, ice, compression, elevation); warm compress after 48 hours to aid movement; early protected weight-bearing and ankle pumps to prevent stiffness.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts, add Niu Xi to guide downward, Hong Hua, Ru Xiang, Mo Yao to reduce swelling and stop pain

For acute sprain stasis-swelling, Guizhi Fuling Wan warms, unblocks, and dissolves stasis, with Niu Xi guiding downward and Ru-Mo-Hong Hua reducing swelling and settling pain — matching lateral malleolar Blood stasis.

Textbook reference: Qi Li San / Taohong Siwu Tang (trauma modification)

Taohong Siwu Tang (Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao, Sheng Di) add Niu Xi, Mu Gua to guide down and relax sinew; for severe cases take Qi Li San infused

Quickens Blood, transforms stasis, reduces swelling and stops pain — a general formula for acute soft-tissue injury stasis-swelling.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors warm slow-dissolving, for stasis with cold or deficiency; Taohong Siwu Tang favors quickening and nourishing Blood, for stasis without cold-deficiency. Acute excess stasis-swelling — the latter; with cold or slow dissolving — the former.

Modifications:

  • Marked swelling — add Yi Yi Ren, Ze Xie, Fang Ji to drain water and reduce swelling
  • Severe stasis pain — add Yan Hu Suo, Ru Xiang, Mo Yao
  • Late-phase Blood-nourishing collateral-freeing to prevent chronic instability — add Ji Xue Teng, Dang Gui, Xu Duan

Key point: Acute — exclude fracture first, immobilize and reduce swelling first; proper handling prevents progression to chronic instability, and early proprioception training is the key.

Old Sprain · Chronic Lateral Ankle Instability (Ligament Laxity)

Principle: Nourish and quicken Blood, free the collaterals to aid ligament healing, strengthen the peroneal muscles to compensate for stability, and secure the root

Target muscle / point: Lax lateral ligament insertion zones, peroneus longus/brevis (lateral dynamic stabilizers) taut or weak bands

Technique: Encircle the ligament insertion zones with Ashi needling to stimulate healing (equivalent to needling tendinopathy for remodeling), release/activate the peroneal muscles; the emphasis is on pairing with strength and proprioception training, not needling alone.

Safety: Shallow lateral malleolar needling, avoiding the superficial peroneal nerve; local work only in the chronic phase without acute red swelling.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the distal lateral leg, tip toward the lateral malleolar ligament zone

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly inverts-everts and single-leg balances, activating the peroneal muscles and proprioception.

Primary points: GB40 (Qiuxu), BL60 (Kunlun), GB34 (Yanglingquan), KI3 (Taixi)

Adjunct points: BL62 (Shenmai), GB39 (Xuanzhong), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), local ligament Ashi points

Point rationale: GB40 (Qiuxu) and BL60 (Kunlun) free the lateral Foot-Shaoyang/Taiyang and benefit the ligaments; GB34 (Yanglingquan) the sinew meeting and GB39 (Xuanzhong) the marrow meeting strengthen sinew and bone; KI3 (Taixi) supplements the Kidney and nourishes the sinew to aid healing.

External therapy & care: Peroneal strength training, single-leg balance and proprioception training (the root of preventing re-sprain); an ankle brace for marked instability.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) intent with Blood-nourishing collateral-freeing

With Liver-Kidney insufficiency as base take the Shen Qi Wan intent (Di Huang, Shan Yao, Shan Zhu Yu, etc.), combined with Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Xu Duan to nourish Blood, free collaterals, and strengthen the sinew

Chronic instability with malnourished ligaments — supplement Liver-Kidney to strengthen the root of sinew and bone, nourish and quicken Blood to aid ligament healing, matching the long-injured unbinding mechanism.

Textbook reference: Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang

Shu Di, Dang Gui, Niu Xi, Shan Zhu Yu, Fu Ling, Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Bai Shao, Qing Pi, Wu Jia Pi

Supplements Liver-Kidney, strengthens sinew and bone, nourishes Blood and frees the collaterals — a key formula for chronic sinew injury with instability and ligament laxity.

Formula analysis: The Shen Qi Wan intent favors warm-supplementing Kidney-Yang to secure the root, Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang favors supplementing Liver-Kidney and nourishing Blood to strengthen the sinew. Yang-deficiency aversion to cold — the former intent; Blood-deficiency weak sinew — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Recurrent sprains, weak sinew — heavy Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Wu Jia Pi
  • Old stasis unresolved — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Ji Xue Teng
  • Qi deficiency — add Huang Qi, Dang Shen to support

Key point: Chronic instability weighs needling and training equally; strength and proprioception training is the root of preventing re-sprain; needles and herbs only aid ligament healing, stability comes from muscular compensation.

Achilles Tendinopathy Ankle Pain (Most Distal Bladder-Channel Tendinopathy)

Principle: Quicken Blood and free the collaterals, release upstream gastrocnemius-soleus tension, stimulate tendon remodeling by needling

Target muscle / point: Achilles mid-portion thickened tender zone, plus upstream gastrocnemius and soleus trigger points (their tension increases Achilles load)

Technique: First release the gastrocnemius-soleus trigger points (BL57 Chengshan region) to reduce upstream tension; then needle alongside the Achilles mid-portion along the tendon (equivalent to needling tendinopathy to stimulate collagen remodeling), post-needling soreness to be forewarned. Needle beside the tendon body, not transecting it.

Safety: Avoid direct needling of the tendon body causing injury; do not probe deep into the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid-posterior calf, tip toward the Achilles

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly raises and lowers the heel (eccentric); sweeping releases the posterior calf fascia.

Primary points: BL57 (Chengshan), BL60 (Kunlun), BL61 (Pucan), local Achilles-side Ashi points

Adjunct points: KI3 (Taixi), GB34 (Yanglingquan), BL58 (Feiyang)

Point rationale: BL57 (Chengshan) releases upstream gastrocnemius tension, BL60 (Kunlun) and BL61 (Pucan) adjoin the Achilles insertion and free the most distal Foot-Taiyang; KI3 (Taixi) supplements the Kidney and nourishes the sinew; GB34 (Yanglingquan) the sinew meeting benefits the tendon.

External therapy & care: Eccentric heel-raise training (core Achilles-tendinopathy rehab); avoid sudden increases in mileage or hill running, change to cushioned shoes.

Classical formula: Shaoyao Gancao Tang (★Classical Original★) with Niu Xi and Mu Gua

Bai Shao, Zhi Gan Cao from 30 g each, add Niu Xi to guide down, Mu Gua to relax sinew, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals

Sour-sweet transforming Yin, relaxing the sinew's tension to soften the Achilles' rigidity, with Niu Xi guiding down and Mu Gua relaxing sinew and benefiting the ankle — matching the tendon's rigid degeneration.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (leg modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Wu Jia Pi, Niu Xi, Hong Hua, Mu Gua, Shen Jin Cao

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood; Niu Xi, Mu Gua, and Shen Jin Cao guide down and relax the sinew to benefit the tendon, consolidating the Achilles release.

Formula analysis: Shaoyao Gancao with additions favors relaxing the sinew and softening the tendon, for rigidity predominant; Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang favors quickening Blood and relaxing sinew, for strain-stasis. Rigid stiff pain — the former; strain-stasis — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Marked degenerative thickening — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Tu Bie Chong to quicken Blood and soften hardness
  • Liver-Kidney insufficiency (elderly) — add Du Zhong, Xu Duan, Shu Di
  • Insertional type (at the calcaneus) — different technique, specialist assessment for Haglund deformity

Key point: Achilles tendinopathy must release upstream gastrocnemius-soleus tension plus eccentric training; Thompson positive, unable to raise the heel is a rupture — go to warning signs.

Medial Tibialis Posterior Tendinopathy Ankle Pain

Principle: Quicken Blood and free the collaterals, release tibialis posterior tension, strengthen the foot intrinsics to support the arch, and supplement Liver-Kidney to nourish the sinew

Target muscle / point: Tibialis posterior tendon course (posteroinferior medial malleolus) tender zone, plus upstream tibialis posterior and the foot intrinsics

Technique: Needle alongside the tibialis posterior tendon at the posteroinferior medial malleolus to release, activate the foot intrinsics (paired with active arch contraction training); post-needling soreness possible.

Safety: The posterior medial malleolus is the passage of the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle — needle shallowly, avoiding the tarsal-tunnel neurovasculature, do not probe deep.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the distal medial leg, tip toward the posteroinferior medial malleolus

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly inverts the foot and raises the heel with arch contraction, activating tibialis posterior and the foot intrinsics.

Primary points: KI3 (Taixi), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), BL60 (Kunlun), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: KI6 (Zhaohai), SP9 (Yinlingquan), GB34 (Yanglingquan)

Point rationale: KI3 (Taixi) and SP6 (Sanyinjiao) free the Foot-Shaoyin/Taiyin and supplement Liver-Kidney to nourish the medial malleolar sinew; KI6 (Zhaohai) opens the Yin Qiao at the inferior medial malleolus; together they free the medial channel Qi and strengthen the arch-supporting sinew.

External therapy & care: Foot-intrinsic (short-foot exercise) and tibialis posterior strength training to support the arch; orthotic insoles supporting the medial longitudinal arch for flatfoot.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) intent with Blood-nourishing sinew-strengthening

Take the Shen Qi Wan intent to supplement Liver-Kidney, combined with Dang Gui, Bai Shao, Niu Xi, Mu Gua, Xu Duan to nourish Blood, relax and strengthen the sinew

The tibialis posterior tendon is malnourished and the arch collapses — supplement Liver-Kidney to strengthen the root of the sinew, nourish and relax the sinew to strengthen the arch-supporting force, matching medial sinew degeneration.

Textbook reference: Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang

Shu Di, Dang Gui, Niu Xi, Shan Zhu Yu, Fu Ling, Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Bai Shao, Qing Pi, Wu Jia Pi

Supplements Liver-Kidney, strengthens sinew and bone, nourishes Blood and frees the collaterals, suited to sinew degeneration and flatfoot pain.

Formula analysis: The Shen Qi Wan intent favors warm-supplementing, Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang favors nourishing Blood and strengthening the sinew. Yang-deficiency — the former intent; Blood-deficiency weak sinew — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Progressive arch collapse — heavy Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Wu Jia Pi, plus orthotic support
  • Damp swelling — add Yi Yi Ren, Ze Xie
  • Stasis — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Ji Xue Teng

Key point: Tibialis posterior tendinopathy centers on arch support — foot-intrinsic and tibialis posterior training plus orthotic support; progressive rigid flatfoot is referred to orthopedics.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Inability to bear weight after trauma + bony tenderness = suspect fracture; posterior ｢kicked｣ sensation + unable to raise heel + Thompson positive = Achilles rupture, urgent referral.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of fracture, tendon rupture, or infection.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: unable to bear weight + bony tenderness — imaging to exclude fracture (Ottawa thinking); Achilles rupture — urgent orthopedic referral; red hot swollen with fever — joint fluid to exclude infection; sudden red swollen severe first-MTP pain — exclude crystal arthritis such as gout (see the Gout chapter).

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes fracture and tendon rupture before writing any ankle treatment plan.

Key point: Exclude fracture for trauma inability to bear weight, exclude Achilles rupture for the posterior kicked sensation; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Plantar Heel Pain (Plantar Fasciitis)

The sole belongs to Foot-Taiyin and Foot-Shaoyin crossing the base of the foot, with KI1 (Yongquan) at the sole, the most distal Foot-Shaoyin point. The plantar fascia arises from the medial calcaneal tubercle and fans to the five toes like a bowstring maintaining the foot's longitudinal arch. Plantar fasciitis is the most common clinical foot condition — essentially a chronic degenerative fasciopathy at the calcaneal insertion (not acute inflammation), the same pathology as tennis elbow and Achilles tendinopathy (collagen disarray, avascular degeneration), so ｢-itis｣ is truly a misnomer.

Structurally, plantar heel pain is maintained by three anatomical layers: one in the calf — gastrocnemius/soleus tension reduces ankle dorsiflexion and increases plantar fascia tension per step; two in the sole — the abductor hallucis and other foot intrinsics, whose trigger points refer to the heel and great toe and are almost always hypertonic; three at the calcaneal insertion — the degeneration at the fascial attachment itself. Treating one layer alone leaves the fascial tension unresolved, so plantar fasciitis must be treated across all three.

Pattern recognition identifies this disease: worst heel pain on the first step out of bed in the morning, easing after 5–10 minutes of walking, recurring after prolonged standing — is classic plantar fasciitis (insertional type); pain in the mid-arch is the mid-substance type. During overnight rest, Blood stasis pools at the fascia-calcaneus attachment, and the first-step morning stretch tears micro-adhesions and hurts; after walking the fascia warms with Blood flow and loosens.

In its ordinary course, plantar fasciitis's branch is plantar Foot-Shaoyin Blood stasis (fascial insertion at the KI1 region), its root is Kidney deficiency — the Kidney governs bone and generates marrow, and the calcaneus-fascia interface depends on Kidney essence for structural integrity; the elderly with depleted Kidney essence suffer bone withering and interface degeneration (the calcaneal spur is TCM ｢bone Bi｣ from Kidney deficiency). So this disease is root-deficiency with branch-excess: supplement the Kidney to secure the root, quicken Blood to transform the insertion stasis, and release the three anatomical layers' tension.

Clinical pursuit of plantar fasciitis distinguishes typical from atypical first (excluding stress fracture · nerve entrapment), then fixes the three layers: step one asks whether there is first-step pain easing after walking (typical); step two palpates the medial calcaneal tubercle point of maximal tenderness (fixing the insertional type) and the windlass test; step three assesses gastrocnemius tension and abductor hallucis hypertonicity; and finally Kidney-deficiency root-branch settles the Qi dynamics. If pain is persistent or worse after walking, consider stress fracture or nerve entrapment (Baxter's nerve). The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain in the heel and sole — trace the responsible layer by probability:

Kidney-Deficiency Blood-Stasis Plantar Heel Pain (Plantar Fasciitis)

Principle: Supplement the Kidney to secure the root, quicken Blood and free the collaterals to transform the insertion stasis, release the three anatomical layers' tension — treat all three layers

Target muscle / point: Three layers together: (1) gastrocnemius/soleus trigger points (BL57 Chengshan region, upstream) (2) abductor hallucis and foot-intrinsic trigger points (medial plantar margin) (3) calcaneal insertion fascia (medial attachment)

Technique: First release the gastrocnemius-soleus trigger points to reduce upstream tension (whatever the heel pain, always treat the gastrocnemius); then needle the abductor hallucis — from the medial foot surface, 1–2 cm above the plantar surface, angled toward the sole, to elicit a foot-cramping twitch (this step is the most painful, forewarn; the immediate heel-pain relief after is often marked); finally needle the medial calcaneal insertion toward the calcaneal bone face, 2–3 needles (equivalent to needling tendinopathy to stimulate collagen remodeling), with marked 24–48 hour soreness after, to be forewarned.

Safety: Plantar needling avoids the plantar arterial arch and tibial-nerve plantar branches; the calcaneal insertion is needled toward the bone face, not too deep; caution with plantar needling in diabetic foot or reduced sensation.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid-posterior calf, tip toward the heel/Achilles

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly dorsiflexes-plantarflexes and raises-lowers the heel; upstream posterior-calf fascial release indirectly reduces plantar fascia tension.

Primary points: KI3 (Taixi), KI6 (Zhaohai), BL57 (Chengshan), BL60 (Kunlun), KI1 (Yongquan)

Adjunct points: BL23 (Shenshu, moxa for Yang-deficiency type), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), local Ashi points, BL57–KI1 electroacupuncture 2/100 Hz

Point rationale: KI3 (Taixi) enriches the Kidney and strengthens bone, KI6 (Zhaohai) opens the Yin Qiao specifically connecting to the heel, BL23 (Shenshu) supplements the Kidney root — treating the Kidney-deficiency root; BL57 (Chengshan) releases the upstream gastrocnemius, BL60 (Kunlun) at the ankle, KI1 (Yongquan) at the most distal plantar insertion — treating the Blood-stasis branch; root and branch together.

External therapy & care: Gastrocnemius and plantar fascia stretching, foot-intrinsic (short-foot exercise) strengthening; a night dorsiflexion splint and cushioned insoles reduce the morning first-step stretch pain; weight control reduces load.

Classical formula: Ba Wei Shen Qi Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gan Di Huang 8 liang, Shan Yao 4 liang, Shan Zhu Yu 4 liang, Ze Xie 3 liang, Fu Ling 3 liang, Mu Dan Pi 3 liang, Gui Zhi 1 liang, Fu Zi 1 piece (blast-fried); combined with Niu Xi to guide down, Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Ji Xue Teng to transform the insertion stasis

Shen Qi Wan supplements the Kidney root and seeks Yang within Yin to secure the degenerating source of the calcaneus-fascia interface, combined with Niu Xi-Tao-Hong-Ji Xue Teng to guide down and transform the formed insertion stasis — matching plantar fasciitis's Kidney-deficiency Blood-stasis root and branch. For Yin-deficiency fire, reduce Gui-Fu toward the Liu Wei method.

Textbook reference: Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang with Blood-quickening

Shu Di, Dang Gui, Niu Xi, Shan Zhu Yu, Fu Ling, Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Bai Shao, Qing Pi, Wu Jia Pi; add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Mu Gua

Supplements Liver-Kidney and strengthens sinew and bone as root, adding Tao-Hong-Mu Gua to quicken Blood and relax sinew to benefit the foot as branch, suited to Liver-Kidney deficiency with stasis heel pain.

Formula analysis: The Shen Qi Wan combination favors warm-supplementing Kidney-Yang with stasis transformation, for Yang-deficiency aversion to cold; Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang favors nourishing Blood and strengthening the sinew with quickening, for Blood-deficiency weak sinew. Yang-deficiency cold heel — the former; Blood-deficiency weak sinew — the latter.

Modifications:

  • Calcaneal spur, marked stasis — heavy Tao Ren, Hong Hua, add Tu Bie Chong to quicken Blood and soften hardness
  • Yin-deficiency fire, hot soles — reduce Gui-Fu, add Zhi Mu, Huang Bai, Gui Ban
  • Heavy damp swelling — add Yi Yi Ren, Cang Zhu, Ze Xie

Key point: Plantar fasciitis treats all three layers: whatever the location, always release the gastrocnemius; abductor hallucis needling gives often-marked immediate relief (forewarn of the pain); post-insertion soreness is normal; Kidney deficiency is the root, the course measured in months, and stretching training with load reduction is the root of maintenance.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend plantar-needling procedures and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Persistent pain or worse after walking (not first-step pain) = suspect calcaneal stress fracture, image first; sudden plantar tearing sensation with depression = plantar fascia rupture, specialist assessment.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, plantar needling may delay recognition of stress fracture, nerve entrapment, or rupture.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: not-first-step pain worse after walking — imaging to exclude calcaneal stress fracture; medial heel numbness radiation — Baxter's nerve entrapment, nerve examination; sudden tearing sensation with depression — plantar fascia rupture, specialist; bilateral heel pain + polyarthritis / psoriasis / inflammatory back pain — rheumatologic assessment to exclude seronegative spondyloarthropathy.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes stress fracture and systemic inflammatory disease before writing any plantar heel-pain treatment plan.

Key point: Not-first-step pain worse after walking — exclude stress fracture; bilateral heel pain with polyarthritis — exclude spondyloarthropathy; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Wrist Pain

The wrist is the gate where the three hand-Yin and three hand-Yang channels cross into the hand, palm and dorsum belonging to Yin and Yang: the palmar side to the three hand-Yin — the Pericardium's carpal tunnel (the median nerve and flexor tendons passing beneath PC7 Daling), the Lung's radial wrist (LU9 Taiyuan); the dorsoradial side to the Hand-Yangming Large Intestine (the LI5 snuffbox region, where the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendon sheaths lie); the dorsoulnar side to Hand-Taiyang and Hand-Shaoyang (where the TFCC and dorsal wrist ligaments lie). Wrist pain is differentiated first by palm-dorsum-radial-ulnar to fix the channel, then by quality to fix the disease.

Structurally, the three types of wrist pain each belong to a different tissue layer and channel: median nerve compression in the carpal tunnel (beneath the flexor retinaculum) on the palmar side is carpal tunnel syndrome (Pericardium · PC7 Daling gate); inflammation of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons in their shared sheath at the radial styloid on the dorsoradial side is De Quervain's tenosynovitis (Large Intestine · colloquially mother's wrist); injury of the intercarpal ligaments (scapholunate most common) or the triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) causes wrist instability. The three types differ in location, trigger, and tests.

Pain location and quality directly sort the type: palmar-radial numbness and tingling in three and a half digits (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring), worse at night, eased by shaking, is carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve); pain and swelling at the radial styloid and thumb base, worse with thumb movement and gripping, is mother's wrist (Large Intestine sheath); diffuse wrist pain, worse with loading, dorsal aching, is wrist instability / TFCC. Palmar numbness is Yin-channel compression, radial pain-swelling is Large Intestine, dorsal pain is Taiyang-Shaoyang.

In its ordinary course, wrist pain is mostly strain: carpal tunnel syndrome is mostly repetitive wrist activity or systemic disease (hypothyroidism, pregnancy, diabetes, rheumatoid) compressing the median nerve, in TCM the Pericardium channel obstructed at the Daling gate, phlegm-damp or Blood stasis blocking its narrowest point; mother's wrist is mostly repetitive thumb-radial-deviation load (new mothers, hairdressers, assembly workers), the Large Intestine channel obstructed at the radial styloid sheath; instability is mostly ligament failing to heal after trauma.

Clinical pursuit of wrist pain fixes palm-dorsum-radial-ulnar first, confirms by special tests, and excludes cervicogenic: step one asks the exact location and quality (numbness / pain-swelling / aching), night symptoms, occupational injury history; step two uses Phalen/Tinel/Durkan for the carpal tunnel, Finkelstein for mother's wrist, palpation for instability; step three uses Spurling to exclude cervical source (median nerve compression can arise at the cervical root — compare all levels); and finally stasis-damp-deficiency settles the Qi dynamics. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain in the wrist — trace the responsible structure and channel by probability:

Pericardium-Channel Obstruction Wrist Pain (Carpal Tunnel Syndrome)

Principle: Free the Pericardium Qi, transform phlegm-damp and Blood stasis, release the flexor retinaculum's compression — also release the upstream forearm flexors

Target muscle / point: Carpal tunnel region (PC7 Daling · flexor retinaculum) Ashi points, and the upstream forearm flexor taut bands (whose tension increases tunnel pressure)

Technique: First release the forearm flexor belly taut bands to reduce upstream tension; then needle shallowly around the tunnel (avoiding the median nerve body) to release the retinaculum's compression, the PC7 (Daling) region needled shallowly to avoid the neurovascular bundle. For severe acute numbness keep it light, cautious with strong stimulation.

Safety: The carpal tunnel is dense with the median nerve and vessels — needle PC7 (Daling) shallowly, avoiding direct needling of the nerve; on fingertip electric sensation withdraw and redirect; refer severe wasting for surgical evaluation.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid-to-distal palmar forearm, tip toward the carpal tunnel

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes-extends wrist and fingers; releasing the forearm flexor fascia reduces tunnel pressure. Avoid the median nerve course.

Primary points: PC7 (Daling), PC6 (Neiguan), PC8 (Laogong), local Ashi points

Adjunct points: PC3 (Quze), forearm flexor belly trigger point, LI4 (Hegu), TE5 (Waiguan)

Point rationale: PC7 (Daling) at the tunnel, the Hand-Jueyin Yuan-source point, directly frees the obstruction at the tunnel gate; PC6 (Neiguan) frees the Pericardium channel and benefits the forearm; PC8 (Laogong) distally guides the channel Qi; with the forearm flexor belly to release upstream tension. Together they free the channel, transform phlegm-stasis, and release the tunnel's compression.

External therapy & care: A neutral-wrist night splint (reduces nocturnal tunnel pressure); avoid prolonged wrist flexion or repetitive wrist movements; change mouse/keyboard posture; treat reversible causes (hypothyroidism, blood sugar).

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with phlegm-transforming collateral-freeing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; for phlegm-damp add Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Bai Jie Zi, for Blood stasis add Ji Xue Teng, Jiang Huang to guide to the arm

Carpal tunnel phlegm-damp and Blood stasis obstruct the gate; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, with Ban Xia and Bai Jie Zi to transform phlegm and Ji Xue Teng and Jiang Huang to free the collaterals and guide to the arm, matching Pericardium-channel obstruction.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (upper-limb modification) with Er Chen

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Wu Jia Pi, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke; remove Niu Xi, add Jiang Huang, Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb; for phlegm-damp combine Er Chen Tang

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood; for heavy phlegm-damp combine Er Chen to transform phlegm, with Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi guiding to the upper limb, consolidating the tunnel release.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors dissolving the stasis knot, for Blood stasis predominant; combining Er Chen / adding Bai Jie Zi favors transforming phlegm, for phlegm-damp predominant. Stabbing pain with marked stasis — the former; numb distension with phlegm-damp — the phlegm-transforming method. Needling (upstream + tunnel release) leads, herbs adjunct.

Modifications:

  • Severe numbness — add Quan Xie, Ji Xue Teng, Huang Qi (boost Qi and free the collaterals)
  • Phlegm-damp swelling — add Ban Xia, Bai Jie Zi, Yi Yi Ren
  • Pregnancy-secondary — mostly self-resolves postpartum, use caution with Blood-quickening/breaking; rely on acupuncture and splinting

Key point: Carpal tunnel syndrome first releases the upstream forearm flexors + a neutral-wrist night splint; PC7 Daling is needled shallowly to avoid the median nerve; progressive thenar wasting is severe — go to warning signs for surgical evaluation.

Large-Intestine-Channel Obstruction Wrist Pain (De Quervain's Tenosynovitis · Mother's Wrist)

Principle: Free the Large Intestine Qi, quicken Blood and reduce swelling, release the radial styloid sheath's congestion

Target muscle / point: Radial styloid sheath region (snuffbox · LI5 region) Ashi points, and the upstream forearm APL/EPB

Technique: First release the forearm abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis belly taut bands to reduce upstream tension; then encircle the radial styloid sheath with Ashi needling (equivalent to needling tenosynovitis for remodeling), avoiding the superficial radial nerve and radial artery. Post-needling soreness to be forewarned.

Safety: The snuffbox contains the radial artery and superficial radial nerve — needle shallowly, avoiding the vessel and nerve; use caution with strong stimulation when acute swelling is marked.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the mid dorsoradial forearm, tip toward the radial styloid

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly abducts the thumb and radially deviates the wrist; releasing the forearm extensor fascia reduces sheath tension.

Primary points: LI5 (Yangxi), LI4 (Hegu), local Ashi points (radial styloid sheath), forearm APL/EPB trigger points

Adjunct points: LU7 (Lieque), LI11 (Quchi), LI10 (Shousanli)

Point rationale: LI5 (Yangxi) right at the radial styloid snuffbox, a Hand-Yangming point, directly frees the sheath's congestion; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Large Intestine Qi; LU7 (Lieque) at the radial side benefits the wrist; with the forearm thumb-muscle trigger points to release upstream. Together they free the channel, quicken Blood, and release the sheath.

External therapy & care: A thumb-spica splint for immobilization (reduces sheath load); avoid repetitive thumb radial deviation and lifting (new mothers change carrying posture); eccentric training for later remodeling.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening swelling-reducing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Jiang Huang to guide to the arm, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals, Ru Xiang and Mo Yao to reduce swelling and stop pain

Sheath stasis with swelling and pain; Guizhi Fuling Wan dissolves the stasis knot, with Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi guiding to the arm and Ru-Mo reducing swelling and settling pain, matching Large-Intestine radial-styloid-segment obstruction.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (upper-limb modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Dang Gui, Xu Duan, Wu Jia Pi, Hong Hua, Zhi Ke; remove Niu Xi, add Jiang Huang, Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood, with Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi guiding to the upper limb, consolidating the sheath release; for acute swelling and pain add heat-clearing Blood-quickening.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors dissolving the stasis knot, for chronic sheath stasis; for acute red hot swollen pain add heat-clearing Blood-quickening (Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Chi Shao). Needling (upstream + encircling the sheath) leads, immobilization is the root of relapse prevention.

Modifications:

  • Acute red swelling — add Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Chi Shao to clear heat and quicken Blood
  • Marked stasis pain — add Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Yan Hu Suo
  • Postpartum Qi-Blood deficiency — add Huang Qi, Dang Gui to aid healing

Key point: Mother's wrist first releases the upstream forearm thumb muscles + encircles the radial styloid sheath; the snuffbox avoids the radial artery and radial nerve; a thumb-spica splint and changed carrying posture are the root of relapse prevention.

Cervicogenic Referred Wrist Pain (C6/C7 Radicular · Exclude)

Principle: This is cervicogenic, target in the neck — full treatment in the Neck Pain radicular type

Target muscle / point: Deep splenius and semispinalis at the involved cervical segment (C6/C7), plus overlaid wrist-hand symptoms; the true wrist disease is not the main battleground

Technique: This is a differentiation item: for positive Spurling and neck-movement provocation, transfer to the Neck Pain radicular type; compare the neck, elbow, and wrist levels to determine the main compression site (in double-crush syndrome, prioritize).

Safety: Cervical needling toward the lamina is safe; on arm electric sensation, withdraw and redirect.

Entry: See the Neck Pain radicular type (insert subcutaneously proximal on the radiation line toward the cervical segment)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves neck and affected arm, comparing radiation extent.

Primary points: See the Neck Pain radicular type (EX-B2 Jiaji C6/C7, GB20 Fengchi, GV14 Dazhui, distal LI4 Hegu)

Adjunct points: Hand-Yangming distal LI4 (Hegu), LI11 (Quchi)

Point rationale: The cervicogenic target is the cervical root and channel line; here only differentiation, full treatment in the Neck Pain radicular type, not repeated.

External therapy & care: See the Neck Pain radicular type; limit prolonged neck flexion, deep cervical flexor training.

Classical formula: See the Neck Pain radicular type

Gegen Tang combined by cold-stasis-deficiency

Cervicogenic treatment is in the Neck Pain radicular type, not repeated.

Textbook reference: See the Neck Pain radicular type

Juan Bi Tang with insect medicinals

As above.

Formula analysis: This item is a differentiation, not this disease's treatment; for confirmed cervicogenic cases, full treatment is navigated to the Neck Pain radicular type; it can coexist with true carpal tunnel syndrome (double-crush syndrome) — prioritize.

Key point: Before diagnosing carpal tunnel, always use Spurling to compare the neck, elbow, and wrist levels; neck-movement provocation + Spurling positive = cervicogenic, target in the neck not the wrist; double-crush syndrome requires prioritizing.

Wrist Instability · TFCC Injury Wrist Pain

Principle: Nourish and quicken Blood to free the collaterals and aid ligament healing, strengthen the wrist muscles to compensate for stability, and secure the root

Target muscle / point: Injured ligament region (scapholunate / ulnar TFCC) Ashi points, and the wrist stabilizer muscles (wrist extensors/flexors) weak or taut bands

Technique: Encircle the injured ligament region with Ashi needling to stimulate healing (equivalent to needling tendinopathy for remodeling), activate the wrist stabilizer muscles; the emphasis is on pairing with wrist-muscle strength training and necessary immobilization, not needling alone.

Safety: The ulnar TFCC region avoids the ulnar nerve and ulnar artery; for acute injury immobilize and exclude fracture first, local work only without fracture.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the dorsal or ulnar forearm, tip toward the injured wrist region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly flexes-extends and rotates the wrist (pain-free range), activating the wrist stabilizer muscles.

Primary points: TE4 (Yangchi), SI4 (Wangu), LI5 (Yangxi), local ligament Ashi points

Adjunct points: TE5 (Waiguan), SI5 (Yanggu, ulnar side), LI4 (Hegu), wrist-muscle trigger points

Point rationale: TE4 (Yangchi) at the dorsal wrist center, SI4 (Wangu) at the ulnar side, LI5 (Yangxi) at the radial side — three points encircling the wrist free the Hand-Shaoyang/Taiyang/Yangming and benefit and stabilize the wrist; with wrist-muscle trigger points to release tension and local encircling needling to aid ligament healing.

External therapy & care: A wrist brace for immobilization (acute phase); wrist-muscle strength and proprioception training (the root of relapse prevention); avoid loaded rotation; refer severe TFCC injury to orthopedics.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-nourishing collateral-freeing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Xu Duan to nourish Blood, free collaterals, strengthen the sinew, Jiang Huang to guide to the arm

Wrist instability with failing ligament; Guizhi Fuling Wan dissolves old stasis, with Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Xu Duan nourishing Blood and freeing collaterals to aid healing, Jiang Huang guiding to the arm, matching the unbinding ligament mechanism.

Textbook reference: Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang (upper-limb modification)

Shu Di, Dang Gui, Niu Xi, Shan Zhu Yu, Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Bai Shao, Wu Jia Pi; remove Niu Xi, add Jiang Huang, Sang Zhi to guide to the upper limb

Supplements Liver-Kidney, strengthens sinew and bone, nourishes Blood and frees the collaterals to aid ligament healing; remove the downward-guiding Niu Xi, add upward Jiang Huang and Sang Zhi.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan with Blood-nourishing favors dissolving stasis and aiding healing, for old-injury stasis; Bu Shen Zhuang Jin Tang favors supplementing Liver-Kidney and strengthening the sinew, for weak malnourished sinew. Stasis — the former; weak sinew — the latter. Needles and herbs only aid healing, stability comes from muscular compensation.

Modifications:

  • Old-injury stasis — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Tu Bie Chong
  • Weak unstable sinew — heavy Xu Duan, Du Zhong, Wu Jia Pi
  • Ulnar TFCC pain — add Wei Ling Xian, Shen Jin Cao to free the collaterals

Key point: Wrist instability weighs needling and training equally; wrist-muscle strength and proprioception training is the root of relapse prevention; acute cases exclude fracture first; marked tear with functional impairment is referred to orthopedics.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Snuffbox tenderness after trauma = suspect scaphoid fracture (easily missed, leading to nonunion), imaging; progressive thenar wasting = severe carpal tunnel, nerve conduction + surgical evaluation.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of fracture, infection, inflammatory arthritis, or severe nerve entrapment.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: snuffbox tenderness after trauma — imaging to exclude scaphoid fracture (easily missed); red hot swollen with fever — blood count and inflammatory markers to exclude infection; symmetric polyarticular swelling with morning stiffness >1 hour — rheumatologic evaluation to exclude rheumatoid; progressive thenar wasting + persistent numbness — nerve conduction + surgical evaluation for severe carpal tunnel.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes scaphoid fracture, infection, inflammatory arthritis, and severe nerve entrapment before writing any wrist treatment plan.

Key point: Snuffbox tenderness after trauma — exclude scaphoid fracture (easily missed, leading to nonunion); symmetric polyarticular swelling — exclude rheumatoid; thenar wasting — exclude severe carpal tunnel; once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

TMJ Pain

TMJ pain is the pivot of the orofacial region, governing opening-closing and mastication; its pain is the most common orofacial pain clinically, often misdiagnosed or over-medicalized. Its root is mostly a combination of masticatory-muscle myofascial dysfunction and joint-capsule / disc dysfunction — problems of both the muscle and joint layers. By channel, the cheek and jaw are governed by the Foot-Yangming Stomach channel (ST6 Jiache, ST7 Xiaguan at its site), the pre-auricular region is traversed by Hand-Shaoyang and Hand-Taiyang (TE21 Ermen, SI19 Tinggong), and the temporal region is where Foot-Shaoyang Gallbladder and Foot-Yangming meet (ST8 Touwei at the anterosuperior temporalis).

Structurally, TMJ pain divides into two layers and four structures: the masseter (angle of the jaw to the zygomatic arch, pain at the jaw + pre-auricular + molar region) and temporalis (temporal region, pain reaching the temple + teeth) are the superficial masticatory muscles; the lateral and medial pterygoids are deep masticatory muscles (accessible intra- or extra-orally), lateral pterygoid dysfunction causing jaw deviation on opening and limited opening, medial pterygoid dysfunction causing pain on swallowing and ear fullness; the capsule and disc govern clicking (reducible disc displacement), locking (irreducible disc displacement), and limited opening (capsular tightness).

Pain location, opening range, and clicking directly localize the layer and structure: pain at the jaw + pre-auricular + molar region is mostly masseter, pain reaching the temple + teeth is mostly temporalis; clicking + jaw deviation on opening is mostly lateral pterygoid + disc; limited opening (<35mm) without clicking is mostly capsule or pterygoid tightness. Normal opening is about 40mm (three fingers), <35mm being limited. Worse in the morning is mostly nocturnal grinding, worse at night is mostly daytime clenching.

In its ordinary course, TMJ pain relates to emotion and occlusal habit: Liver Qi stagnates and rebels transversely on the Yangming, the masticatory muscles staying tense and causing grinding and clenching (the most common pattern of stress-related TMJ disorder, wiry pulse, slightly red tongue margins); in chronic cases local channel Qi-Blood stagnates, the joint space narrows, and the structure changes, with fixed pain refusing pressure, clicking, dark tongue. So the acute is Liver stagnation with muscle tension, the chronic is local Blood stasis, both with masticatory-muscle trigger points as the treatment core.

Clinical pursuit of TMJ pain fixes the pain location, opening range, and clicking first, palpates the masticatory-muscle trigger points, then differentiates the Qi dynamics: step one asks whether the pain is in the jaw / temple / ear / teeth, the opening-closing clicking, the maximum opening range, and morning or evening aggravation; step two palpates masseter / temporalis / pterygoid trigger points (palpable taut bands, local twitch on pressure, may refer to teeth and ear); step three differentiates Liver stagnation (stress grinding) from local Blood stasis (chronic structural change); and finally warning signs exclude organic joint disease and referred pain. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Pain in the TMJ — first fix the pain location, opening range, and clicking to distinguish the muscle and joint layers:

Liver Qi Stagnation TMJ Pain (Stress-related · Bruxism)

Principle: Course the Liver and resolve stagnation, release masticatory-muscle trigger points — treat the branch (grinding) with a splint, the root (source of tension) with trigger points + coursing the Liver

Target muscle / point: Masseter trigger points (jaw angle to zygomatic arch, taut bands) primarily, anterior temporalis trigger points secondarily; contralateral compensatory tension also addressed

Technique: Needle the masseter trigger points, elicit local twitch (for palpable taut bands with the jaw deviating to the tense pterygoid side, release the lateral pterygoid especially); add the anterior temporalis. ST6 (Jiache)–ST7 (Xiaguan) can take 2 Hz electroacupuncture for 20 minutes. In chronic cases release the contralateral masseter's compensatory tension together.

Safety: The face is richly vascular with dense facial nerve branches — needle shallowly avoiding vessels and nerve; intra-oral approach requires disinfection and depth control; avoid overly strong stimulation causing hematoma.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the superficial masseter beside the jaw angle or zygomatic arch, tip toward the trigger point

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly opens-closes and moves laterally; releasing the masseter-temporalis fascia reduces masticatory tension. Keep the facial sweep small.

Primary points: ST6 (Jiache), ST7 (Xiaguan), masseter Ashi points, LV3 (Taichong)

Adjunct points: ST8 (Touwei, temporalis), TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong), LI4 (Hegu), GB20 (Fengchi)

Point rationale: ST6 (Jiache) and ST7 (Xiaguan) are the key local points for jaw pain, freeing the Yangming facial Qi; masseter Ashi points directly release the trigger points; LV3 (Taichong) courses the Liver and resolves stagnation to treat the root; ST8 (Touwei) releases the temporalis; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Yangming (Hegu governs the face and mouth).

External therapy & care: Emotional regulation to reduce grinding-clenching (this pattern's root is stress); an occlusal splint for nocturnal grinding; avoid hard food, wide opening, unilateral chewing; jaw relaxation training.

Classical formula: Sini San (★Classical Original★) with cramp-easing

Chai Hu, Shao Yao, Zhi Shi, Gan Cao; for muscle tension and cramp add Ge Gen, heavy Bai Shao to ease the sinew, for concurrent heat add Mu Dan Pi, Zhi Zi

Sini San penetratingly resolves Liver stagnation and regulates Qi dynamics, matching Liver Qi rebelling on the Yangming; heavy Bai Shao with Gan Cao (the Shaoyao Gancao Tang intent) eases the masticatory cramp, with Ge Gen resolving the muscle and relaxing the neck-face sinew.

Textbook reference: Chaihu Shugan San with Shaoyao Gancao Tang

Chai Hu, Bai Shao, Zhi Ke, Chen Pi, Chuan Xiong, Xiang Fu, Gan Cao; combined with Shaoyao Gancao Tang to ease the sinew cramp

Course the Liver and move Qi to resolve stagnation, Shaoyao Gancao easing the masticatory cramp, addressing both branch and root of stress-related grinding.

Formula analysis: Sini San is the ancestral Liver-coursing Qi-regulating formula, concise and forceful; Chaihu Shugan San adds Xiang Fu, Chuan Xiong for stronger Qi-moving. Both combine Shaoyao Gancao Tang to ease the sinew cramp. Needling (releasing masticatory trigger points) leads, coursing the Liver and easing the sinew adjunct.

Modifications:

  • Severe muscle tension and cramp — heavy Bai Shao, add Ge Gen, Mu Gua
  • With heat (red tongue margins) — add Mu Dan Pi, Zhi Zi, Xia Ku Cao
  • Insomnia and anxiety — add Suan Zao Ren, He Huan Pi

Key point: The most common stress-related TMJ disorder; masseter trigger points treat the root (source of tension), a splint treats the branch (grinding); emotional regulation and jaw relaxation are the root of relapse prevention.

Temporalis Trigger Point TMJ Pain

Principle: Release temporalis trigger points, free the local channel Qi — often treated with the masseter

Target muscle / point: Anterior temporalis trigger points (at ST8 Touwei) primarily, palpable across the whole temporalis band; often coexists with masseter trigger points, treat together

Technique: Needle the anterior temporalis trigger points (at ST8 Touwei to release the anterior temporalis), elicit a local response; release tension along the temporalis band, with the masseter. Needle the temple shallowly, along the bone.

Safety: The superficial temporal artery runs in the temporal region — needle shallowly avoiding the artery; the temple is richly vascular, avoid hematoma.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the superficial temporalis of the temple, tip toward the trigger point

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly opens-closes and occludes; releasing the temporalis fascia. Keep the range small, avoid the superficial temporal artery.

Primary points: ST8 (Touwei), temporalis Ashi points, EX-HN5 (Taiyang), ST7 (Xiaguan)

Adjunct points: GB8 (Shuaigu), TE23 (Sizhukong), LI4 (Hegu)

Point rationale: ST8 (Touwei) at the anterosuperior temporalis directly releases the anterior temporalis trigger point; EX-HN5 (Taiyang) at the temple benefits the temporal channel Qi; ST7 (Xiaguan) frees the jaw; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Yangming.

External therapy & care: Avoid clenching and hard food; temporalis and masseter relaxation training; warm compress on the temple to aid release.

Classical formula: Shaoyao Gancao Tang (★Classical Original★) with Ge Gen

Bai Shao, Zhi Gan Cao 30g each, add Ge Gen to resolve the muscle, Chuan Xiong to move Qi upward

Shaoyao Gancao eases the temporalis cramp, Ge Gen resolves the muscle and relaxes the face-neck sinew, Chuan Xiong moves Qi upward, matching temporalis cramping.

Textbook reference: Chuanxiong Chatiao San (temporal referral modification)

Chuan Xiong, Jing Jie, Bai Zhi, Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Bo He, Gan Cao; for temporal referral pain add Man Jing Zi, Ju Hua

Dispels wind and stops pain, moves Qi upward, suited to temporal referred pain with head-face wind; for muscle tension predominant combine Shaoyao Gancao Tang to ease the sinew.

Formula analysis: Shaoyao Gancao with Ge Gen favors easing the sinew cramp, for muscle tightness predominant; Chuanxiong Chatiao San favors dispersing wind and ascending, for concurrent head-face wind referral. Muscle tightness — the former; wind referral — the latter. Needling to release the temporalis leads.

Modifications:

  • Muscle tightness — heavy Bai Shao, add Mu Gua
  • With head wind — add Man Jing Zi, Ju Hua, Bai Zhi
  • Comorbid with masseter — add ST6 Jiache, masseter Ashi points

Key point: Temporalis trigger points often coexist with the masseter and must be released together; ST8 Touwei is key to releasing the anterior temporalis; needle the temple shallowly to avoid the superficial temporal artery.

Lateral Pterygoid + Disc Dysfunction TMJ Pain (Clicking · Jaw Deviation)

Principle: Release the lateral pterygoid's tension, restore disc-condyle coordination — beyond the superficial muscles to pterygoid- and joint-specific handling

Target muscle / point: Lateral pterygoid (extra-oral approach: needle anterior to the condyle in the open position, up and in toward the infratemporal fossa; or intra-oral approach) primarily; disc coordination

Technique: Extra-oral approach: in the open position needle anterior to the condyle, directed up and in toward the infratemporal fossa to reach the lateral pterygoid; elicit a local response to release its tension. Add the pre-auricular joint region. The technique must be precise, unlike the superficial masseter.

Safety: The lateral pterygoid is deep, surrounded by dense vessels and nerves (maxillary artery, pterygoid plexus) — requires anatomical familiarity, controlling depth and direction, slow insertion; avoid hematoma; without confidence use the extra-oral superficial approach or refer to oral specialist.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously below the pre-auricular zygomatic arch, tip toward the pre-condylar lateral pterygoid region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly opens-closes, observing clicking and deviation changes; releasing the lateral pterygoid region fascia.

Primary points: TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong), ST7 (Xiaguan), lateral pterygoid Ashi points

Adjunct points: GB2 (Tinghui), ST6 (Jiache), LI4 (Hegu)

Point rationale: TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong), GB2 (Tinghui) at the pre-auricular joint region directly regulate the site of the lateral pterygoid and disc; ST7 (Xiaguan) frees the jaw and reaches anterior to the condyle; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Yangming face and mouth.

External therapy & care: Avoid wide opening, hard food, unilateral chewing; jaw control training (limit excessive protrusion); with marked clicking, a soft diet reduces load.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with sinew-relaxing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Ge Gen, Bai Shao to ease the sinew, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals

Long-standing disc displacement causes local stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, with Ge Gen and Bai Shao easing the pterygoid cramp and Ji Xue Teng freeing the collaterals to benefit the joint.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (head-face modification)

Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Dang Gui, Hong Hua, Bai Shao, Mu Gua; add Ge Gen to guide to the head-face, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals

Relaxes the sinew and quickens Blood, Ge Gen guiding to the head-face, aiding lateral pterygoid release and disc-condyle coordination.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors dissolving the stasis knot, for chronic disc-displacement stasis; Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang favors relaxing the sinew and freeing collaterals, for muscle tension and sinew stagnation. Needling (release lateral pterygoid + regulate joint) leads, herbs adjunct.

Modifications:

  • Marked stasis, long clicking — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Ji Xue Teng
  • Pterygoid cramp — heavy Bai Shao, add Mu Gua, Ge Gen
  • Progressing to locking — transfer to the capsule branch, acute locking to specialist reduction

Key point: Lateral pterygoid dysfunction causes disc displacement (clicking, deviation), requiring pterygoid- and joint-specific handling; the lateral pterygoid is deep with dense vessels and nerves, refer to oral specialist without confidence; progression to locking requires prompt reduction.

Capsule · Disc Dysfunction TMJ Pain (Limited Opening / Locking)

Principle: Release the capsule, free the channel and quicken Blood to benefit the joint; locking requires specialist manual reduction

Target muscle / point: Pre-auricular capsule region Ashi points, and the surrounding contracted masticatory muscles; irreducible disc displacement is not primarily needling-treated and requires reduction

Technique: Needle the pre-auricular joint region to release the capsule, with the surrounding masticatory muscles, and adjunct gentle joint mobilization (limited-opening type); locking (irreducible disc displacement) requires prompt specialist manual reduction, not resolved by needling alone.

Safety: The pre-auricular joint region avoids the superficial temporal artery and facial nerve; do not forcibly manipulate acute locking, refer promptly for reduction; delay makes reduction difficult.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the pre-auricular joint region, tip toward the capsule

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly opens-closes within the pain-free range; releasing the peri-capsular fascia.

Primary points: ST7 (Xiaguan), TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong), joint region Ashi points

Adjunct points: GB2 (Tinghui), ST6 (Jiache), TE17 (Yifeng), LI4 (Hegu)

Point rationale: ST7 (Xiaguan), TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong) encircle the pre-auricular joint, releasing the capsule and benefiting the joint; TE17 (Yifeng) behind the ear frees the local area; LI4 (Hegu) distally frees the Yangming; together they release the capsule and free the opening-closing.

External therapy & care: A soft diet reduces joint load; joint mobilization and opening training (progressive for the limited type); disc stabilization training after locking reduction; avoid wide opening.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with collateral-freeing joint-benefiting

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Wei Ling Xian, Shen Jin Cao, Ji Xue Teng to free the collaterals and benefit the joint

Capsular contracture with stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan dissolves stasis, with Wei Ling Xian and Shen Jin Cao freeing the collaterals to benefit the joint and release the capsular stagnation.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (joint modification)

Qiang Huo, Dang Gui, Hong Hua, Bai Shao, Mu Gua, Wei Ling Xian, Shen Jin Cao; Ge Gen guiding to the head-face

Relaxes the sinew, quickens Blood, frees the collaterals and benefits the joint, aiding capsular release and opening recovery.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors dissolving stasis and freeing collaterals, for capsular contracture with stasis; needling and joint mobilization lead, locking reduction is key. Herbs adjunctively soften and free the collaterals.

Modifications:

  • Capsular contracture with limited opening — add Wei Ling Xian, Shen Jin Cao, Ji Xue Teng
  • Marked stasis — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Tu Bie Chong
  • Locking — adjust after specialist manual reduction

Key point: Limited opening (capsular tightness) can improve with needling + joint mobilization; locking (irreducible disc displacement) requires prompt specialist manual reduction, delay making it difficult; a soft diet reduces load, progressive training.

Local Blood Stasis TMJ Pain (Chronic Structural Change)

Principle: Quicken Blood and resolve stasis to free the collaterals, release the chronically contracted masticatory muscles, and protect the joint

Target muscle / point: Chronically contracted masseter-temporalis-pterygoid trigger points, and the joint's local stasis region

Technique: Needle the chronically contracted masticatory-muscle trigger points, elicit a response to release; encircle the joint's local region to quicken Blood. For chronic structural change, treat with sustained effort and progression, protecting the joint from over-stimulation.

Safety: Needle the face shallowly avoiding vessels and nerves; do not forcibly manipulate a chronically degenerated joint; assess the degree of structural change by imaging.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously in the superficial masseter or temporalis, tip toward the stasis region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly opens-closes; releasing the chronically contracted masticatory-muscle fascia.

Primary points: ST6 (Jiache), ST7 (Xiaguan), masseter-temporalis Ashi points, SP10 (Xuehai)

Adjunct points: TE21 (Ermen), SI19 (Tinggong), LI4 (Hegu), LV3 (Taichong)

Point rationale: ST6 (Jiache), ST7 (Xiaguan) free the local jaw; masseter-temporalis Ashi points release the chronic contracture; SP10 (Xuehai) quickens Blood and resolves stasis; LI4 (Hegu) and LV3 (Taichong) open the Four Gates to move Qi and quicken Blood.

External therapy & care: A soft diet reduces load; for chronic cases long-term masticatory-muscle relaxation and joint protection; warm compress to aid Blood-quickening; avoid hard food and wide opening.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Ji Xue Teng, Jiang Huang to guide upward, Yan Hu Suo to quicken Blood and stop pain

Chronic local stasis; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, with Ji Xue Teng and Jiang Huang freeing the collaterals and guiding upward and Yan Hu Suo quickening Blood and stopping pain, matching TMJ local Blood stasis.

Textbook reference: Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang (head-face modification)

Qiang Huo, Dang Gui, Hong Hua, Chi Shao, Tao Ren, Mu Gua; Ge Gen guiding to the head-face, Ji Xue Teng freeing the collaterals

Quickens Blood and relaxes the sinew, suited to chronic stasis with structural change; Ge Gen guiding to the head-face, Ji Xue Teng freeing the collaterals.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan favors slowly dissolving the stasis knot, for chronic stasis; Shu Jin Huo Xue Tang favors quickening Blood and relaxing the sinew, for contracture with stasis. Stabbing pain with marked stasis — the former; contracture and sinew stagnation — the latter. Chronic treatment requires sustained effort.

Modifications:

  • Marked stasis with night pain — add Yan Hu Suo, Ru Xiang, Mo Yao
  • Degeneration with bone damage — add Gu Sui Bu, Xu Duan, Tu Bie Chong
  • Muscle contracture — heavy Bai Shao, add Mu Gua, Shen Jin Cao

Key point: Chronic TMJ disorder with structural change requires sustained effort; free the collaterals by quickening Blood + release the chronically contracted masticatory muscles + protect the joint; progressive limitation with wasting goes to warning signs.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/refer, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and complete exclusion or referral.

Safety: Joint red hot swollen painful with fever = septic arthritis, no needling; elderly severe temporal pain with visual symptoms = temporal arteritis, urgent ESR, prevent blindness.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Before warning signs are cleared, needling may delay recognition of infection, cardiac source, mass, or temporal arteritis.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: joint red hot swollen with fever — infection, blood count and inflammatory markers; jaw referred pain with chest oppression and sweating — exclude cardiac (angina referral), cardiac evaluation; progressive opening limitation with wasting and night pain — mass direction, imaging specialist; elderly severe temporal pain + visual symptoms + temporal artery induration — temporal arteritis, urgent ESR, steroids, prevent blindness.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With the diagnosis unsettled, a prescription is itself a risk.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes infection, cardiac referral, mass, and temporal arteritis before writing any TMJ treatment plan.

Key point: Joint red hot swollen with fever excludes infection; jaw referred pain excludes cardiac source; elderly severe temporal pain with visual symptoms excludes temporal arteritis (emergency, prevent blindness); once warning signs are cleared, return to the standard pathway.

← Back to Pain home

Gout

Gout maps the TCM framework precisely onto modern pathology: acute gout is ｢heat-Bi｣ — damp-heat crystallizing in the joint (monosodium urate crystals = the material precipitate of damp-heat, activating the NLRP3 inflammasome = the ｢fire｣ of heat-Bi); chronic tophaceous gout and rheumatoid are ｢recalcitrant-Bi｣ — phlegm-stasis obstructing the joint channels, tophi being the accumulation of phlegm; cold-triggered or degenerative acute flares approach ｢cold-Bi｣. By channel, gout favors the first metatarsophalangeal joint (traversed by Foot-Taiyin Spleen and Foot-Jueyin Liver) and mostly involves the lower-limb joints.

This gate's first duty is safety and coordination: acute gout with red hot swollen severe pain can benefit from TCM adjunct, but often needs colchicine or NSAIDs for rapid pain relief; with fever, septic arthritis must be excluded. A confirmed gout patient receiving TCM should continue allopurinol/febuxostat to lower urate — TCM herbs are most effective for chronic urate management and reducing flare frequency, coordinating with Internal Medicine in the acute phase.

The form of the pain directly sorts the pattern: joint red hot swollen + sudden + first metatarsophalangeal is heat-Bi (acute gout · damp-heat); polyarticular deformity + tophi + chronic is recalcitrant-Bi (phlegm-stasis · tophaceous gout, late rheumatoid); joint stiff cold pain, worse with cold and damp, eased by warmth, with lumbar soreness and fatigue is wind-cold-damp Bi with Liver-Kidney deficiency (chronic joint pain). Acute damp-heat should be cleared, chronic phlegm-stasis transformed, deficiency-cold warmly supplemented.

By the mainstream textbook framework, gout's root is ｢damp-heat｣: damp-heat accumulates in the joint channels → crystallizes → inflammation → red hot swollen pain; acutely clear heat and drain damp, chronically transform turbidity and excrete urate. Tu Fu Ling is gout's most important single herb (specifically excreting the joint turbidity corresponding to urate), Bi Xie drains lower-burner turbid damp, Yi Yi Ren drains damp and is anti-inflammatory. Yet some take Spleen-Kidney Yang deficiency as the root (see the other branch of the navigator) — the two views each have grounds and must be differentiated clinically.

Clinical pursuit of gout screens for acute coordination first, sorts the Bi pattern, then differentiates the root deficiency: step one asks whether it is acutely red hot swollen (acute gout · requires Internal Medicine coordination for pain relief and urate lowering), whether there are tophi and deformity, whether cold aggravates; step two differentiates the branch of heat-Bi / recalcitrant-Bi / wind-cold-damp; step three differentiates damp-heat root versus Spleen-Kidney Yang deficiency root (the latter non-mainstream, requiring solid deficiency-cold signs); and finally warning signs exclude septic arthritis and renal damage. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Joint pain suspecting gout — first sort the Bi pattern; acute red hot swollen requires Internal Medicine coordination:

Heat-Bi · Acute Gout (Damp-Heat Crystallization · Requires Internal Medicine Coordination)

Principle: Clear heat and drain damp, free the collaterals and stop pain, transform turbidity and excrete urate; in the acute phase coordinate with Internal Medicine (colchicine/NSAIDs for rapid relief, continue urate lowering)

Target muscle / point: Ashi points around the red swollen joint (avoiding direct needling of the extremely swollen red hot joint cavity), and distal points guiding heat downward

Technique: For an acute red swollen joint, prefer encircling needling and bloodletting (pricking the small collaterals around the joint to bleed and drain heat-toxin), avoiding direct needling of the extremely swollen joint cavity; take distal points to guide heat down. For acute severe pain, Internal Medicine drugs for rapid relief come first, with needling as an adjunct to drain heat.

Safety: Acute red hot swollen severe pain, do not strongly stimulate the swollen joint cavity; bloodletting requires strict disinfection; with fever exclude septic arthritis first, no needling.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the red swollen joint (avoiding the red swollen area), tip toward the affected site
