Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: use caution in the acutely very swollen phase, better in recovery to reduce tension; acutely rely on heat-clearing herbs and bloodletting.

Primary points: Ashi points (bloodletting around the red swollen joint), SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), ST44 (Neiting)

Adjunct points: First metatarsophalangeal local (around SP2 Dadu, SP3 Taibai), SP10 (Xuehai), GB34 (Yanglingquan), LV2 (Xingjian)

Point rationale: Ashi bloodletting directly drains the joint's damp-heat fire-toxin; SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao) drain damp and fortify the Spleen; ST44 (Neiting) clears Yangming Stomach heat; LV2 (Xingjian) clears Liver-channel heat (the first metatarsophalangeal belongs to Liver-Spleen); SP10 (Xuehai) quickens Blood. Together they clear heat, drain damp, and free the collaterals.

External therapy & care: In the acute phase elevate the limb, rest, drink plenty of water; avoid high-purine food (organ meats, seafood, alcohol); coordinate with Internal Medicine (colchicine/NSAIDs for rapid relief, allopurinol/febuxostat for urate lowering).

Classical formula: Baihu Jia Guizhi Tang (★Classical Original★) / Si Miao San with additions

For marked heat: Baihu Jia Guizhi Tang (Shi Gao, Zhi Mu, Gui Zhi, Gan Cao, Jing Mi); for damp-heat Bi: Si Miao San (Cang Zhu, Huang Bai, Yi Yi Ren, Niu Xi) add Tu Fu Ling 30g, Bi Xie 15g

Baihu Jia Guizhi Tang clears heat and frees Bi, suited to heat-exuberant red swollen joint; Si Miao San clears lower-burner damp-heat, add Tu Fu Ling (gout's most important single herb, specifically excreting joint damp-turbidity), Bi Xie (draining lower-burner turbid damp), Yi Yi Ren (draining damp, anti-inflammatory), matching damp-heat crystallization.

Textbook reference: Xuan Bi Tang / Danggui Niantong Tang with modification

Xuan Bi Tang (Fang Ji, Xing Ren, Hua Shi, Lian Qiao, Zhi Zi, Yi Yi Ren, Ban Xia, Can Sha, Chi Xiao Dou) add Tu Fu Ling, Bi Xie; or Danggui Niantong Tang

Xuan Bi Tang clears heat, drains damp, and frees Bi, a key formula for damp-heat Bi, add Tu Fu Ling, Bi Xie, Yi Yi Ren to strengthen urate excretion and damp draining; Danggui Niantong Tang drains damp, clears heat, and stops pain.

Formula analysis: Baihu Jia Guizhi Tang favors clearing Qi-level heat, for heat-exuberance; Si Miao San / Xuan Bi Tang favor clearing-draining lower-burner damp-heat, for damp-heat Bi (gout). Tu Fu Ling 30g is gout's specific herb (excreting joint damp-turbidity corresponding to urate), Bi Xie and Yi Yi Ren strengthen damp draining, the core of chronically reducing flares. Acutely rely on heat-clearing bloodletting + Internal Medicine coordination.

Modifications:

  • Blazing heat-toxin — add Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Pu Gong Ying, Ren Dong Teng
  • Severe pain — add Yan Hu Suo, Ru Xiang, Mo Yao
  • Chronically reducing flares — heavy Tu Fu Ling, add Bi Xie, Yi Yi Ren, Che Qian Zi (Xuan Bi Tang modification · long-term regulation)

Key point: Acute gout requires Internal Medicine coordination (colchicine/NSAIDs for rapid relief, continue urate lowering); with fever exclude septic arthritis first; Tu Fu Ling 30g is gout's specific herb and the core of chronic treatment; avoid high-purine food.

Recalcitrant-Bi · Tophaceous Gout (Phlegm-Stasis into Bone · Chronic Deformity)

Principle: Transform phlegm and disperse binding, quicken Blood and free the collaterals, with draining damp and excreting turbidity, and lower urate to control the source

Target muscle / point: Ashi points around tophi and deformed joints, and chronically stagnant channel segments

Technique: Needle the peri-articular Ashi points to free the collaterals and disperse binding, encircle the local region to quicken Blood; a tophus ulceration is not primarily needling-treated and requires anti-infection specialist handling. Chronic treatment requires sustained effort.

Safety: Tophus ulceration infection, no needling, refer to specialist; do not forcibly manipulate deformed joints; must run urate-lowering drugs in parallel.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the deformed joint, tip toward the stasis region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves the affected joint (pain-free range); releasing the chronically stagnant fascia.

Primary points: Ashi points (peri-articular), SP9 (Yinlingquan), ST40 (Fenglong, transform phlegm), SP10 (Xuehai, resolve stasis)

Adjunct points: SP6 (Sanyinjiao), GB34 (Yanglingquan), encircling around local tophi, KI3 (Taixi, protect the Kidney)

Point rationale: Ashi encircling needling frees the collaterals and disperses binding; ST40 (Fenglong) transforms phlegm, SP10 (Xuehai) resolves stasis, matching phlegm-stasis into bone; SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao) drain damp; KI3 (Taixi) protects the Kidney (preventing urate nephropathy).

External therapy & care: Strict low-purine diet, weight control, plenty of water; long-term urate-lowering drugs (allopurinol/febuxostat); regular urate and renal function checks; protect the joint.

Classical formula: Guizhi Fuling Wan (★Classical Original★) with phlegm-transforming binding-dispersing

Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao in equal parts; add Ban Xia, Bai Jie Zi, Dan Nan Xing to transform phlegm and disperse binding, Tu Fu Ling and Bi Xie to excrete turbidity

Phlegm and stasis bind into stones; Guizhi Fuling Wan slowly dissolves the stasis knot, add Bai Jie Zi and Dan Nan Xing to transform phlegm, soften hardness, and disperse binding, Tu Fu Ling and Bi Xie to excrete turbidity and drain damp, matching phlegm-stasis into bone.

Textbook reference: Shang Zhong Xia Tong Yong Tong Feng Fang / Taohong Yin with modification

Shang Zhong Xia Tong Yong Tong Feng Fang (Huang Bai, Cang Zhu, Tian Nan Xing, Gui Zhi, Fang Ji, Wei Ling Xian, Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Long Dan Cao, Qiang Huo, Bai Zhi, Chuan Xiong, Shen Qu) add Tu Fu Ling, Bi Xie

Shang Zhong Xia Tong Yong Tong Feng Fang is a famous gout formula, clearing heat and draining damp, transforming phlegm and quickening Blood, freeing the collaterals and stopping pain, add Tu Fu Ling, Bi Xie to strengthen turbidity excretion, suited to chronic phlegm-stasis tophaceous gout.

Formula analysis: Guizhi Fuling Wan with phlegm-transforming favors resolving stasis and softening hardness, for phlegm-stasis stone accumulation; Shang Zhong Xia Tong Yong Tong Feng Fang is a famous gout formula, clearing heat, transforming phlegm, quickening Blood in general treatment, suited to chronic recalcitrant-Bi. Chronic treatment requires sustained effort and urate lowering.

Modifications:

  • Large tophi — add Bai Jie Zi, Dan Nan Xing, Zhe Bei Mu, raw Mu Li to soften hardness
  • Marked stasis deformity — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Tu Bie Chong, Di Long
  • Renal damage — add Tu Fu Ling, Che Qian Zi, Jin Qian Cao, protect the Kidney (Shan Zhu Yu, Tu Si Zi)

Key point: Tophaceous recalcitrant-Bi requires sustained effort, transforming phlegm and dispersing binding, quickening Blood; must run urate lowering in parallel to control the source; tophus ulceration infection to specialist; protect the Kidney to prevent urate nephropathy.

Wind-Cold-Damp Bi with Liver-Kidney Deficiency (Chronic Joint Pain)

Principle: Dispel wind, scatter cold, and drain damp, warm the channel and free the collaterals, with supplementing Liver-Kidney and strengthening sinew and bone

Target muscle / point: Ashi points around the involved joint and cold-damp congealing channel segments, with Liver-Kidney back-shu points

Technique: Needle the joint Ashi points to warm and free, may add moxa to scatter cold and drain damp; add Liver-Kidney back-shu points to supplement deficiency. For severe cold-damp, prefer warm needling / moxibustion.

Safety: Moxibustion avoids burns; if it turns to an acute red hot swelling, stop moxa and switch to clearing heat; do not forcibly manipulate a degenerated joint.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the involved joint, tip toward the cold-damp congealing region

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves the affected joint; releasing the cold-damp congealing fascia. May add warm moxa.

Primary points: Joint Ashi points, GB34 (Yanglingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), KI3 (Taixi)

Adjunct points: Joint local (ST35 Dubi for knee, BL60 Kunlun for ankle etc. by site), moxa CV4 Guanyuan, BL23 Shenshu, BL18 Ganshu

Point rationale: Joint Ashi warms and frees the local; GB34 (Yanglingquan), the Sinew-meeting, benefits the joint; SP6 (Sanyinjiao), KI3 (Taixi) supplement Liver-Kidney; moxa CV4 (Guanyuan), BL23 (Shenshu) warms and supplements Kidney Yang, scattering cold-damp; BL18 (Ganshu) nourishes the Liver.

External therapy & care: Keep the joint warm, avoid cold and damp; moderate exercise to strengthen the sinew; Liver-Kidney warming-supplementing diet; avoid high-purine (to prevent turning to acute gout).

Classical formula: Guizhi Shaoyao Zhimu Tang (★Classical Original★) / Wutou Tang (★Classical Original★)

Cold-damp with heat: Guizhi Shaoyao Zhimu Tang (Gui Zhi, Shao Yao, Zhi Mu, Ma Huang, Fang Feng, Bai Zhu, Fu Zi, Sheng Jiang, Gan Cao); cold-Bi severe pain: Wutou Tang (requires strict control of processing and decoction)

Guizhi Shaoyao Zhimu Tang warms the channel, scatters cold, drains damp, and clears the stagnant heat, a key formula for cold-heat-complex Bi; Wutou Tang warms the channel, scatters cold, and stops pain, suited to cold-Bi severe pain (Wutou requires pre-decocting, long-decocting, strict control).

Textbook reference: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang (pill form, long-term)

Du Huo, Sang Ji Sheng, Du Zhong, Niu Xi, Xi Xin, Qin Jiao, Fu Ling, Rou Gui, Fang Feng, Chuan Xiong, Ren Shen, Gan Cao, Dang Gui, Shao Yao, Di Huang (pill form concentrated for long-term)

Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang dispels wind-damp, stops Bi pain, boosts Liver-Kidney, and supplements Qi-Blood, a key formula for Liver-Kidney-deficiency chronic Bi, taken long-term as a pill (as listed in the Low Back module, concentrated as a pill).

Formula analysis: Guizhi Shaoyao Zhimu Tang suits cold-heat-complex with joint swelling and pain; Wutou Tang suits pure cold-Bi severe pain (Wutou requires strict control); Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang suits Liver-Kidney-deficiency chronic Bi, taken long-term as a pill to secure the root. If it turns to acute red hot, switch to heat-Bi clearing-draining.

Modifications:

  • Severe cold pain — Guizhi Shaoyao Zhimu Tang with heavier Fu Zi (strict control) or Wutou Tang
  • Liver-Kidney deficiency with lumbar-knee soreness — Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang pill long-term
  • Heavy damp — add Yi Yi Ren, Cang Zhu, Bi Xie

Key point: Wind-cold-damp Bi with Liver-Kidney deficiency is common in chronic joint pain; warm the channel, scatter cold, drain damp + supplement Liver-Kidney; Wutou/Fuzi formulas require strict control of processing and decoction; if it turns to acute red hot swelling, switch to heat-Bi clearing-draining with Internal Medicine coordination.

Spleen-Kidney Yang Deficiency Root (Non-Mainstream View · Requires Differentiating Deficiency-Cold)

Principle: Warm and supplement Spleen-Kidney Yang Qi, restore its transformation to metabolize urate turbidity — ⚠non-mainstream experiential view, requires solid deficiency-cold signs, use Aconitum-class with caution

Target muscle / point: Joint local Ashi points (easing the acute), emphasizing warming-supplementing Spleen-Kidney back-shu and Ren-vessel key points

Technique: Local Ashi eases the acute, emphasizing heavy moxa on Spleen-Kidney back-shu (BL20 Pishu, BL23 Shenshu) and Guanyuan-Qihai to warm Yang; this view takes warming Yang and transformation as the root, not clearing heat. Acute red hot swelling still requires Internal Medicine coordination for pain relief.

Safety: ⚠This is a non-mainstream view, requiring confirmation of deficiency-cold signs before use; when acute red hot swollen branch appearance is marked, do not blindly pure-warm — still requires Internal Medicine coordination; Aconitum-class requires strict control.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the joint, tip toward the affected site

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion assists in easing the acute; this pattern emphasizes internal Yang-warming treatment, with FSN as adjunct.

Primary points: Moxa BL20 (Pishu), BL23 (Shenshu), CV4 (Guanyuan), CV6 (Qihai)

Adjunct points: ST36 (Zusanli), KI3 (Taixi), KI7 (Fuliu), joint local Ashi points

Point rationale: Moxa BL20 (Pishu), BL23 (Shenshu) warms and supplements Spleen-Kidney Yang Qi; CV4 (Guanyuan), CV6 (Qihai) cultivate the origin, secure the root, warm Yang; ST36 (Zusanli) fortifies the Spleen and boosts Qi; KI3 (Taixi), KI7 (Fuliu) supplement the Kidney. This view takes warming Yang and transformation to restore urate metabolism as the root.

External therapy & care: Warm and supplement Spleen-Kidney, avoid raw-cold; this view still requires purine control and, when needed, Internal Medicine coordination; must be differentiated and applied by an experienced practitioner.

Classical formula: Zhen Wu Tang (★Classical Original★) / Si Ni Tang (★Classical Original★) (⚠Aconitum-class · strict control)

Zhen Wu Tang (Fu Zi, Bai Zhu, Fu Ling, Bai Shao, Sheng Jiang); for severe Yang deficiency: Si Ni Tang (Fu Zi, Gan Jiang, Zhi Gan Cao). Fu Zi requires pre-decocting, long-decocting, strict control of processing and dosage

This view holds gout's root is Spleen-Kidney Yang deficiency, the Yang Qi insufficient to transform and break down urate causing accumulation; Zhen Wu Tang warms Yang and drains water, Si Ni Tang restores Yang and rescues counterflow, warming and restoring Spleen-Kidney Yang Qi so transformation proceeds and urate is transformed and the damp-heat appearance resolves of itself. (★Not textbook mainstream, requires confirming deficiency-cold signs, Aconitum strict control★)

Textbook reference: Fuzi Lizhong Wan / Jingui Shenqi Wan (mild long-term regulation)

Spleen Yang deficiency: Fuzi Lizhong Wan; Kidney Yang deficiency: Jingui Shenqi Wan (Bawei Shenqi Wan) — mild Yang-warming for long-term regulation

Milder than Zhen Wu and Si Ni, suited to long-term warming-supplementing Spleen-Kidney Yang Qi regulation; Fuzi Lizhong warms Spleen Yang, Jingui Shenqi warms Kidney Yang and transforms Qi.

Formula analysis: ⚠This is not textbook standard (the textbook mainstream is the damp-heat framework, see the heat-Bi branch). Zhen Wu Tang / Si Ni Tang drastically warm and restore Yang, suited to severe Yang deficiency short-term; Fuzi Lizhong Wan / Jingui Shenqi Wan are mild, suited to long-term regulation. This view originates from a herb-specialist colleague's experience with many recurrent gout cases (akin to Zhou Meisheng grasping the deeper mechanism with moxa in heat patterns), requiring solid deficiency-cold signs and cautious use by the experienced, not a default substitute for the damp-heat framework; Aconitum-class processing, dosage, and decoction (pre-decoct, long-decoct) require strict control.

Modifications:

  • Spleen Yang deficiency with loose stool — Fuzi Lizhong Wan, add Pao Jiang, Rou Dou Kou
  • Kidney Yang deficiency with cold lumbus — Jingui Shenqi Wan, add Lu Jiao Jiao, Tu Si Zi
  • Acute red hot swollen branch appearance — temporarily add heat-clearing (Tu Fu Ling, Bi Xie) with Internal Medicine coordination, do not pure-warm

Key point: ⚠Non-mainstream experiential view: gout's root is Spleen-Kidney Yang deficiency, warm Yang to transform urate; requires solid deficiency-cold signs before use, cautious trial by the experienced, not a default substitute for the damp-heat framework; Aconitum-class strict control of processing, dosage, decoction; acute red hot swelling still requires Internal Medicine coordination.

⚠ Warning Signs — Referral / Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, exclude/coordinate with Internal Medicine, follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, suspend needling and complete exclusion or referral, coordinating with Internal Medicine.

Safety: Joint red hot swollen painful + fever = exclude septic arthritis first (joint fluid examination), do not treat as gout alone, no needling; tophus ulceration infection, no needling, refer to specialist.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Mistaking septic arthritis for gout can cause joint destruction and sepsis; here needling is useless and delays.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: joint red hot swollen + fever — joint fluid examination to exclude septic arthritis (do not treat as gout alone); tophus ulceration with discharge — anti-infection + specialist; progressive renal function decline, elevated creatinine — urate nephropathy, nephrology; lumbar pain with hematuria — uric acid kidney stones, urology. A confirmed gout patient always coordinates with Internal Medicine on urate lowering.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

Septic arthritis and other emergencies must be excluded and handled first; a prescription delays treatment.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user excludes septic arthritis (especially with fever), tophus infection, and urate renal damage before treating as gout, always coordinating with Internal Medicine on urate lowering.

Key point: Joint red hot swollen + fever excludes septic arthritis first (do not treat as gout alone); tophus ulceration excludes infection; progressive renal damage excludes urate nephropathy; a confirmed gout patient always coordinates with Internal Medicine on urate lowering.

← Back to Pain home

Facial Palsy

Facial palsy (deviated mouth and eye) is a classic strong indication for acupuncture; its root is wind pathogen assaulting the facial channels, the channel Qi obstructed, and the sinews and flesh losing nourishment so they slacken and fail to contract. The face is the region traversed by the three hand and foot Yang channels (especially Foot-Yangming Stomach, Hand and Foot Shaoyang): the forehead creases and eye closure belong to the sinews of Foot-Taiyang and Foot-Yangming, the nasolabial fold and mouth corner to Foot-Yangming (ST4 Dicang, ST6 Jiache at their site), the retroauricular region to Hand-Shaoyang (TE17 Yifeng). When wind strikes the channels, the affected-side sinews and flesh slacken and the healthy side pulls, so deviation.

The first duty in differentiating facial palsy is distinguishing peripheral from central: peripheral facial palsy (mostly Bell's palsy) has loss of forehead creases, inability to wrinkle the forehead or close the eye, flattened nasolabial fold, mouth corner deviating to the healthy side, and is the main indication for acupuncture; central facial palsy (stroke, mass) has preserved forehead creases, ability to wrinkle the forehead and close the eye (the forehead being bilaterally innervated), with only lower-face deviation, and is a sign of stroke or mass requiring urgent workup, not resolved by acupuncture alone. For every facial palsy, first check the forehead creases — preserved creases go to warning signs to exclude a central cause.

The disease phase of peripheral facial palsy directly fixes the treatment urgency: the acute phase (within one week of onset) has wind pathogen freshly striking the channels with upright-pathogen struggle, calling for dispelling wind and freeing the collaterals, needling shallow and light (avoid over-disturbing acute inflammation); the recovery phase (one to several weeks later) has the wind pathogen gradually retreating and channel Qi awaiting restoration, calling for stronger collateral-freeing, facial through-needling, with moxa for warm freeing, supplementing Qi and quickening Blood; the sequelae phase (months without recovery) is mostly Qi deficiency with Blood stasis and malnourished sinews, calling for boosting Qi and quickening Blood, nourishing the sinews and freeing the collaterals.

By cause, wind pathogen assaulting the channels divides into cold and heat: wind-cold assaulting the collaterals mostly has a history of chill or wind exposure, initial retroauricular or facial tightness and pain, no marked heat sign (Qianzheng San primarily); wind-heat assaulting the collaterals mostly comes with fever, sore throat, retroauricular herpes (must guard against the herpes zoster of Hunt syndrome), calling for dispelling wind and clearing heat. Delayed recovery with facial muscle atrophy turns to Qi deficiency with Blood stasis (Buyang Huanwu Tang intent, boosting Qi and quickening Blood to free the collaterals).

Clinical pursuit of facial palsy checks the forehead creases to distinguish central from peripheral first, then sorts by phase and differentiates cold-heat-deficiency-stasis: step one checks whether one can wrinkle the forehead and close the eye (preserved creases = central, go to warning signs); step two fixes the peripheral phase (acute / recovery / sequelae); step three differentiates wind-cold (chill, tightness, no heat) / wind-heat (fever, sore throat, herpes) / Qi deficiency with Blood stasis (protracted, atrophy); and finally warning signs exclude central facial palsy (stroke, mass), Hunt syndrome, traumatic and otogenic causes. The navigator below is laid out along this road.

Deviated mouth and eye — first check the forehead creases to distinguish central from peripheral, then sort by phase and cause:

Wind-Cold Assaulting the Collaterals Facial Palsy (Peripheral · Mainly Acute Phase)

Principle: Dispel wind and scatter cold, warmly free the channel, quicken Blood and nourish the sinews; acute phase shallow and light, recovery phase through-needling with moxa

Target muscle / point: Affected-side facial sinew Ashi (forehead, peri-orbital, nasolabial fold, mouth corner, retroauricular Yifeng) — not musculoskeletal trigger points but freeing the facial sinews

Technique: Facial palsy is not a musculoskeletal trigger-point pattern but centers on facial sinew through-needling: in the acute phase (within 1 week) needle shallow and light, few points, avoid over-disturbing acute inflammation; in recovery add facial through-needling (Dicang through Jiache, Yangbai through Yuyao), may add moxa to TE17 Yifeng for warm freeing.

Safety: The face is richly vascular, do not needle deeply causing hematoma; avoid strong stimulation in the acute phase (to prevent aggravation); through-needle horizontally under the skin, avoiding deep-tissue injury.

Entry: Facial palsy centers on traditional facial through-needling, not a typical FSN indication; retroauricular tightness can take shallow subcutaneous sweeping behind the ear

Sweep & reperfusion: The face is treated mainly by through-needling; for retroauricular and neck tightness, shallow subcutaneous sweeping to release the local area, with reperfusion (slow facial movement).

Primary points: ST4 (Dicang) through ST6 (Jiache), GB14 (Yangbai) through Yuyao, ST2 (Sibai), TE17 (Yifeng), LI4 (Hegu, healthy side or bilateral)

Adjunct points: SI18 (Quanliao), LI20 (Yingxiang), GV26 (Shuigou), CV24 (Chengjiang), GB20 (Fengchi), EX-HN5 (Taiyang)

Point rationale: ST4 (Dicang) through ST6 (Jiache) frees the mouth-corner Yangming sinew, GB14 (Yangbai) through Yuyao restores the forehead and eye; ST2 (Sibai) benefits the peri-orbital region; TE17 (Yifeng) behind the ear dispels wind and frees the collaterals (where the facial nerve exits the stylomastoid foramen); LI4 (Hegu) is the key point for face and mouth (Hegu governs the face and mouth), distally dispelling wind and freeing the collaterals; GB20 (Fengchi) dispels wind.

External therapy & care: Keep the face warm and avoid wind-cold (especially the affected side); for incomplete eye closure protect the eye (artificial tears, eye patch to prevent keratitis); facial muscle functional training (wrinkle forehead, close eye, puff cheeks, show teeth); avoid overwork and chill.

Classical formula: Gui Zhi Tang (★Classical Original★) with wind-dispelling collateral-freeing / Xiao Xu Ming Tang intent

Gui Zhi Tang (Gui Zhi, Shao Yao, Gan Cao, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao) add Fang Feng, Bai Fu Zi, Jiang Can, Quan Xie to dispel wind and free the collaterals; or Xiao Xu Ming Tang intent

Gui Zhi Tang harmonizes Ying-Wei and resolves the muscle to dispel wind, add Bai Fu Zi, Jiang Can, Quan Xie (the core of Qianzheng San) to dispel wind-phlegm, free the channels, and correct the deviation, matching wind-cold assaulting the collaterals.

Textbook reference: Qianzheng San with modification

Bai Fu Zi, Jiang Can, Quan Xie (equal parts as powder, or add Fang Feng, Bai Zhi, Wu Gong); for severe wind-cold add Gui Zhi, Fang Feng

Qianzheng San is the famous formula for facial palsy deviation, Bai Fu Zi dispelling wind and transforming phlegm, Jiang Can and Quan Xie searching wind, freeing the collaterals, and stopping spasm, specifically treating wind-phlegm obstructing the collaterals in deviated mouth and eye.

Formula analysis: Qianzheng San is the facial-palsy specific formula (dispelling wind-phlegm, freeing the collaterals, correcting deviation); Gui Zhi Tang plus Qianzheng San herbs harmonizes Ying-Wei with wind-dispelling, suited to marked wind-cold exterior pattern. Acutely dispel wind and free the collaterals primarily, with strong insect-medicinal wind-searching.

Modifications:

  • Severe wind-cold with retroauricular pain — add Gui Zhi, Fang Feng, Qiang Huo
  • Exuberant wind-phlegm — add Dan Nan Xing, Tian Ma, Wu Gong
  • Recovery phase collateral-freeing — add Huang Qi, Ji Xue Teng, Di Long

Key point: Facial palsy first checks the forehead creases to distinguish central; peripheral wind-cold assaulting the collaterals uses Qianzheng San primarily; acute phase needle shallow and light, recovery phase facial through-needling with moxa; incomplete eye closure requires eye protection against keratitis.

Wind-Heat Assaulting the Collaterals Facial Palsy (Fever, Sore Throat · Guard Against Hunt Herpes)

Principle: Dispel wind and clear heat, resolve toxin and free the collaterals; Hunt syndrome requires antiviral coordination

Target muscle / point: Affected-side facial sinew Ashi, retroauricular herpes region needled with caution; acute heat pattern needle shallow and light

Technique: Facial sinew shallow needling and through-needling primarily, clearing heat and freeing the collaterals; do not directly needle the retroauricular herpes region (Hunt), needle with caution; acute heat pattern prefers shallow and light, with distal heat-clearing points.

Safety: Do not directly needle the herpes region (to prevent spreading infection); avoid strong stimulation in the acute phase; Hunt requires antiviral coordination and facial care.

Entry: The face is treated mainly by through-needling, not a typical FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Facial through-needling primarily; the retroauricular and neck can take shallow subcutaneous sweeping (avoiding the herpes region), with reperfusion.

Primary points: ST4 (Dicang) through ST6 (Jiache), GB14 (Yangbai) through Yuyao, TE17 (Yifeng), LI4 (Hegu), LI11 (Quchi, clear heat)

Adjunct points: ST2 (Sibai), SI18 (Quanliao), GB20 (Fengchi), GV14 (Dazhui, clear heat), TE5 (Waiguan)

Point rationale: Facial through-needling frees the collaterals and corrects deviation as in wind-cold; add LI11 (Quchi), GV14 (Dazhui), TE5 (Waiguan) to dispel wind and clear heat; TE17 (Yifeng) dispels wind and frees the collaterals (Hunt's location near the geniculate ganglion); LI4 (Hegu) the key face-mouth point clearing Yangming heat too.

External therapy & care: Wind-dispelling heat-clearing diet, avoid pungent-spicy; Hunt syndrome requires antiviral (acyclovir) + steroid coordination; incomplete eye closure requires eye protection; ear herpes care to prevent infection.

Classical formula: Ge Gen Tang (★Classical Original★) with heat-clearing / Yin Qiao San intent

Wind-heat exterior pattern: Yin Qiao San add Jiang Can, Chan Tui; heat-toxin herpes (Hunt): add Ban Lan Gen, Da Qing Ye, Long Dan Cao to clear heat and resolve toxin

Wind-heat assaulting the collaterals calls for dispelling wind and clearing heat, Yin Qiao San add Jiang Can, Chan Tui to dispel wind and free the collaterals; Hunt heat-toxin with epidemic add Ban Lan Gen, Da Qing Ye, Long Dan Cao to clear heat and resolve toxin, matching wind-heat congesting the collaterals.

Textbook reference: Qianzheng San with Yin Qiao San / Longdan Xiegan Tang (Hunt)

Qianzheng San (dispel wind, free collaterals, correct deviation) with Yin Qiao San (dispel wind, clear heat); for Hunt herpes with exuberant heat-toxin combine Longdan Xiegan Tang to clear Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat

Qianzheng San frees the collaterals and corrects deviation primarily, with Yin Qiao San to dispel wind and clear heat; for Hunt ear herpes (Liver-Gallbladder channel) with exuberant heat-toxin combine Longdan Xiegan Tang to clear and drain.

Formula analysis: For mild wind-heat, Qianzheng San with Yin Qiao San dispels wind, clears heat, and frees the collaterals; for exuberant Hunt heat-toxin herpes (Liver-Gallbladder channel) combine Longdan Xiegan Tang to clear Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat and resolve toxin. Hunt has a slower prognosis than Bell's and requires antiviral coordination.

Modifications:

  • Fever and sore throat — add Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Ban Lan Gen
  • Ear herpes (Hunt) — add Da Qing Ye, Long Dan Cao, Ban Lan Gen, Yan Hu Suo to stop pain
  • Recovery phase — transfer to the Qi-deficiency-with-Blood-stasis branch to boost Qi and quicken Blood

Key point: Wind-heat assaulting the collaterals dispels wind and clears heat + Qianzheng San frees the collaterals; ear herpes = Hunt syndrome, requires antiviral (acyclovir) + steroid coordination with slower prognosis; do not directly needle the herpes region, protect the eye and ear.

Qi Deficiency with Blood Stasis Facial Palsy (Delayed Recovery Phase · Sequelae Phase)

Principle: Boost Qi and quicken Blood, nourish the sinews and free the collaterals; sustained effort, with moxa and facial through-needling

Target muscle / point: Affected-side facial sinew Ashi (slack atrophied facial muscles), facial through-needling as the core, with moxa for warm freeing

Technique: Facial through-needling (Dicang through Jiache, Yangbai through Yuyao, Jiache through Dicang multi-directional through-needling) with moxa for warm freeing, boosting Qi and quickening Blood; the sequelae phase requires sustained effort and progression, with gentle technique for synkinesis and abnormal movement (to avoid aggravating abnormal synkinesis).

Safety: Do not needle the face deeply causing hematoma; use caution with strong stimulation in marked synkinesis and abnormal movement (to prevent aggravating abnormal movement); persistent non-improvement requires re-examination to exclude a mass.

Entry: The face is treated mainly by through-needling with moxa, not a typical FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Facial through-needling primarily, with reperfusion (active facial muscle training); retroauricular and neck tightness can take shallow sweeping.

Primary points: ST4 (Dicang) through ST6 (Jiache), GB14 (Yangbai) through Yuyao, ST2 (Sibai), ST36 (Zusanli, boost Qi), LI4 (Hegu)

Adjunct points: SI18 (Quanliao), LI20 (Yingxiang), TE17 (Yifeng), SP10 (Xuehai, quicken Blood), BL20 (Pishu, boost Qi), moxa the affected-side face

Point rationale: Facial through-needling frees the collaterals and nourishes the sinews as before; ST36 (Zusanli), BL20 (Pishu) boost Qi and fortify the Spleen (when Qi moves Blood moves); SP10 (Xuehai) quickens Blood and resolves stasis; adding moxa to the affected-side face warmly frees, boosting Qi and quickening Blood to aid recovery-phase sinew-flesh restoration.

External therapy & care: Persist with facial muscle functional training (mirror practice of wrinkling forehead, closing eye, puffing cheeks, showing teeth, pursing lips); facial warm compress to aid Blood-quickening; avoid fatigue; for synkinesis do separation training; re-examine if long non-improvement.

Classical formula: Huangqi Guizhi Wuwu Tang (★Classical Original★) with Qianzheng San

Huangqi Guizhi Wuwu Tang (Huang Qi, Gui Zhi, Shao Yao, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao) with Qianzheng San (Bai Fu Zi, Jiang Can, Quan Xie), add Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Di Long

Huangqi Guizhi Wuwu Tang boosts Qi and warmly frees Blood-Bi, suited to recovery-phase Qi deficiency with Blood stasis, with Qianzheng San to dispel wind-phlegm, free the collaterals, and correct deviation, add Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Di Long to nourish Blood, quicken Blood, and free the collaterals, matching Qi deficiency with Blood stasis malnutrition.

Textbook reference: Buyang Huanwu Tang with Qianzheng San

Buyang Huanwu Tang (Huang Qi, Dang Gui, Chi Shao, Chuan Xiong, Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Di Long) with Bai Fu Zi, Jiang Can, Quan Xie

Buyang Huanwu Tang heavily uses Huang Qi to boost Qi and quicken Blood and free the collaterals, a key formula for Qi deficiency with Blood stasis, with Qianzheng San insect-medicinals to search wind and free the collaterals, suited to the sequelae phase with facial muscle atrophy and delay.

Formula analysis: Huangqi Guizhi Wuwu Tang favors boosting Qi and warmly freeing Blood-Bi, suited to the recovery phase; Buyang Huanwu Tang heavily uses Huang Qi to greatly supplement original Qi with Blood-quickening, suited to the sequelae phase with atrophy and delay. Both combine Qianzheng San to dispel wind and free the collaterals. The sequelae phase requires sustained effort, boosting Qi primarily.

Modifications:

  • Severe Qi deficiency — heavy Huang Qi (Buyang Huanwu Tang's Huang Qi may reach 60-120g)
  • Blood stasis atrophy — add Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Ji Xue Teng, Quan Xie, Wu Gong
  • Synkinesis, abnormal movement — add Bai Shao, Mu Gua to soften the sinew, gentle technique

Key point: The recovery and sequelae phases of Qi deficiency with Blood stasis require sustained effort; Buyang Huanwu Tang / Huangqi Guizhi Wuwu Tang boost Qi and quicken Blood with Qianzheng San to free the collaterals; facial through-needling with moxa + facial muscle training; long non-improvement requires re-examination to exclude a mass.

⚠ Warning Signs — Central Exclusion / Referral Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; check the forehead creases to distinguish central — urgent workup/referral — follow up

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Checking the forehead creases is the first step: preserved creases (central) require immediate imaging to exclude stroke/mass, not the main indication for acupuncture.

Safety: Preserved forehead creases + able to wrinkle and close the eye = central, urgent head imaging; with limb weakness and slurred speech = stroke, immediate emergency care.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Mistaking central facial palsy (stroke, mass) for peripheral and treating with acupuncture, delaying stroke care, can cause serious consequences.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: preserved forehead creases + able to wrinkle and close the eye + only lower-face deviation — central, urgent head CT/MRI to exclude stroke/mass; with limb weakness, slurred speech, altered consciousness — stroke, immediate emergency care (thrombolysis/thrombectomy within the window); severe ear pain + herpes + hearing loss — Hunt syndrome, antiviral + steroid; trauma or otitis media history — traumatic/otogenic, specialist evaluation.

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

Central facial palsy (stroke, mass) requires emergency handling; herbs delay treatment.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page exists so that the user checks the forehead creases for every facial palsy: preserved creases require urgent workup to exclude stroke/mass, never mistaking it for peripheral facial palsy and delaying with acupuncture.

Key point: The first step in facial palsy is checking the forehead creases: preserved creases = central, urgent imaging to exclude stroke/mass (do not mistake for peripheral acupuncture); with limb weakness and slurred speech, immediate emergency care; Hunt requires antiviral; peripheral returns to the standard pathway after excluding central.

← Back to Pain home

Visceral Referral Station

This station differs from every other chapter in the module: the source of musculoskeletal pain lies in the sinews and trigger points, but the source of visceral pain lies in the organs — it is the external manifestation, along the organ's channel, of stagnated organ Qi dynamics. Treating only the painful site without addressing the organ is treating the branch, not the root. So this station's task is ｢recognition｣: to recognize this as visceral-source pain, attribute it to its organ-channel, screen for emergencies first, then route to the corresponding Internal Medicine chapter for deeper treatment.

By channel, each visceral pain has its organ-channel attribution, and the pain location is the clue: epigastric pain (upper abdomen below the heart) belongs to Foot-Yangming Stomach and the related Spleen, the Stomach Qi favoring descent; hypochondriac pain (below the costal margins, flanks) is in the domain of Foot-Jueyin Liver and Foot-Shaoyang Gallbladder, the Liver Qi favoring free coursing; chest pain belongs to Hand-Shaoyin Heart and Hand-Taiyin Lung, the Heart-Yang favoring flow. Where the pain lies, suspect that organ first.

By Qi dynamics, the common thread of visceral pain is stagnation of organ Qi: Liver Qi stagnates and rebels transversely on the Stomach, giving epigastric-hypochondriac distending pain, emotionally triggered, briefly eased by belching or sighing; cold congeals the Stomach, giving sudden gripping pain eased by warmth; Stomach Yin is depleted, giving dull burning pain with hunger without desire to eat; Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat gives right hypochondriac burning pain possibly with jaundice; phlegm-stasis obstructs the Heart, giving chest oppression and stabbing pain. The disorder of ascent-descent-exit-entry follows each organ.

This station's first duty is safety: in the chest-pain gate, exclude cardiac emergency first — acute chest pain radiating to the left arm or jaw, with sweating, nausea, and shortness of breath, is a cardiac emergency; call emergency services immediately, and acupuncture is never the sole intervention for acute chest pain. TCM chest-Bi treatment addresses only chronic stable angina and non-cardiac chest pain after medical workup has excluded cardiac causes. The abdomen likewise: severe gripping pain, board-like abdomen, bloody stool, violent vomiting require excluding a surgical acute abdomen first.

Recognizing visceral referred pain screens for emergencies first, then fixes the organ-channel, then routes to deeper treatment: step one asks whether the pain is epigastric, hypochondriac, or chest; step two differentiates the Qi dynamics of cold-heat-deficiency-excess; step three screens each system's warning signs (cardiac, acute abdomen, GI bleeding, malignancy signs); step four routes non-emergency cases to the corresponding Internal Medicine chapter (epigastric pain / hypochondriac pain / chest-Bi / abdominal pain / dysmenorrhea). Acupuncture can relieve the branch symptom immediately; root treatment is in Internal Medicine.

Pain in the abdomen / flank / chest — first sort the organ-channel; exclude any emergency first:

Liver Qi Invading the Stomach (Functional Epigastric Pain · to Internal Medicine)

Principle: Course the Liver and move Qi, harmonize the Stomach and descend counterflow; acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter

Target muscle / point: Visceral pain is not a myofascial target — this station uses organ-channel points, no trigger-point needling. Gentle pressure / mild needling of the abdominal Mu-point region (CV12 Zhongwan) to harmonize Stomach Qi

Technique: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel differentiation needling: main point PC6 (Neiguan) to level and descend Stomach Qi counterflow, with CV12 (Zhongwan) to harmonize the Stomach and LV3 (Taichong) to course the Liver. Technique should be gentle Qi-moving, not the strong-stimulation release of the musculoskeletal chapters.

Safety: Abdominal needling requires attention to depth, avoiding the viscera, especially in thin patients; use caution with Hegu, Sanyinjiao etc. in pregnancy; for severe pain refusing pressure with a board-like abdomen, exclude acute abdomen first.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs, not FSN (fascial sweeping), which is outside its scope.

Primary points: PC6 (Neiguan), CV12 (Zhongwan), ST36 (Zusanli), LV3 (Taichong)

Adjunct points: ST34 (Liangqiu, Xi-cleft · acute stomach pain), SP4 (Gongsun, Chong vessel), LV14 (Qimen, course the Liver)

Point rationale: PC6 (Neiguan) is the main point for all epigastric conditions, leveling and descending Stomach Qi counterflow; CV12 (Zhongwan), Stomach Mu, harmonizes the Stomach; ST36 (Zusanli), Stomach He-sea, fortifies the Stomach; LV3 (Taichong) courses the Liver to treat the root; for acute stomach pain add ST34 (Liangqiu), the Xi-cleft, for speed.

External therapy & care: Emotional regulation is key (this pattern's root is stress); regular meals, avoid raw-cold and spicy; after acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Classical formula: Sini San (★Classical Original★) with Liver-coursing Stomach-harmonizing

Chai Hu, Shao Yao, Zhi Shi, Gan Cao; for marked distension add Xiang Fu, Chen Pi; for acid reflux add Wa Leng Zi, Wu Zei Gu

Sini San penetratingly resolves Liver stagnation and regulates Qi dynamics, matching the mechanism of Liver Qi rebelling transversely on the Stomach; Chai Hu and Shao Yao course and soften the Liver, Zhi Shi descends Qi, combined with Xiang Fu and Chen Pi to move Qi and harmonize the Stomach. (The textbook Chaihu Shugan San is Sini San plus Xiang Fu, Chuan Xiong, Chen Pi — the same lineage.)

Textbook reference: Chaihu Shugan San

Chai Hu, Bai Shao, Zhi Ke, Chen Pi, Chuan Xiong, Xiang Fu, Gan Cao

The set formula for coursing the Liver, moving Qi, and harmonizing the Stomach, commonly used for Liver-Qi-invading-Stomach and functional stomach pain; full differentiation (including heat transformation, Blood stasis) is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Formula analysis: Sini San is the ancestral Liver-coursing Qi-regulating formula, concise and forceful; Chaihu Shugan San adds Xiang Fu, Chuan Xiong, Chen Pi to it, with stronger Qi-moving power. Qi stagnation predominant — the former; Qi stagnation with marked distending pain — the latter. Proton-pump inhibitors treat the branch (acid suppression), coursing the Liver and harmonizing the Stomach treats the root.

Modifications:

  • Acid reflux, heartburn — add Wa Leng Zi, Wu Zei Gu, Huang Lian (stagnation transforming to heat)
  • Marked distension, belching — add Xiang Fu, Mu Xiang, Sha Ren
  • With Blood stasis stabbing pain — add Dan Shen, Yan Hu Suo

Key point: H. pylori-negative, stress-related functional stomach pain is almost always Liver Qi invading the Stomach; acupuncture relieves it immediately, the root is coursing the Liver, harmonizing the Stomach, and emotional regulation; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Cold Congealing the Stomach (Acute Cold Stomach Pain · to Internal Medicine)

Principle: Warm the Stomach and scatter cold, move Qi and stop pain; warm needling is fast, root treatment in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter

Target muscle / point: This station uses organ-channel points. The warm-needling zone of CV12 (Zhongwan) and ST36 (Zusanli), warmed immediately for acute cold pain

Technique: Chiefly warm needling / moxibustion: warm needling of CV12 (Zhongwan) and ST36 (Zusanli) directly warms the Stomach, with PC6 (Neiguan) and SP4 (Gongsun) to descend Stomach Qi. This is the fastest immediate method for acute cold stomach pain.

Safety: Moxibustion avoids burns; abdominal needling requires attention to depth; for acute severe pain with board-like abdomen refusing pressure, exclude acute abdomen first — go to warning signs.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs.

Primary points: CV12 (Zhongwan, warm needling), ST36 (Zusanli, warm needling), PC6 (Neiguan), SP4 (Gongsun)

Adjunct points: ST34 (Liangqiu, acute stomach pain Xi-cleft), CV6 (Qihai, moxa), GV12 (Shenzhu)

Point rationale: Warm needling of CV12 (Zhongwan) and ST36 (Zusanli) directly warms the Stomach and scatters cold, the most effective immediate method for acute cold stomach pain; PC6 (Neiguan) with SP4 (Gongsun) is an Eight-Confluence pairing that forcefully levels and descends Stomach Qi; ST34 (Liangqiu), the Xi-cleft, quickly stops acute pain.

External therapy & care: Keep the abdomen warm, avoid raw-cold; warm compress and warm drinks help; after acupuncture and moxibustion ease the acute, root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Classical formula: Liang Fu Wan (warm the Stomach, scatter cold, move Qi)

Gao Liang Jiang, Xiang Fu in equal parts; for severe cold add Gan Jiang, Wu Zhu Yu; for severe pain add Yan Hu Suo

Gao Liang Jiang warms the Stomach and scatters cold, Xiang Fu moves Qi and stops pain — the most concise, on-target formula for cold stomach pain, matching the mechanism of cold congelation and Qi stagnation in the Stomach.

Textbook reference: Liang Fu Wan with Xiang Sha Yang Wei (deficiency-cold)

Liang Fu Wan as base; for recurrent Spleen-Stomach deficiency-cold, combine the intent of Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi or Huang Qi Jian Zhong

For the acute, Liang Fu Wan warms and scatters; for recurrent deficiency-cold, also warm the center and fortify the Spleen; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Formula analysis: Liang Fu Wan specifically treats acute pain of cold congelation with Qi stagnation, concise and focused; for recurrent Spleen-Stomach deficiency-cold, combine Jianzhong / Xiang Sha Yang Wei to warm the center and secure the root. Acute cold pain — the former; deficiency-cold chronic illness — also the latter.

Modifications:

  • Severe cold with clear-fluid vomiting — add Gan Jiang, Wu Zhu Yu, Ban Xia
  • Severe pain — add Yan Hu Suo, Mu Xiang
  • Recurrent Spleen-Stomach deficiency-cold — combine the intent of Huang Qi Jian Zhong Tang

Key point: Warm needling of CV12 and ST36 is the fastest immediate method for acute cold stomach pain; for severe gripping pain with board-like abdomen refusing pressure, exclude acute abdomen first; for recurrent deficiency-cold, root treatment is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Stomach Yin Deficiency (Chronic Dull Pain · to Internal Medicine)

Principle: Nourish Yin and boost the Stomach, harmonize the center and stop pain; acupuncture as adjunct, root treatment in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter

Target muscle / point: This station uses organ-channel points. Gentle needling of the abdominal Mu-point region, avoiding strong stimulation that consumes Yin

Technique: Chiefly organ-channel differentiation needling with light, gentle technique: CV12 (Zhongwan), ST36 (Zusanli), SP6 (Sanyinjiao) to nourish Yin and harmonize the Stomach, avoiding warm-drying strong stimulation.

Safety: Yin deficiency avoids excessive warm moxibustion; abdominal needling requires attention to depth; for progressive wasting, melena, or worsening night waking pain, exclude malignancy first — go to warning signs.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs.

Primary points: CV12 (Zhongwan), ST36 (Zusanli), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), PC6 (Neiguan)

Adjunct points: KI3 (Taixi, enrich Yin), SP3 (Taibai), ST44 (Neiting, clear Stomach heat)

Point rationale: CV12 (Zhongwan) and ST36 (Zusanli) harmonize the Stomach and nourish Yin; SP6 (Sanyinjiao) enriches the Yin of the three Yin; PC6 (Neiguan) harmonizes the Stomach and descends counterflow; for Stomach fire add ST44 (Neiting) to clear heat, for Kidney Yin also deficient add KI3 (Taixi).

External therapy & care: Avoid spicy, drying, fried food; prefer moistening, easily digested food; chew slowly; acupuncture as adjunct, root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Classical formula: Mai Men Dong Tang (★Classical Original★) intent with Stomach-boosting

Mai Dong, Ren Shen, Ban Xia, Gan Cao, Jing Mi, Da Zao; combined with Sha Shen, Yu Zhu, Shi Hu to nourish Stomach Yin

Mai Men Dong Tang moistens and nourishes the Lung-Stomach Yin and descends counterflow, matching the mechanism of Stomach Yin insufficiency losing moistening and descent; combined with the Sha Shen, Yu Zhu, Shi Hu of Yi Wei Tang to strengthen Stomach-Yin nourishment.

Textbook reference: Yi Wei Tang / Yi Guan Jian

Yi Wei Tang (Sha Shen, Mai Dong, Sheng Di, Yu Zhu, rock sugar); for concurrent Liver-Yin deficiency hypochondriac pain combine Yi Guan Jian

Yi Wei Tang specifically nourishes Stomach Yin; for concurrent Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency, Yi Guan Jian; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Formula analysis: Mai Men Dong Tang favors moistening the Lung-Stomach and descending counterflow, for concurrent Qi counterflow and choking cough; Yi Wei Tang purely nourishes Stomach Yin, for Stomach Yin deficiency predominant; Yi Guan Jian also enriches Liver-Kidney, for concurrent hypochondriac pain and dry mouth. Choose by concurrent signs.

Modifications:

  • Severe dry mouth — add Shi Hu, Tian Hua Fen
  • With Stomach heat — add Huang Lian, Pu Gong Ying
  • Dry stool — add Huo Ma Ren, Xuan Shen

Key point: Nourishing Stomach Yin takes time and avoids warm-drying; for progressive wasting, melena, or worsening night waking pain, exclude malignancy first; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine epigastric-pain chapter.

Liver Stagnation / Liver-Gallbladder Damp-Heat Hypochondriac Pain (to Internal Medicine)

Principle: For Liver stagnation course the Liver and move Qi, for damp-heat clear and drain Liver-Gallbladder; acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment in the Internal Medicine hypochondriac-pain chapter

Target muscle / point: This station uses organ-channel points. Local Qimen, Riyue region and Liver-Gallbladder channel points, no trigger-point needling

Technique: Chiefly Liver-Gallbladder channel differentiation needling: LV3 (Taichong), GB34 (Yanglingquan) to course and drain the Liver-Gallbladder, LV14 (Qimen), GB24 (Riyue) locally to move Qi. For damp-heat add Yinlingquan to drain damp.

Safety: Hypochondriac needling (especially the right subcostal region) requires attention to depth, avoiding the liver and gallbladder; high fever with jaundice and severe pain (acute cholangitis) needs emergency care first.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs.

Primary points: LV3 (Taichong), GB34 (Yanglingquan), LV14 (Qimen), TE6 (Zhigou)

Adjunct points: GB24 (Riyue, Gallbladder Mu), Dan Nang point below Yanglingquan, for damp-heat add SP9 (Yinlingquan), GB40 (Qiuxu)

Point rationale: LV3 (Taichong) courses the Liver and GB34 (Yanglingquan) drains the Gallbladder — the main point-pair for hypochondriac pain; LV14 (Qimen), Liver Mu, and TE6 (Zhigou) are effective points for hypochondriac pain; for damp-heat add SP9 (Yinlingquan) to drain damp and GB40 (Qiuxu) to drain the Gallbladder.

External therapy & care: Emotional regulation, avoid greasy food (damp-heat); after acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine hypochondriac-pain chapter.

Classical formula: Sini San (★Classical Original★) / Da Chaihu Tang (★Classical Original★)

Liver stagnation: Sini San add Xiang Fu, Yu Jin; Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat: Da Chaihu Tang (Chai Hu, Huang Qin, Shao Yao, Zhi Shi, Da Huang, Ban Xia, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao)

For Liver-Qi stagnation, Sini San penetratingly courses the Liver; for Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat with bowel excess, Da Chaihu Tang harmonizes Shaoyang and frees the bowel to drain heat, matching the mechanism of damp-heat accumulating in the Liver-Gallbladder.

Textbook reference: Chaihu Shugan San / Longdan Xiegan Tang

Liver stagnation: Chaihu Shugan San; Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat: Longdan Xiegan Tang

For Liver stagnation course the Liver and move Qi, for damp-heat clear and drain the Liver-Gallbladder; full differentiation (including Blood stasis, Yin-deficiency hypochondriac pain) is in the Internal Medicine hypochondriac-pain chapter.

Formula analysis: Liver-Qi stagnation (fullness, sighing, wiry pulse), coursing the Liver predominant — Sini San / Chaihu Shugan San; Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat (burning pain, jaundice, yellow greasy coat), clearing-draining predominant — Da Chaihu / Longdan Xiegan. Choose by cold-heat-deficiency-excess.

Modifications:

  • Damp-heat jaundice — add Yin Chen, Zhi Zi, Jin Qian Cao
  • Qi-stagnation Blood-stasis stabbing pain — add Chuan Lian Zi, Yan Hu Suo, Dan Shen
  • Stones — add Jin Qian Cao, Hai Jin Sha, Ji Nei Jin

Key point: Hypochondriac pain first suspects the Liver-Gallbladder; course stagnation, clear damp-heat; acute cholangitis (high fever, jaundice, severe pain) needs emergency care first; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine hypochondriac-pain chapter.

Phlegm-Stasis Obstructing the Heart Chest-Bi (Exclude Cardiac Emergency First · to Internal Medicine)

Principle: Exclude cardiac emergency first! Free the Yang and sweep phlegm, quicken Blood to resolve the Bi; acupuncture as adjunct, root treatment in the Internal Medicine chest-Bi chapter

Target muscle / point: This station uses organ-channel points, and only after medical exclusion of emergency. Pericardium and Heart channel points, no trigger-point needling

Technique: Must first confirm acute coronary syndrome has been medically excluded. Chronic stable type: PC6 (Neiguan), Xinshu, Danzhong to loosen the chest and free the Yang, with Fenglong to transform phlegm and Xuehai to resolve stasis. Gentle technique.

Safety: Acute chest pain radiating to left arm/jaw + cold sweat and dyspnea = cardiac emergency, call emergency services immediately, never use acupuncture alone! Acupuncture is only for the chronic stable type after emergency exclusion.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs.

Primary points: PC6 (Neiguan), CV17 (Danzhong), BL15 (Xinshu), ST40 (Fenglong, transform phlegm)

Adjunct points: SP10 (Xuehai, resolve stasis), BL14 (Jueyinshu), HT5 (Tongli), CV6 (Qihai)

Point rationale: PC6 (Neiguan) is the main point for Heart-chest conditions, loosening the chest and regulating Qi; CV17 (Danzhong), Qi-meeting, loosens the chest and frees the Yang; BL15 (Xinshu) regulates the Heart; for phlegm-stasis add ST40 (Fenglong) to transform phlegm and SP10 (Xuehai) to resolve stasis.

External therapy & care: Control risk factors (lipids, weight), follow cardiology treatment; acupuncture is adjunct, not a substitute for biomedicine; root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine chest-Bi chapter.

Classical formula: Gualou Xiebai Banxia Tang (★Classical Original★) with Blood-quickening

Gua Lou, Xie Bai, Ban Xia, white wine (decocted in rice wine); combined with Dan Shen, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao to quicken Blood

Gualou Xiebai Banxia Tang frees the Yang and disperses binding, sweeps phlegm and loosens the chest, matching the mechanism of phlegm-turbidity obstructing the chest-Yang; combined with Dan Shen, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao to quicken Blood and resolve stasis, treating phlegm and stasis together.

Textbook reference: Gualou Xiebai Banxia Tang with Taohong Siwu / Xuefu Zhuyu Tang

Phlegm-turbidity: Gualou Xiebai Banxia Tang; marked Blood stasis: combine Taohong Siwu or Xuefu Zhuyu Tang

For phlegm-turbidity predominant free the Yang and sweep phlegm, for Blood stasis predominant combine Blood-quickening stasis-resolving; full differentiation (including Qi deficiency, Yang deficiency, cold) is in the Internal Medicine chest-Bi chapter.

Formula analysis: Gualou Xiebai Banxia Tang is the ancestral formula for phlegm-turbidity chest-Bi; for marked Blood stasis combine Xuefu Zhuyu Tang to quicken Blood and free the vessel. Phlegm-turbidity chest oppression — the former; stabbing pain with marked stasis — combine the latter. All used for the chronic stable type after medical exclusion of emergency.

Modifications:

  • Blood-stasis stabbing pain — add Dan Shen, San Qi, Jiang Xiang
  • Qi deficiency — add Huang Qi, Dang Shen
  • Yang deficiency cold — add Gui Zhi, Fu Zi (combine the Zhishi Xiebai Guizhi Tang intent)

Key point: The first duty is to exclude cardiac emergency: chest pain radiating to left arm/jaw + cold sweat and dyspnea, call emergency services immediately! Acupuncture is only for the chronic stable type after medical exclusion of emergency; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine chest-Bi chapter.

Gynecologic Lower-Abdominal Pain (Dysmenorrhea etc. · to Internal Medicine / Gynecology)

Principle: Regulate the Chong-Ren, order Qi and Blood, warm the uterus; acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment in the Internal Medicine (Gynecology) dysmenorrhea chapter

Target muscle / point: This station uses organ-channel points. Lower-abdominal Guanyuan, Qihai and Chong-Ren related points, no trigger-point needling

Technique: Chiefly Chong-Ren-uterus differentiation needling: CV Guanyuan, Qihai, SP6 (Sanyinjiao) to regulate the Chong-Ren and order Blood, for cold congelation add moxa to warm the uterus. Can ease the acute during menstruation.

Safety: Contraindicate Hegu, Sanyinjiao, Guanyuan etc. in pregnancy; for persistent severe non-menstrual pain or abnormal bleeding, go to Gynecology first to exclude organic disease.

Entry: Not applicable — visceral pain is not an FSN indication

Sweep & reperfusion: Visceral pain is treated chiefly by organ-channel needling and herbs.

Primary points: CV4 (Guanyuan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), CV6 (Qihai), SP8 (Diji, Xi-cleft · acute dysmenorrhea)

Adjunct points: LV3 (Taichong, Qi stagnation), BL32 (Ciliao), for cold congelation add moxa to CV Guanyuan/Qihai, SP10 (Xuehai)

Point rationale: CV4 (Guanyuan) and CV6 (Qihai) supplement and regulate the Chong-Ren and warm the uterus; SP6 (Sanyinjiao) is the key gynecologic point regulating the three Yin; SP8 (Diji), the Spleen-channel Xi-cleft, specifically treats acute dysmenorrhea; for Qi stagnation add LV3 (Taichong), for cold congelation add moxa.

External therapy & care: Avoid cold during menstruation, avoid raw-cold food; emotional regulation; after acupuncture eases the acute, root treatment moves to the Internal Medicine (Gynecology) dysmenorrhea chapter.

Classical formula: Wen Jing Tang (★Classical Original★) / Danggui Shaoyao San (★Classical Original★)

Cold congelation Blood deficiency: Wen Jing Tang; Liver-Spleen disharmony water-Blood binding: Danggui Shaoyao San; Qi stagnation Blood stasis: combine Shixiao San

Wen Jing Tang warms the channel, scatters cold, and nourishes Blood, matching the mechanism of Chong-Ren deficiency-cold; Danggui Shaoyao San nourishes Blood, fortifies the Spleen, and drains water, regulating Liver-Spleen; for Qi stagnation Blood stasis combine Shixiao San to resolve stasis and stop pain.

Textbook reference: Select by pattern (see the Gynecology dysmenorrhea chapter)

Qi stagnation Blood stasis: Gexia Zhuyu Tang; cold congelation: Shaofu Zhuyu Tang; Qi-Blood deficiency: Bazhen Yimu; Liver-Kidney deficiency: Tiao Gan Tang

Dysmenorrhea is treated by pattern type; full differentiation is in the Internal Medicine (Gynecology) dysmenorrhea chapter, not repeated.

Formula analysis: For chronic pain of cold congelation with Blood deficiency, Wen Jing Tang warms and nourishes; for Liver-Spleen disharmony, menstrual pain with edema, Danggui Shaoyao San; for excess Qi-stagnation Blood-stasis menstrual distending pain refusing pressure, the Zhuyu-class with Shixiao San. Choose by deficiency-excess-cold-heat.

Modifications:

  • Cold congelation cold pain — add Ai Ye, Xiao Hui Xiang, Wu Zhu Yu
  • Blood stasis with clots — add Pu Huang, Wu Ling Zhi (Shixiao San), Yi Mu Cao
  • Qi-Blood deficiency post-menstrual dull pain — add Huang Qi, Dang Gui, Shu Di

Key point: Dysmenorrhea follows the cycle, and acupuncture effectively eases the acute (SP8 Diji, SP6 Sanyinjiao); non-menstrual severe pain and abnormal bleeding must go to Gynecology to exclude organic disease; full treatment is in the Internal Medicine (Gynecology) dysmenorrhea chapter.

⚠ Warning Signs — Emergency Exclusion / Emergency Guide

Principle: No pattern identification, mainly no needling; recognize, emergency care / emergency department, exclude

Target muscle / point: Not applicable

Technique: Once a warning sign stands, seek emergency care or the emergency department immediately, do not delay with acupuncture.

Safety: Acute chest pain radiating to left arm/jaw + cold sweat and dyspnea = cardiac emergency, call emergency services immediately; sudden severe abdominal pain + board-like abdomen = acute abdomen, emergency department.

Entry: Not applicable

Sweep & reperfusion: Not applicable.

Primary points: Not applicable

Adjunct points: Not applicable

Point rationale: Visceral-pain emergencies (myocardial infarction, acute abdomen, GI bleeding) count in minutes; acupuncture is useless and delays lifesaving care.

External therapy & care: Graded disposition: chest pain radiating with cold sweat and dyspnea — call emergency services immediately (suspected acute coronary syndrome); severe pain with board-like abdomen refusing pressure — emergency department (suspected perforation/pancreatitis/strangulation); hematemesis or melena — emergency department (GI bleeding); progressive wasting, night waking pain, jaundice — specialist as soon as possible (suspected malignancy); high fever with jaundice and severe pain — emergency department (suspected acute cholangitis).

Classical formula: Not applicable

None

With an emergency uncleared, both prescription and acupuncture delay lifesaving care.

Textbook reference: Not applicable

None

As above.

Formula analysis: This page is the safety floor of the visceral referral station: for suspected visceral-pain emergency, save life first, exclude first; only after confirming non-emergency, differentiate by organ-channel and route to the corresponding Internal Medicine chapter.

Key point: Chest pain radiating with cold sweat — call emergency services immediately; board-like abdomen, hematemesis, melena — emergency department; progressive wasting with jaundice — exclude malignancy. Save life first, exclude first, then differentiate and route to Internal Medicine.

← Back to Pain home

Bi-Syndrome Framework

Bi means blockage — Qi and Blood blocked by pathogens and failing to flow, or the sinew and bone losing nourishment and failing to be nourished, become Bi pain. This chapter is the framework, addressing no single joint but establishing the framework running through the pain of all body joints and limbs: differentiating the predominance of pathogen (wind-cold-damp-heat), the branch-root of disease nature (the excess of blockage-pain, the deficiency of malnourishment-pain), and the depth of location (shallow in the channels and flesh, deep into the sinew-bone and organs), to unify the commonalities of the preceding joint chapters (low back, neck, shoulder, elbow, wrist, knee, ankle, foot, sciatica, piriformis, etc.).

The Suwen Bi Discussion establishes the framework of Bi: ｢when the three Qi of wind, cold, and damp arrive mixed, uniting they become Bi. Where the wind Qi predominates it is migratory-Bi, where cold Qi predominates it is painful-Bi, where damp Qi predominates it is fixed-Bi.｣ Later generations added heat-Bi (damp-heat or long stagnation transforming to heat). Thus external-pathogen Bi divides into four: migratory-Bi (wind predominant · pain wandering), painful-Bi (cold predominant · severe pain eased by warmth), fixed-Bi (damp predominant · heavy and fixed), heat-Bi (heat predominant · red swollen burning pain). This is the framework of differentiating Bi's pathogen, and the cold-heat-deficiency-excess of each joint chapter all belong to this framework.

From ascent-descent-exit-entry, Bi pain never leaves two poles: ｢blockage causes pain｣ is excess — wind-cold-damp-heat or phlegm-stasis obstructs the channels and joints, exit-entry stifled, Qi and Blood congested and not flowing, so the painful site refuses pressure and eases when freed; ｢malnourishment causes pain｣ is deficiency — Qi-Blood and Liver-Kidney deficient, the sinew-bone and joints losing moistening, the generative source insufficient, so the painful site prefers pressure, is sore and weak, and worse with exertion. Acute new disease is mostly excess (blockage), chronic long illness mostly deficiency (malnourishment) or deficiency-excess complex, this being the framework of differentiating Bi's branch-root.

From the depth of location, Bi has a tendency to go from shallow to deep: initially in the channels and flesh (skin-Bi, flesh-Bi), the pathogen shallow and easily treated; long-standing it enters the sinew-bone (sinew-Bi, bone-Bi), the joint deforming and losing free flexion, that is recalcitrant-Bi and crippling-Bi (phlegm-stasis into bone, like chronic tophaceous gout, rheumatoid bone destruction); deeper it may lodge in the organs (as ｢vessel-Bi unresolved, re-contracting the pathogen, lodges within the Heart｣), exceeding the scope of limb Bi pain. So treating Bi values earliness, not letting it go from channel into sinew-bone, from limb to organ.

Clinical application of the Bi framework differentiates the pathogen and branch-root first, fixes the joint location, then follows the safety exit: step one differentiates which of wind-cold-damp-heat predominates (the four Bi of migratory-painful-fixed-heat) and the branch-root deficiency-excess of blockage-malnourishment; step two navigates by involved joint to the corresponding chapter (low back / neck / shoulder / knee / ankle / gout etc.) for fine differentiation; step three, whenever the module-wide warning signs appear (infection with red hot swollen fever, inflammatory arthritis with symmetric polyarticular morning stiffness, tumor with progressive wasting night pain, fracture trauma, progressive nerve damage, visceral emergency referral), excludes first and does not treat as Bi. The navigator below is laid out along this road — the framework establishes the structure, specific treatment is in each joint chapter.

Limb-joint Bi pain — first differentiate pathogen predominance and branch-root, then navigate to the corresponding joint chapter:

Migratory-Bi (Wind Predominant · Pain Wandering)

Principle: Dispel wind and free the collaterals primarily, with scattering cold and draining damp, assisted by nourishing Blood (to treat wind first treat Blood); navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter

Target muscle / point: Ashi points at the joints the wandering pain reaches (taken as the pain shifts), with key wind-dispelling points

Technique: Take the Ashi point at whichever joint hurts (Ashi-point needling, moving with the wandering pain), with distal wind-dispelling points; migratory-Bi has no fixed painful site, so use ｢pain as the point｣ with channel-following point selection. See each chapter for specific joint release technique.

Safety: Depth and vessel-nerve avoidance for each joint's local needling are in the corresponding chapter; for migratory arthritis with symmetric polyarticular swelling, exclude inflammatory arthritis first.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the joint the wandering pain reaches (as the pain shifts)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves the affected joint; release the fascia wherever the wandering pain reaches. See each joint chapter.

Primary points: Involved joint Ashi points, GB20 (Fengchi), GB31 (Fengshi), BL17 (Geshu, Blood-meeting)

Adjunct points: LI4 (Hegu), TE5 (Waiguan), channel-following points by involved joint (see each chapter), SP10 (Xuehai)

Point rationale: Involved joint Ashi points take pain as the point; GB20 (Fengchi), GB31 (Fengshi) dispel wind as key points for migratory-Bi; BL17 (Geshu), the Blood-meeting, SP10 (Xuehai) nourish and quicken Blood (to treat wind first treat Blood, when Blood moves wind extinguishes itself); LI4 (Hegu), TE5 (Waiguan) dispel wind and free the collaterals.

External therapy & care: Avoid wind-cold, keep warm; protect each joint the wandering pain reaches; specific joint functional training see each chapter.

Classical formula: Fang Feng Tang intent / Gui Zhi Tang (★Classical Original★) with wind-dispelling

Fang Feng, Ma Huang, Gui Zhi, Dang Gui, Qin Jiao, Qiang Huo, Fang Ji etc. to dispel wind and free the collaterals; for Blood deficiency add Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng (to treat wind first treat Blood)

Migratory-Bi dispels wind primarily, Fang Feng Tang dispelling wind, freeing the collaterals, scattering cold, and draining damp; assisted by Dang Gui and Ji Xue Teng to nourish Blood (to treat wind first treat Blood, when Blood moves wind extinguishes itself), matching wind-predominant wandering.

Textbook reference: Fang Feng Tang / Juan Bi Tang with modification

Fang Feng, Qiang Huo, Dang Gui, Qin Jiao, Chi Fu Ling, Gan Cao, Gui Zhi, Huang Qin, Ge Gen, ginger, jujube; or Juan Bi Tang (Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Gui Xin, Qin Jiao, Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Hai Feng Teng, Sang Zhi, Ru Xiang, Mu Xiang, Gan Cao)

Fang Feng Tang dispels wind and frees the collaterals, a key formula for migratory-Bi; Juan Bi Tang dispels wind, drains damp, frees the collaterals, and stops pain, suited to wind-damp Bi wandering pain. Specific joint differentiation in each chapter.

Formula analysis: Fang Feng Tang specializes in dispelling wind and freeing the collaterals, for wind-predominant wandering; Juan Bi Tang has both wind-dispelling and damp-draining, for wind-damp equally. Both assist with nourishing Blood (Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng) to ｢treat wind first treat Blood.｣ Fine differentiation of specific joints is in each chapter, this being the framework.

Modifications:

  • Severe wind wandering — heavy Fang Feng, Qiang Huo, add Bai Zhi, Wei Ling Xian
  • Blood deficiency — add Dang Gui, Ji Xue Teng, Shu Di
  • Specific joint — navigate to the corresponding chapter for site modification

Key point: Migratory-Bi (wind predominant) dispels wind and frees the collaterals assisted by nourishing Blood (to treat wind first treat Blood); pain without fixed site uses pain as the point; navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter; symmetric polyarticular swelling with morning stiffness excludes inflammatory arthritis.

Painful-Bi (Cold Predominant · Severe Pain Eased by Warmth)

Principle: Warm the channel and scatter cold primarily, with dispelling wind and draining damp; navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter (Aconitum-class strict control)

Target muscle / point: Ashi points at the fixed cold-painful joint, may add moxa to warm-scatter

Technique: Take the Ashi point at the fixed painful joint (Ashi-point needling), with warm needling / moxibustion to scatter cold; cold-Bi eases with warmth, so emphasize warm freeing. See each chapter for specific joints.

Safety: Moxibustion avoids burns; if it turns red hot swollen, stop moxa and switch to clearing heat; joint needling see the corresponding chapter.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the cold-painful joint, may add warm moxa

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves the affected joint; release the cold-congealing fascia, with warm moxa. See each chapter.

Primary points: Involved joint Ashi points, CV4 (Guanyuan, moxa), BL23 (Shenshu, moxa), GB20 (Fengchi)

Adjunct points: GV4 (Mingmen, moxa), channel-following points by involved joint, GV14 (Dazhui, moxa)

Point rationale: Involved joint Ashi warms and frees the local; moxa CV4 (Guanyuan), BL23 (Shenshu), GV4 (Mingmen) warms and supplements Yang Qi to scatter cold (warm what is cold); GB20 (Fengchi) also dispels wind; together they warm the channel, scatter cold, and free the collaterals.

External therapy & care: Keep the joint warm and avoid cold, warm compress; avoid cold water and cold food; specific joint protection see each chapter.

Classical formula: Wutou Tang (★Classical Original★) / Mahuang Fuzi Xixin Tang (★Classical Original★)

Cold-Bi severe pain: Wutou Tang (Ma Huang, Shao Yao, Huang Qi, Gan Cao, Chuan Wu); Yang-deficiency cold-Bi: Mahuang Fuzi Xixin Tang. Wutou and Fuzi require pre-decocting, long-decocting, strict control of processing and dosage

Wutou Tang warms the channel, scatters cold, drains damp, and stops pain, a key formula for cold-Bi severe pain; Mahuang Fuzi Xixin Tang warms Yang and scatters cold, suited to Yang-deficiency cold-Bi. Wutou and Fuzi are drastic and require strict control of processing (pre-decoct, long-decoct) for safety, matching cold-predominant congelation.

Textbook reference: Wutou Tang / Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang (with deficiency)

Cold-excess painful-Bi: Wutou Tang; cold-Bi with Liver-Kidney deficiency: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang (with Xi Xin, Rou Gui to warm-scatter, Sang Ji Sheng, Du Zhong to supplement Liver-Kidney)

Wutou Tang warm-scatters cold-excess painful-Bi; for concurrent Liver-Kidney deficiency, Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang addresses both warm-scattering and supplementing. Specific joints see each chapter.

Formula analysis: Wutou Tang drastically warm-scatters cold, for cold-excess severe pain (Wutou requires strict control); Mahuang Fuzi Xixin Tang warms Yang and scatters cold, for Yang-deficiency cold-Bi; Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang warm-scatters and supplements Liver-Kidney, for cold-Bi with deficiency. Choose by cold's deficiency-excess. Aconitum-class requires strict control of processing, dosage, decoction.

Modifications:

  • Severe cold pain — Wutou Tang (strict control, pre-decoct long-decoct)
  • Yang deficiency — add Fu Zi, Gui Zhi, Lu Jiao Jiao
  • With Liver-Kidney deficiency — Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang

Key point: Painful-Bi (cold predominant) warms the channel and scatters cold, moxa at the fixed painful site; Aconitum-class strict control of processing, dosage, decoction (pre-decoct, long-decoct); navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter; turning red hot swollen switch to heat-Bi.

Fixed-Bi (Damp Predominant · Heavy Fixed Numbness)

Principle: Drain damp and free the collaterals primarily, with dispelling wind and scattering cold, fortifying the Spleen to move damp (damp's root is in the Spleen); navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter

Target muscle / point: Ashi points at the heavy fixed joint, with Spleen-fortifying damp-draining points

Technique: Take the Ashi point at the heavy joint (Ashi-point needling), with Foot-Taiyin Spleen channel to fortify the Spleen and drain damp; for heavy damp add warm needling to help transform damp. See each chapter for specific joints.

Safety: Joint needling see the corresponding chapter; damp-heat with red swelling is heat-Bi not simple fixed-Bi.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the heavy joint

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: patient slowly moves the affected joint; release the damp-trapping fascia. See each chapter.

Primary points: Involved joint Ashi points, SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao), ST36 (Zusanli)

Adjunct points: ST40 (Fenglong, transform phlegm-damp), BL20 (Pishu, fortify Spleen), channel-following points by involved joint

Point rationale: Involved joint Ashi frees the local; SP9 (Yinlingquan), SP6 (Sanyinjiao) drain damp and fortify the Spleen; ST36 (Zusanli), BL20 (Pishu) fortify the Spleen to move damp (damp's root is in the Spleen, fortify the Spleen to cut off the damp source); ST40 (Fenglong) transforms phlegm-damp.

External therapy & care: Avoid dampness, keep the dwelling dry; Spleen-fortifying damp-draining diet; specific joint protection see each chapter.

Classical formula: Yi Yi Ren Tang intent / Ganjiang Lingzhu Tang (★Classical Original★ · Shen Zhuo)

Yi Yi Ren, Cang Zhu, Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Fang Feng, Ma Huang, Gui Zhi to drain damp, dispel wind, scatter cold; for heavy cold lumbar Bi, Ganjiang Lingzhu Tang (Shen Zhuo Tang)

Fixed-Bi drains damp primarily, Yi Yi Ren Tang draining damp, dispelling wind, scattering cold, and freeing the collaterals; for heavy cold-painful lumbar Bi, Ganjiang Lingzhu Tang (Shen Zhuo Tang) warmly transforms cold-damp, matching damp-predominant heaviness.

Textbook reference: Yi Yi Ren Tang / Qianghuo Shengshi Tang with modification

Yi Yi Ren Tang (Yi Yi Ren, Dang Gui, Shao Yao, Ma Huang, Gui Zhi, Cang Zhu, Qiang Huo, Du Huo, Fang Feng, Chuan Wu, Gan Cao, ginger); upper-body damp-Bi Qianghuo Shengshi Tang

Yi Yi Ren Tang drains damp, frees the collaterals, scatters cold, and stops pain, a key formula for fixed-Bi; upper-body damp-Bi Qianghuo Shengshi Tang dispels wind and overcomes damp. Specific joints see each chapter.

Formula analysis: Yi Yi Ren Tang has both damp-draining and wind-cold-scattering, for damp-Bi throughout the body; Qianghuo Shengshi Tang suits upper-body neck damp-Bi; Ganjiang Lingzhu Tang suits lumbar cold-damp (Shen Zhuo). Choose by damp's location and cold-heat. Fortifying the Spleen to move damp treats the root.

Modifications:

  • Heavy damp swelling — add Fang Ji, Bi Xie, Yi Yi Ren, Fu Ling
  • Spleen deficiency with damp trapping — add Bai Zhu, Fu Ling, Dang Shen to fortify the Spleen
  • Specific joint — navigate to the corresponding chapter (lumbar fixed-Bi see low back cold-damp)

Key point: Fixed-Bi (damp predominant) drains damp and frees the collaterals, fortifying the Spleen to move damp treats the root (damp's root is in the Spleen); lingering and hard to cure requires sustained effort; navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter; red hot swollen is damp-heat (heat-Bi) not simple fixed-Bi.

Heat-Bi (Heat Predominant · Red Swollen Burning Pain)

Principle: Clear heat and free the collaterals primarily, with draining damp (for damp-heat); navigate specific joints to the corresponding chapter; with fever exclude infection first

Target muscle / point: Ashi points around the red swollen joint (avoiding direct needling of the extremely swollen joint cavity), may bloodlet to drain heat

Technique: Encircling needling around the red swollen joint and bloodletting (pricking collaterals to bleed and drain heat-toxin) primarily, avoiding direct needling of the extremely swollen joint cavity; with distal heat-clearing points to guide heat down. See each chapter for specific joints (like gout heat-Bi).

Safety: For acute red hot swelling do not strongly stimulate the joint cavity; bloodletting strict disinfection; with fever exclude septic arthritis first, no needling.

Entry: Insert subcutaneously proximal to the red swollen joint (avoiding the red swollen area)

Sweep & reperfusion: Sweeping with reperfusion: use caution in the acutely very swollen phase, better in recovery; acutely rely on heat-clearing bloodletting. See each chapter.

Primary points: Involved joint Ashi points (bloodletting), GV14 (Dazhui, clear heat), LI11 (Quchi), SP9 (Yinlingquan, drain damp)
